decision.title: Business Registration Halted Across Montenegro Due to Regulatory Gaps in New Tax System
decision.summary: Since the beginning of 2026, no new businesses have been registered in Montenegro as entrepreneurs face a non-functioning registration process tied to regulatory misalignment in the new tax system. The Tax Administration says the issue is normative rather than technical, meaning regulations have not been updated to match the new system. The halt affects all new company formation nationwide. Reported by CdM.
decision.title_mne: Registracija preduzeća u Crnoj Gori obustavljena zbog regulatornih propusta u novom poreskom sistemu
decision.summary_mne: Od početka 2026. godine, nijedno novo preduzeće nije registrovano u Crnoj Gori, jer preduzetnici nailaze na nefunkcionalan proces registracije usljed neusklađenosti propisa sa novim poreskim sistemom. Poreska uprava navodi da je problem normativne, a ne tehničke prirode, što znači da propisi nijesu ažurirani kako bi odgovarali novom sistemu. Obustava pogađa osnivanje svih novih kompanija na nivou cijele države. Prema izvještaju CdM-a.

assessments.summary: While this decision is primarily an economic and administrative matter, the complete freeze on business registration since January 2026 carries meaningful negative consequences for Montenegro's environmental sector. The country faces urgent waste management deficits, is behind schedule on EU Chapter 27 compliance, and needs new green enterprises in recycling, renewable energy, and environmental services. A nearly two-month nationwide halt on company formation blocks the entry of precisely the kind of businesses Montenegro needs to address its environmental infrastructure gaps, particularly ahead of the tourist season when waste generation and coastal pressures intensify.
assessments.executive_summary: The business registration freeze is a negative development for environmental protection, as it blocks the formation of new waste management, recycling, and green energy companies at a time when Montenegro critically needs them for EU Chapter 27 compliance and ahead of the peak tourist season. The Ministry recommends urgent prioritization of environmental service companies in any interim registration mechanism and calls for environmental impact screening to be built into the reformed registration framework.
assessments.reasoning:
The regulatory gap that has halted all business registration in Montenegro since January 2026 is fundamentally a failure of legislative coordination between the new integrated tax management system (IRMS) and the Law on Business Entities. The Tax Administration itself acknowledges this is a normative rather than technical problem, meaning the legal framework was not updated before the new system went live. From the environmental perspective, this creates several interconnected problems.

First, Montenegro's waste management infrastructure remains critically underdeveloped. According to the European Environment Agency and domestic reporting, the country is only partially aligned with the EU waste acquis. The government's own strategy envisions four waste management centres (Podgorica, Niksic, Bijelo Polje, and Bar) but implementation has lagged. Organizations like Zero Waste Montenegro, GES Montenegro, and Hemosan Eco Service represent the small but growing private sector in environmental services. The registration freeze means no new entrants can join this sector precisely when Montenegro needs to scale up recycling capacity, collection infrastructure, and circular economy businesses to meet EU Chapter 27 benchmarks.

Second, Chapter 27 (Environment and Climate Change) remains one of Montenegro's most challenging EU accession chapters. The European Commission has noted that only the civil protection sub-area has been provisionally closed, with nine other sub-areas requiring significantly greater effort. Green Home, a leading Montenegrin environmental NGO, has repeatedly flagged limited progress. Meeting these obligations requires not just government action but a vibrant private sector in environmental services, renewable energy, and green technology. Every month that new companies cannot be registered is a month lost in building this capacity.

Third, the timing is particularly damaging. Montenegro's Adriatic coast faces extreme seasonal pressure from tourism, with waste generation, water consumption, and spatial planning challenges peaking between May and September. Environmental service companies, beach cleanup operations, and eco-tourism ventures that would normally register in early Q1 to prepare for the summer season are now unable to do so. This compounds the chronic problems of illegal dumping, insufficient coastal waste collection, and inadequate wastewater treatment that the coastal zone already faces.

Fourth, climate change mitigation and adaptation projects are also affected. Montenegro has committed to reducing greenhouse gas emissions and transitioning toward renewable energy. Solar energy installers, energy efficiency consultants, and other green businesses that need to register as legal entities cannot do so. This disrupts the pipeline of climate-related investments, including those potentially funded by the EU's Green Agenda for the Western Balkans and the recently established Environmental Protection Fund (Eco Fund).

The fact that this problem has persisted for nearly two months without resolution suggests a systemic failure in regulatory planning. The Tax Administration reports processing over 100,000 tax filings and 10,000 administrative requests, indicating the system works for existing businesses. But the complete inability to register new entities represents a regulatory gap that should have been identified and addressed before the January 2026 rollout. The environmental sector, which is already underserved and under-invested in Montenegro, cannot afford further delays.
key_concerns: The freeze blocks formation of new waste management and recycling companies at a time when Montenegro has serious waste infrastructure deficits and needs private sector growth to meet EU standards
EU Chapter 27 compliance timelines are effectively set back, as environmental service capacity cannot expand while registration is frozen
The approaching tourist season (May-September) will intensify coastal environmental pressures, and new environmental service providers cannot register in time to prepare
Climate mitigation and renewable energy project pipelines are disrupted, as new companies in solar installation, energy efficiency, and green technology cannot be legally established
The nearly two-month duration without resolution suggests poor regulatory planning, with no environmental impact assessment conducted before the system transition
Tax clearance certificate delays of up to 15 days also affect existing environmental companies needing timely documentation for permits and EU-funded project applications
recommendations: Establish an emergency interim registration mechanism that prioritizes environmental service companies, waste management operators, and renewable energy businesses to prevent further delays in building green infrastructure
Conduct an environmental impact screening of any regulatory reform before implementation, ensuring that transitions in tax or business law do not inadvertently harm environmental sector development
Set a hard deadline of no later than March 15, 2026 for resolving the normative gaps, given the approaching tourist season and its environmental pressures on the coast

Create a fast-track registration pathway for companies operating in EU Chapter 27 priority areas (waste management, water treatment, air quality monitoring, biodiversity services) as part of the reformed system

Coordinate with the Environmental Protection Fund (Eco Fund) to ensure that grant and investment recipients are not blocked from registering the legal entities needed to receive and deploy environmental funding
Include the Ministry of Environment in inter-ministerial coordination on business registration reform to ensure environmental sector needs are represented in the new regulatory framework

counter_proposal.title: Green-Priority Emergency Registration Framework
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Environment would advocate for an immediate two-track approach: first, an emergency manual registration window for environmentally critical businesses (waste management, recycling, renewable energy, water treatment, environmental consulting) that bypasses the stalled digital system using paper-based procedures at the Central Registry; second, integrating environmental classification criteria into the reformed registration system so that green economy businesses receive expedited processing as a permanent feature, not just a crisis measure. This approach recognizes that environmental infrastructure cannot wait for general bureaucratic fixes.
key_changes: Activate paper-based emergency registration at the Central Registry of Business Entities specifically for companies with environmental NACE codes (waste collection, treatment, recycling, remediation, water supply, renewable energy generation)
Establish a Ministry of Environment liaison desk at the Tax Administration to verify environmental business classifications and expedite their processing
Mandate that any reformed registration system includes an environmental sector fast-track with processing times no longer than 3 business days
Create a temporary environmental business registration fund covering registration fees for green economy startups affected by the freeze, funded from the Eco Fund budget
Require inter-ministerial environmental screening for future regulatory transitions affecting business formation
expected_benefits: Immediate unblocking of environmental sector company formation, enabling waste management and recycling capacity to grow before the tourist season
Stronger alignment with EU Chapter 27 timelines by ensuring environmental infrastructure investment is not collateral damage of tax system reforms
Precedent-setting integration of environmental priorities into business registration policy, supporting long-term green economy development
Protection of EU and Eco Fund grant pipelines by ensuring recipient entities can be legally established on schedule
Reduced risk of illegal waste dumping and environmental degradation during the tourist season due to inadequate service provider capacity
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: Moderately feasible. Paper-based emergency registration uses existing Central Registry infrastructure and requires only an administrative directive, not legislative change. The environmental NACE code filtering is straightforward. The main barrier is inter-ministerial coordination, as the Ministry of Finance and Tax Administration would need to agree to the workaround. However, given that the registration freeze is already causing political embarrassment and economic harm, there should be motivation to accept a targeted interim solution. The permanent fast-track feature would require inclusion in the forthcoming legislative amendments that the Tax Administration has already acknowledged are necessary.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Okvir za hitnu registraciju sa zelenim prioritetom
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo životne sredine zalagalo bi se za neposredan dvostepeni pristup: prvo, hitni prozor za ručnu registraciju za preduzeća od ekološkog značaja (upravljanje otpadom, reciklaža, obnovljivi izvori energije, prečišćavanje voda, ekološko savjetovanje) koji zaobilazi blokirani digitalni sistem putem papirnih procedura u Centralnom registru; drugo, ugrađivanje kriterijuma ekološke klasifikacije u reformisani sistem registracije, kako bi preduzeća zelene ekonomije trajno imala ubrzanu obradu — a ne samo kao kriznu mjeru. Ovaj pristup prepoznaje da ekološka infrastruktura ne može čekati na opšta birokratska rješenja.
key_changes_mne: Aktivirati hitnu registraciju na papiru u Centralnom registru privrednih subjekata, namijenjenu isključivo preduzećima sa ekološkim NACE kodovima (prikupljanje, obrada i reciklaža otpada, remedijacija, vodosnabdijevanje, proizvodnja energije iz obnovljivih izvora)
Uspostaviti šalter za vezu Ministarstva životne sredine pri Poreskoj upravi radi provjere ekoloških klasifikacija preduzeća i ubrzavanja njihove obrade
Obavezati da svaki reformisani sistem registracije uključuje ubrzani postupak za ekološki sektor sa rokom obrade ne dužim od 3 radna dana
Osnovati privremeni fond za registraciju ekoloških preduzeća koji pokriva troškove registracije za startape zelene ekonomije pogođene zamrzavanjem, finansiran iz budžeta Eko fonda
Uvesti obavezni međuresorni ekološki pregled za buduće regulatorne promjene koje utiču na osnivanje preduzeća
expected_benefits_mne: Neposredno deblokiranje osnivanja preduzeća u ekološkom sektoru, čime se omogućava rast kapaciteta za upravljanje otpadom i reciklažu prije turističke sezone
Jače usklađivanje sa rokovima iz Poglavlja 27 EU, osiguravanjem da ulaganja u ekološku infrastrukturu ne budu kolateralna šteta reformi poreskog sistema
Uvođenje presedan integracije ekoloških prioriteta u politiku registracije preduzeća, uz podršku dugoročnom razvoju zelene ekonomije
Zaštita tokova EU i Eko fonda putem osiguravanja da korisničke organizacije mogu biti pravno osnovane u predviđenom roku
Smanjen rizik od nelegalnog odlaganja otpada i ekološke degradacije tokom turističke sezone usljed nedovoljnih kapaciteta pružalaca usluga
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Umjereno izvodljivo. Hitna registracija na papiru koristi postojeću infrastrukturu Centralnog registra i zahtijeva samo administrativnu direktivu, a ne zakonodavnu promjenu. Filtriranje po ekološkim NACE kodovima je jednostavno. Glavna prepreka je međuresorna koordinacija, budući da bi Ministarstvo finansija i Poreska uprava morali pristati na ovaj privremeni način rada. Međutim, s obzirom na to da zamrzavanje registracije već izaziva politička nelagodnost i ekonomsku štetu, trebalo bi postojati motivacija za prihvatanje ciljnog privremenog rješenja. Trajno uvođenje ubrzanog postupka zahtijevalo bi uključivanje u predstojeće zakonodavne izmjene koje je Poreska uprava već prepoznala kao neophodne.

assessments.summary_mne: Iako je ova odluka prevashodno ekonomsko i administrativno pitanje, potpuno zamrzavanje registracije privrednih subjekata od januara 2026. godine nosi značajne negativne posljedice po ekološki sektor Crne Gore. Zemlja se suočava s hitnim nedostacima u upravljanju otpadom, kasni s ispunjavanjem obaveza iz Poglavlja 27 EU, a potrebna su joj nova zelena preduzeća u oblasti reciklaže, obnovljivih izvora energije i ekoloških usluga. Gotovo dvomjesečna obustava osnivanja preduzeća na nacionalnom nivou sprečava upravo ona preduzeća koja su Crnoj Gori neophodna za prevazilaženje nedostataka u ekološkoj infrastrukturi, posebno u periodu pred turističku sezonu, kada se povećava količina otpada i pojačavaju pritisci na priobalno područje.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Zamrzavanje registracije privrednih subjekata predstavlja negativan razvoj događaja za zaštitu životne sredine, jer sprečava osnivanje novih preduzeća za upravljanje otpadom, reciklažu i zelenu energiju u trenutku kada su Crnoj Gori hitno potrebna radi usklađivanja s Poglavljem 27 EU i u periodu pred vršnu turističku sezonu. Ministarstvo preporučuje hitno davanje prioriteta preduzećima za ekološke usluge u okviru bilo kojeg privremenog mehanizma registracije i zahtijeva da procjena uticaja na životnu sredinu bude ugrađena u reformisani okvir registracije.
key_concerns_mne: Zamrzavanje sprečava osnivanje novih preduzeća za upravljanje otpadom i reciklažu u trenutku kada Crna Gora ima ozbiljne nedostatke u infrastrukturi za otpad i potreban joj je rast privatnog sektora kako bi ispunila standarde EU
Rokovi za usklađivanje s Poglavljem 27 EU efektivno se odgađaju, jer kapacitet ekoloških usluga ne može da raste dok je registracija zamrznuta
Nadolazeća turistička sezona (maj–septembar) intenzivirat će ekološke pritiske na prioblje, a novi pružaoci ekoloških usluga ne mogu se na vrijeme registrovati i pripremiti

Tokovi projekata u oblasti ublažavanja klimatskih promjena i obnovljivih izvora energije su poremećeni, jer nova preduzeća u oblasti ugradnje solarnih panela, energetske efikasnosti i zelenih tehnologija ne mogu biti zakonski osnovana
Gotovo dvomjesečno trajanje bez rješenja ukazuje na loše regulatorno planiranje, a prije tranzicije sistema nije sprovedena procjena uticaja na životnu sredinu

Kašnjenja u izdavanju potvrda o izmirenim poreskim obavezama do 15 dana takođe pogađaju postojeća ekološka preduzeća kojima je pravovremena dokumentacija potrebna za dozvole i prijave na projekte finansirane od strane EU
recommendations_mne: Uspostaviti privremeni mehanizam hitne registracije koji daje prioritet preduzećima za ekološke usluge, operaterima za upravljanje otpadom i preduzećima iz oblasti obnovljivih izvora energije, kako bi se spriječila dalja kašnjenja u izgradnji zelene infrastrukture
Sprovesti ekološki screening svake regulatorne reforme prije njene primjene, osiguravajući da tranzicije u poreskom ili privrednom zakonodavstvu ne naštete nenamjerno razvoju ekološkog sektora
Postaviti čvrst rok, najkasnije do 15. marta 2026. godine, za otklanjanje normativnih nedostataka, imajući u vidu nadolazeću turističku sezonu i ekološke pritiske koje ona donosi na prioblje

Uvesti ubrzanu proceduru registracije za preduzeća koja djeluju u prioritetnim oblastima Poglavlja 27 EU (upravljanje otpadom, prečišćavanje voda, monitoring kvaliteta vazduha, usluge zaštite biodiverziteta) kao dio reformisanog sistema

Uskladiti aktivnosti s Fondom za zaštitu životne sredine (Eko fondom) kako bi se osiguralo da korisnici grantova i investicija ne budu spriječeni u registraciji pravnih lica potrebnih za primanje i raspoređivanje sredstava namijenjenih zaštiti životne sredine
Uključiti Ministarstvo ekologije u međuresornu koordinaciju reforme registracije privrednih subjekata kako bi se potrebe ekološkog sektora zastupale u novom regulatornom okviru

assessments.summary: The complete halt of business registration across Montenegro since January 2026 represents a serious governance failure with direct implications for interior security. By forcing aspiring entrepreneurs into limbo, the regulatory vacuum actively pushes economic activity toward the informal sector -- already estimated at 30-40% of GDP -- undermining the Ministry of Interior's ability to monitor financial flows, detect money laundering, and combat organized crime. The normative misalignment between the Law on Business Entities and the Law on Registration of Business and Other Subjects, both adopted in July 2025, reflects a failure of inter-institutional coordination that erodes public trust in state institutions and jeopardizes Montenegro's EU accession progress on Chapters 23 and 24.
assessments.executive_summary: The Ministry of Interior assesses this decision negatively. A nationwide halt to business registration since January 2026 creates a dangerous vacuum that risks expanding the informal economy, complicating law enforcement surveillance of financial flows, and undermining EU accession benchmarks on justice and security. The government must treat this as an urgent security matter -- not merely an administrative inconvenience -- and fast-track legislative amendments while establishing an interim registration mechanism within days, not weeks.
assessments.reasoning:
From the Ministry of Interior's perspective, the business registration halt poses several interconnected security risks that extend far beyond administrative inconvenience.

First, the expansion of the informal economy is the most immediate concern. Montenegro's shadow economy already accounts for an estimated 30-40% of GDP according to the Tax Administration's own figures, with annual VAT evasion of 120-150 million euros and an estimated 25-30 thousand unreported workers. When legitimate registration is impossible, entrepreneurs who need to earn a living do not simply wait -- they operate informally. Every unregistered business is a business that cannot be monitored through standard financial oversight mechanisms. This directly undermines the work of the Financial Intelligence Unit, the Tax Administration's anti-evasion efforts, and the Ministry of Interior's capacity to detect suspicious financial patterns linked to money laundering and organized crime financing.

Second, the situation has direct implications for organized crime monitoring. Montenegro's Serious and Organized Crime Threat Assessment identifies 11 organized crime groups operating domestically, with the Kavac and Skaljari clans controlling significant cocaine trafficking operations. These groups systematically launder proceeds through legitimate business structures. The Central Registry of Business Entities is a critical tool for law enforcement to track beneficial ownership, identify shell companies, and monitor suspicious corporate activity. A non-functioning registration system creates a blind spot precisely where vigilance is most needed. Moreover, if legitimate businesses cannot register, the distinction between formal and informal economic activity blurs, making it harder to identify genuinely illicit operations.

Third, the halt undermines Montenegro's EU accession progress on Chapters 23 and 24, which are the most critical and difficult chapters. The European Commission's 2025 report noted that Montenegro received a positive Interim Benchmark Assessment Report in June 2024, but structural reforms remain essential. Chapter 24 requires credible prosecution of organized crime, effective border management, and robust administrative capacity. A government that cannot maintain basic business registration functionality sends a deeply negative signal about institutional capacity and inter-agency coordination -- precisely the areas the EU scrutinizes most closely. The fact that two laws adopted simultaneously in July 2025 contain incompatible provisions suggests a legislative process that lacks the quality control mechanisms the EU expects.

Fourth, there are border management and migration implications. Many foreign nationals in Montenegro hold residence permits tied to business ownership or employment in registered companies. If new businesses cannot be registered, foreign entrepreneurs face legal uncertainty about their immigration status. This creates potential for exploitation by intermediaries offering irregular solutions, and complicates the work of border police and the Directorate for Foreigners in verifying the legitimacy of residence claims.

Fifth, the governance failure itself is a security concern. Tax Administration Director Laketic acknowledged the system was not functioning and that his administration was not fully aware of the impact on businesses. This admission of institutional unawareness, nearly two months into the crisis, reveals dangerous gaps in inter-institutional communication and crisis response. The Ministry of Interior relies on functional partner institutions -- particularly the Tax Administration and the Central Registry -- for its own operations. When core state functions break down without timely detection or response, it signals systemic weakness that organized crime groups and corrupt actors are well-positioned to exploit.

The root cause -- normative misalignment between the Law on Business Entities and the Law on Registration of Business and Other Subjects, both adopted in July 2025 -- represents a preventable failure. Proper regulatory impact assessment and inter-ministerial consultation during the legislative process should have identified the incompatibility before both laws entered into force simultaneously. That this was not caught suggests either inadequate legal review procedures or insufficient coordination between the Ministry of Finance, the Ministry of Economic Development, and the legislative drafting bodies.
key_concerns: The registration halt actively expands the informal economy, already 30-40% of GDP, by forcing aspiring entrepreneurs to operate outside legal frameworks where they cannot be monitored by law enforcement or financial intelligence units.

Organized crime groups operating in Montenegro exploit gaps in business oversight to launder illicit proceeds; a non-functioning Central Registry creates a dangerous blind spot for anti-money laundering efforts.

The crisis directly undermines EU accession benchmarks under Chapters 23 and 24, signaling to the European Commission that Montenegro lacks the institutional capacity and inter-agency coordination required for membership.

Foreign nationals holding business-linked residence permits face legal uncertainty, creating potential for irregular migration situations and complicating the work of border police and the Directorate for Foreigners.

The Tax Administration's admission that it was unaware of the crisis for nearly two months reveals systemic failures in inter-institutional communication and crisis detection that have broader security implications.

The normative conflict between two laws adopted simultaneously in July 2025 indicates a broken legislative quality control process, raising concerns about the reliability of other recently adopted legislation affecting security institutions.
recommendations: Treat this as a security-relevant emergency: the government should convene an urgent inter-ministerial session including the Ministry of Interior, Ministry of Finance, and Ministry of Economic Development to fast-track legislative amendments within days, not weeks.

Establish an interim manual registration mechanism immediately, with proper identity verification and beneficial ownership documentation, so that legitimate businesses can register while the electronic system is being fixed -- a complete vacuum is unacceptable from a security standpoint.

Direct the Financial Intelligence Unit and the Uprava policije to conduct enhanced monitoring of informal economic activity during the registration gap period, particularly in sectors known to be vulnerable to money laundering such as real estate, hospitality, and construction.

Mandate a comprehensive regulatory impact assessment for all legislation affecting business and tax registration before parliamentary adoption, with mandatory sign-off from the Ministry of Interior on provisions relevant to financial crime prevention and beneficial ownership transparency.

Coordinate with the EU Delegation in Podgorica to transparently communicate the timeline for resolution, preventing this administrative failure from being cited as evidence of institutional incapacity in the next enlargement progress report.

Review the immigration status of foreign nationals whose business-linked residence permits may be affected by the registration halt, and issue interim guidance to the Directorate for Foreigners to prevent these individuals from falling into irregular status through no fault of their own.

counter_proposal.title: Security-First Emergency Registration Protocol
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Interior would approach this crisis as a security emergency rather than a mere administrative problem. Instead of waiting for legislative amendments to pass through normal parliamentary procedure, we would immediately activate an interim registration pathway operated jointly by the Tax Administration and the Ministry of Interior, ensuring that no gap exists in the state's ability to track business formation and beneficial ownership. Simultaneously, we would deploy enhanced financial intelligence monitoring during the gap period and mandate that any future legislation touching business registration undergo a mandatory security impact assessment with MUP sign-off.
key_changes: Activate an emergency interim registration mechanism within 72 hours, accepting paper-based applications verified by notaries under existing legal authority, with mandatory beneficial ownership declarations submitted to the Financial Intelligence Unit.
Deploy a joint MUP-Tax Administration task force to process the backlog of registration applications accumulated since January 1, prioritizing sectors vulnerable to criminal infiltration.
Issue an emergency directive to the Uprava policije Economic Crime Division to increase surveillance of informal business activity in high-risk sectors during the registration gap.
Require mandatory Ministry of Interior security review for all future legislation affecting business registration, beneficial ownership transparency, and financial monitoring infrastructure.

Establish a real-time inter-institutional alert system so that critical failures in partner institutions -- such as the Tax Administration's registration system -- are immediately flagged to security agencies.
expected_benefits: Eliminates the security blind spot created by the registration vacuum within days rather than weeks.
Maintains the state's capacity to monitor business formation and detect potential money laundering or organized crime front companies throughout the crisis period.
Creates a permanent institutional safeguard against future legislative failures that could compromise law enforcement infrastructure.
Demonstrates to the EU that Montenegro treats administrative capacity as a security matter, strengthening the country's position on Chapters 23 and 24.
Protects foreign nationals from falling into irregular immigration status due to government failure, maintaining the integrity of the residence permit system.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The interim paper-based registration mechanism can be activated under existing legal authority within 72 hours, as the previous registration framework remains legally valid until explicitly repealed. The joint task force can be assembled from existing MUP and Tax Administration personnel without new budget allocations. The enhanced monitoring directive requires only an administrative order from the Police Director. The longer-term reforms -- mandatory security review of legislation and inter-institutional alert systems -- require government-level decisions but face no legal obstacles.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Protokol hitne registracije sa prioritetom bezbjednosti
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova pristupilo bi ovoj krizi kao bezbjednosnoj vanrednoj situaciji, a ne kao pukom administrativnom problemu. Umjesto čekanja da zakonodavne izmjene prođu kroz redovnu parlamentarnu proceduru, odmah bismo aktivirali privremeni registracioni put kojim zajednički upravljaju Poreska uprava i Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova, osiguravajući da ne postoji nikakav jaz u sposobnosti države da prati osnivanje privrednih subjekata i stvarne vlasnike. Istovremeno, tokom perioda praznine uveli bismo pojačano praćenje finansijske inteligencije i obavezali da svako buduće zakonodavstvo koje se tiče registracije preduzeća mora proći obaveznu procjenu uticaja na bezbjednost uz saglasnost MUP-a.
key_changes_mne: Aktiviranje hitnog privremenog mehanizma registracije u roku od 72 sata, prihvatanjem zahtjeva u papirnom obliku koje ovjeravaju notari na osnovu postojećih zakonskih ovlašćenja, uz obavezne izjave o stvarnom vlasništvu dostavljene Upravi za finansijsko-obavještajne poslove.
Raspoređivanje zajedničke radne grupe MUP-a i Poreske uprave za obradu zaostalih registracionih zahtjeva koji su se nakupili od 1. januara, s prioritetom za sektore podložne kriminalnoj infiltraciji.
Izdavanje hitne direktive Odjelu za privredni kriminal Uprave policije radi pojačanog nadzora nad neformalnom poslovnom aktivnošću u visokorizičnim sektorima tokom perioda registracionog zastoja.

Uvođenje obavezne bezbjednosne provjere Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova za sve buduće propise koji se odnose na registraciju privrednih subjekata, transparentnost stvarnog vlasništva i infrastrukturu finansijskog nadzora.

Uspostavljanje međuinstitucionalnog sistema upozorenja u realnom vremenu kako bi se kritični propusti u partnerskim institucijama — poput sistema za registraciju Poreske uprave — odmah prijavljivali bezbjednosnim agencijama.
expected_benefits_mne: Otklanja bezbjednosnu slijepu tačku nastalu usljed registracionog vakuuma u roku od dana, a ne sedmica.
Održava sposobnost države da tokom kriznog perioda prati osnivanje privrednih subjekata i otkriva potencijalno pranje novca ili kompanije koje služe kao paravan organizovanom kriminalu.
Stvara trajnu institucionalnu zaštitu od budućih zakonodavnih propusta koji bi mogli ugroziti infrastrukturu organa za sprovođenje zakona.
Evropskoj uniji demonstrira da Crna Gora administrativnu sposobnost tretira kao bezbjednosno pitanje, čime se jača pozicija zemlje u pogledu Poglavlja 23 i 24.
Štiti strane državljane od pada u nepravilan imigracioni status usljed propusta vlasti, čuvajući integritet sistema dozvola za boravak.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Privremeni mehanizam registracije u papirnom obliku može se aktivirati na osnovu postojećih zakonskih ovlašćenja u roku od 72 sata, budući da prethodni registracioni okvir ostaje pravno važeći sve dok se izričito ne ukine. Zajednička radna grupa može se formirati od postojećeg osoblja MUP-a i Poreske uprave bez novih budžetskih izdvajanja. Direktiva o pojačanom nadzoru zahtijeva samo administrativni nalog direktora policije. Dugoročnije reforme — obavezna bezbjednosna provjera zakonodavstva i međuinstitucionalni sistemi upozorenja — zahtijevaju odluke na nivou Vlade, ali ne nailaze na pravne prepreke.

assessments.summary_mne: Potpuni zastoj u registraciji preduzeća širom Crne Gore od januara 2026. godine predstavlja ozbiljan propust u upravljanju s direktnim posljedicama po unutrašnju bezbjednost. Prisiljavajući buduće preduzetnike u stanje neizvjesnosti, regulatorni vakuum aktivno gura ekonomsku aktivnost prema neformalnom sektoru — koji se već procjenjuje na 30–40% BDP-a — podrivajući sposobnost Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova da prati finansijske tokove, otkriva pranje novca i suzbija organizovani kriminal. Normativno neusklađivanje između Zakona o privrednim društvima i Zakona o registraciji privrednih i drugih subjekata, oba usvojena u julu 2025. godine, odražava propust međuinstitucionalne koordinacije koji narušava povjerenje javnosti u državne institucije i dovodi u pitanje napredak Crne Gore u procesu pristupanja EU u okviru Poglavlja 23 i 24.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova negativno ocjenjuje ovu odluku. Zastoj u registraciji preduzeća na nivou cijele države od januara 2026. godine stvara opasan vakuum koji prijeti širenjem sive ekonomije, otežava nadzor organa za sprovođenje zakona nad finansijskim tokovima i podriva mjerila za pristupanje EU u oblasti pravde i bezbjednosti. Vlada mora ovo tretirati kao hitno bezbjednosno pitanje — a ne tek kao administrativnu nepogodnost — te ubrzano donijeti zakonodavne izmjene i uspostaviti privremeni mehanizam registracije u roku od dana, a ne nedjelja.
key_concerns_mne: Zastoj u registraciji aktivno širi sivu ekonomiju, koja već iznosi 30–40% BDP-a, prisiljavajući buduće preduzetnike da posluju izvan zakonskih okvira, gdje ih organi za sprovođenje zakona i jedinice za finansijsku obavještajnu djelatnost ne mogu pratiti.

Grupe organizovanog kriminala koje djeluju u Crnoj Gori iskorišćavaju praznine u nadzoru nad poslovanjem radi pranja nezakonito stečenih prihoda; nefunkcionalni Centralni registar stvara opasnu slijepu tačku u naporima za suzbijanje pranja novca.

Kriza direktno podriva mjerila za pristupanje EU u okviru Poglavlja 23 i 24, šaljući signal Evropskoj komisiji da Crna Gora nema institucionalni kapacitet ni međuagencijsku koordinaciju potrebnu za članstvo.

Strani državljani koji posjeduju dozvole boravka vezane za poslovanje suočavaju se s pravnom neizvjesnošću, što stvara potencijal za situacije neregularnih migracija i komplikuje rad granične policije i Uprave za strance.

Priznanje Poreske uprave da nije bila svjesna krize gotovo dva mjeseca otkriva sistemske propuste u međuinstitucionalnoj komunikaciji i otkrivanju kriznih situacija, koji imaju šire bezbjednosne implikacije.

Normativni sukob između dva zakona usvojena istovremeno u julu 2025. godine ukazuje na narušen proces kontrole kvaliteta zakonodavstva, što izaziva zabrinutost u pogledu pouzdanosti drugih nedavno usvojenih propisa koji se tiču bezbjednosnih institucija.
recommendations_mne: Tretirati ovo kao bezbjednosno relevantan vanredni slučaj: Vlada bi trebalo da sazove hitnu međuresornu sjednicu uz učešće Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova, Ministarstva finansija i Ministarstva ekonomskog razvoja, kako bi se zakonodavne izmjene ubrzano donijele u roku od dana, a ne nedjelja.

Odmah uspostaviti privremeni mehanizam ručne registracije, uz odgovarajuću provjeru identiteta i dokumentaciju o stvarnom vlasništvu, kako bi se legitimnim preduzećima omogućila registracija dok se elektronski sistem popravlja — potpuni vakuum je neprihvatljiv s bezbjednosnog stanovišta.

Naložiti Upravi za sprječavanje pranja novca i finansiranja terorizma i Upravi policije da tokom perioda zastoja u registraciji pojačaju monitoring neformalne ekonomske aktivnosti, posebno u sektorima koji su poznati kao ranjivi na pranje novca, kao što su nekretnine, ugostiteljstvo i građevinarstvo.

Uvesti obavezu sveobuhvatne procjene regulatornog uticaja za sve propise koji se tiču poslovne i poreske registracije prije usvajanja u parlamentu, uz obaveznu saglasnost Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova za odredbe relevantne za sprječavanje finansijskog kriminala i transparentnost stvarnog vlasništva.

Koordinisati se s Delegacijom EU u Podgorici radi transparentnog saopštavanja rokova za rješavanje problema, kako bi se spriječilo da ovaj administrativni propust bude naveden kao dokaz institucionalnog nedostatka kapaciteta u narednom izvještaju o napretku u procesu proširenja.

Preispitati imigracioni status stranih državljana čije dozvole boravka vezane za poslovanje mogu biti pogođene zastojem u registraciji, te izdati privremene smjernice Upravi za strance kako bi se spriječilo da te osobe, bez ikakve vlastite krivice, upadnu u situaciju neregularnog boravka.

assessments.decision_id: Business Registration Halted Across Montenegro Due to Regulatory Gaps in New Tax System
assessments.summary: The nearly two-month halt in business registration across Montenegro represents a serious governance failure that directly undermines education policy objectives. While the decision is not an education policy per se, its cascading effects strike at the heart of what the education system is trying to build: entrepreneurial competence among youth, viable career pathways for VET and university graduates, and reasons for young Montenegrins to stay rather than emigrate. The regulatory misalignment between the new Law on Commercial Companies and supporting regulations reveals a lack of inter-ministerial coordination that is especially damaging given Montenegro's EU accession trajectory and the ongoing reform of its vocational and higher education systems.
assessments.executive_summary: The Ministry of Education views the business registration halt as a negative development that actively damages entrepreneurship education outcomes, worsens brain drain pressures, and undermines VET reform credibility. The top concern is the signal this sends to young graduates and students: that Montenegro cannot provide a functional environment for starting a business. The ministry recommends immediate emergency registration procedures and a formal inter-ministerial coordination mechanism to prevent regulatory gaps from sabotaging education-to-employment pipelines.
assessments.reasoning:
The business registration freeze, ongoing since January 1, 2026, must be assessed against Montenegro's educational landscape and the broader challenge of retaining young talent in a country of approximately 620,000 people.

First, the impact on entrepreneurship education is direct and measurable. Montenegro has invested significantly in VET reform, including the recently concluded VETpreneur project that trained 129 teachers in Montenegro and Bosnia and Herzegovina to deliver the Student Company programme. The 10th EAfA-ETF seminar was hosted in Podgorica in October 2025, where Montenegro showcased its evolving work-based learning landscape, including micro-qualifications and dual education scholarships. University of Donja Gorica hosted its Entrepreneurship and Innovation Festival in 2025. All of these initiatives aim to produce graduates who can create businesses. A two-month (and counting) freeze on business registration renders these programmes hollow. Students completing entrepreneurship modules or student company programmes cannot translate their education into practice.

Second, brain drain is Montenegro's most acute educational crisis. Over 44,000 Montenegrin citizens are studying and working abroad, with the largest cohort aged 20-29. Youth unemployment has historically exceeded 30 percent. The government is piloting the Youth Guarantee programme (2025-2026) with EU support, aiming to offer every young person under 30 a quality opportunity within four months. A non-functioning business registration system directly contradicts this commitment. Young people who might otherwise start a business after graduation will see this dysfunction as confirmation that Montenegro's institutions cannot support their ambitions.

Third, the nature of the failure is particularly concerning from an education policy perspective. The Tax Administration confirmed the issue is normative, not technical, meaning the new Integrated Registry and Management System (IRMS) works, but the Law on Commercial Companies (which entered into force August 13, 2025 and became applicable January 1, 2026) was not properly harmonized with other regulations governing authentication and document preparation. This suggests a failure of inter-ministerial coordination during the legislative process. The education system depends on a functional regulatory environment to deliver on its promises; when the Ministry of Finance, the Tax Administration, and the Ministry of Economy cannot coordinate a major legal transition, the education system's credibility suffers.

Fourth, the Innovation Fund, which allocated 2.4 million euros in 2024 for startup support and innovation in small businesses, cannot function effectively when beneficiaries cannot register their enterprises. Early-stage startup grants become meaningless if recipients cannot legally form companies.

Fifth, regarding EU alignment, Montenegro's EU accession process requires demonstration of institutional capacity and good governance. The Bologna Process and broader EU education acquis emphasize employability and the link between education and the labour market. A systemic failure in business registration signals institutional weakness that could affect EU assessment of Montenegro's readiness, including in education-related chapters.

The silver lining is that the Tax Administration reports the new system itself is functional, processing over 100,000 tax submissions and handling 600-800 cases daily. The problem is solvable through regulatory amendments. However, the fact that nearly two months have passed without resolution suggests either a lack of political urgency or deeper institutional dysfunction.
key_concerns: Entrepreneurship education programmes (VETpreneur Student Company, university-based initiatives) lose credibility when graduates cannot register businesses, potentially discouraging student engagement with these curricula
Brain drain acceleration: young graduates already inclined to leave Montenegro receive further evidence that domestic institutions cannot support business creation, reinforcing emigration decisions
The Youth Guarantee pilot programme (2025-2026) is undermined since self-employment and business creation are key pathways the programme is supposed to offer within four months of registration

VET reform credibility is damaged: Montenegro has showcased its dual education, micro-qualifications, and work-based learning reforms to EU partners, but a non-functional business environment contradicts claims of progress
Inter-ministerial coordination failure signals systemic governance weakness that will affect all policy areas dependent on regulatory coherence, including education-to-employment transitions
Innovation Fund beneficiaries (2.4 million euros allocated in 2024) cannot execute on early-stage startup grants if company formation is impossible
Extended delays in tax compliance certificates (up to 15 days) affect existing educational institutions and training providers that are themselves businesses requiring regular tax documentation
recommendations: Establish an emergency temporary registration procedure within 72 hours to allow business formation while permanent regulatory harmonization is completed, ensuring that graduates of entrepreneurship programmes and Innovation Fund beneficiaries are not indefinitely blocked

Create a formal inter-ministerial regulatory impact assessment process that includes the Ministry of Education as a mandatory consultee for any legislation affecting business formation, labour markets, or employment pathways

Extend deadlines for Innovation Fund startup grants and Youth Guarantee programme targets by the duration of the registration freeze, so that education-linked employment programmes are not penalized for a failure outside their control
Commission an immediate study on the number of recent VET and university graduates who have been unable to register businesses since January 2026, to quantify the education-specific impact
Fast-track the regulatory harmonization of the Law on Commercial Companies with authentication and document preparation procedures, with a public timeline and accountability mechanism

Integrate business registration simulation exercises into VET and university entrepreneurship curricula so students gain practical experience with real procedures, which also serves as an early warning system when procedures break down

Request EU technical assistance through IPA or Erasmus+ capacity-building instruments to support regulatory alignment, framing it as an education-to-employment pipeline issue relevant to Chapter 26 (Education and Culture) of the EU acquis

counter_proposal.title: Education-Proofed Regulatory Transitions Framework
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Education would establish a mandatory education impact assessment for all major regulatory changes affecting business formation, labour markets, and employment. Rather than allowing legislative transitions to proceed without cross-ministerial checks, the ministry would require that every new law or regulation with potential employment or entrepreneurship implications be evaluated against active education programmes, graduate employment pipelines, and Youth Guarantee commitments before taking effect. For the immediate crisis, the ministry would co-lead (with the Ministry of Economy) an emergency task force to implement temporary registration procedures and protect education-linked entrepreneurship pathways.
key_changes: Introduce a mandatory Education and Employment Impact Assessment for all legislation affecting business formation or labour market access, modelled on EU regulatory impact assessment standards
Establish a standing inter-ministerial working group on education-to-employment transitions, including representatives from the Ministries of Education, Economy, Finance, and Labour
Create a real-time dashboard tracking graduate business formation rates, linked to VET and university programme evaluation metrics
Implement a 90-day legislative buffer period for any new law affecting business registration, during which parallel old and new procedures operate simultaneously to prevent service interruptions
Develop a rapid-response protocol allowing the Ministry of Education to flag and escalate regulatory failures that affect graduate employment or entrepreneurship within 48 hours
expected_benefits: Prevention of future registration freezes through mandatory parallel operation periods during legislative transitions
Quantifiable link between education programme outputs and business formation rates, improving programme accountability
Stronger EU accession narrative by demonstrating cross-ministerial coordination on education-to-employment policy
Reduced brain drain pressure by ensuring that entrepreneurship education leads to actual business creation opportunities
Early warning system that catches regulatory failures before they accumulate into months-long crises
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility for the immediate emergency task force (can be established within days using existing institutional structures). Medium feasibility for the legislative buffer period and impact assessment framework (requires regulatory amendment, achievable within 6-12 months). The inter-ministerial working group is highly feasible as it builds on existing coordination mechanisms. The real-time dashboard requires modest IT investment and can leverage the new IRMS infrastructure that the Tax Administration reports is already functional. Total estimated additional cost is minimal, primarily staff time and minor IT development.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Okvir za regulatorne tranzicije provjeren kroz prizmu obrazovanja
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo prosvjete bi uspostavilo obaveznu procjenu uticaja na obrazovanje za sve veće regulatorne promjene koje se tiču osnivanja preduzeća, tržišta rada i zapošljavanja. Umjesto da se zakonodavne tranzicije odvijaju bez međuresornih provjera, ministarstvo bi zahtijevalo da svaki novi zakon ili propis s potencijalnim implikacijama na zapošljavanje ili preduzetništvo bude vrednovan u odnosu na aktivne obrazovne programe, tokove zapošljavanja diplomiranih i obaveze iz Garancije za mlade, prije nego što stupi na snagu. Za trenutnu krizu, ministarstvo bi zajednički vodilo (s Ministarstvom ekonomije) hitnu radnu grupu radi uvođenja privremenih registracionih postupaka i zaštite preduzetničkih puteva povezanih s obrazovanjem.
key_changes_mne: Uvesti obaveznu procjenu uticaja na obrazovanje i zapošljavanje za sve propise koji se tiču osnivanja preduzeća ili pristupa tržištu rada, po uzoru na standarde EU za procjenu regulatornog uticaja
Uspostaviti stalno međuresornu radnu grupu za tranzicije iz obrazovanja u zapošljavanje, uz učešće predstavnika ministarstava prosvjete, ekonomije, finansija i rada
Kreirati dashboard u realnom vremenu za praćenje stopa osnivanja preduzeća od strane diplomiranih, povezan s metrikama vrednovanja programa strukovnog obrazovanja i obuke te visokoškolskih programa
Uvesti zakonodavni zaštitni period od 90 dana za svaki novi propis koji utiče na registraciju preduzeća, tokom kojeg stari i novi postupci funkcionišu paralelno radi sprečavanja prekida usluga
Razviti protokol brzog reagovanja koji Ministarstvu prosvjete omogućava da u roku od 48 sati signalizira i eskalira regulatorne propuste koji utiču na zapošljavanje diplomiranih ili preduzetništvo
expected_benefits_mne: Sprečavanje budućih zamrzavanja registracije uvođenjem obaveznih perioda paralelnog funkcionisanja tokom zakonodavnih tranzicija
Mjerljiva veza između ishoda obrazovnih programa i stopa osnivanja preduzeća, što poboljšava odgovornost programa
Jača naracija o pristupanju EU demonstriranjem međuresorne koordinacije u politici prelaska iz obrazovanja u zapošljavanje
Smanjenje pritiska odljeva mozgova osiguravanjem da preduzetničko obrazovanje vodi do stvarnih mogućnosti osnivanja preduzeća
Sistem ranog upozorenja koji otkriva regulatorne propuste prije nego što se akumuliraju u krize koje traju mjesecima
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost za hitnu radnu grupu (može se uspostaviti u roku od nekoliko dana koristeći postojeće institucionalne strukture). Srednja izvodljivost za zakonodavni zaštitni period i okvir za procjenu uticaja (zahtijeva izmjenu propisa, ostvarivo u roku od 6–12 mjeseci). Međuresorna radna grupa je visoko izvodljiva jer se nadovezuje na postojeće mehanizme koordinacije. Dashboard u realnom vremenu zahtijeva skromna IT ulaganja i može koristiti novu infrastrukturu IRMS-a za koju Poreska uprava navodi da je već funkcionalna. Ukupni procijenjeni dodatni troškovi su minimalni – uglavnom radno vrijeme zaposlenih i manji IT razvoj.

assessments.summary_mne: Skoro dvomjesečni zastoj u registraciji preduzeća u Crnoj Gori predstavlja ozbiljan neuspjeh u upravljanju koji direktno podriva ciljeve obrazovne politike. Iako ova odluka sama po sebi nije mjera obrazovne politike, njeni kaskadni efekti pogađaju samu srž onoga što obrazovni sistem nastoji izgraditi: preduzetničke kompetencije mladih, održive karijerne puteve za polaznike stručnog obrazovanja i usavršavanja (SOO) i studente, te razloge zbog kojih bi mladi Crnogorci ostajali umjesto da emigriraju. Regulatorna neusklađenost između novog Zakona o privrednim društvima i pratećih propisa otkriva nedostatak međuresorne koordinacije, koji je posebno štetan uzimajući u obzir crnogorski put ka EU i tekuće reforme stručnog i visokog obrazovanja.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ministarstvo prosvjete smatra zastoj u registraciji preduzeća negativnim razvojem koji aktivno šteti ishodima obrazovanja za preduzetništvo, pogoršava pritiske odljeva mozgova i narušava kredibilitet reforme SOO-a. Najveća zabrinutost tiče se signala koji se šalje mladim diplomcima i studentima: da Crna Gora ne može obezbijediti funkcionalno okruženje za pokretanje biznisa. Ministarstvo preporučuje uvođenje hitnih procedura registracije i uspostavljanje formalnog međuresornog mehanizma koordinacije kako bi se spriječilo da regulatorne praznine sabotiraju obrazovne cjevovode do zapošljavanja.
key_concerns_mne: Programi obrazovanja za preduzetništvo (učenička kompanija VETpreneur, inicijative na nivou univerziteta) gube kredibilitet kada diplomirani studenti ne mogu da registruju preduzeća, što potencijalno obeshrabruje angažovanje učenika u ovim nastavnim programima

Ubrzanje odljeva mozgova: mladi diplomirani studenti koji su već skloni da napuste Crnu Goru dobijaju još jedan dokaz da domaće institucije ne mogu da podrže osnivanje preduzeća, čime se jačaju odluke o emigraciji

Pilot program Garancije za mlade (2025–2026) biva podrvnut, budući da su samozapošljavanje i osnivanje preduzeća ključni putevi koje ovaj program treba da ponudi u roku od četiri mjeseca od registracije

Kredibilitet reforme SOO-a je narušen: Crna Gora je EU partnerima predstavljala reforme dualnog obrazovanja, mikrokvalifikacija i učenja zasnovanog na radu, ali nefunkcionalno poslovno okruženje protivriječi tvrdnjama o napretku

Neuspjeh međuresorne koordinacije signalizira sistemsku slabost upravljanja koja će uticati na sve oblasti politike zavisne od regulatorne koherentnosti, uključujući prijelaze iz obrazovanja u zapošljavanje
Korisnici Inovacionog fonda (2,4 miliona eura dodijeljenih u 2024. godini) ne mogu da realizuju bespovratna sredstva za startape u ranoj fazi ako je osnivanje preduzeća nemoguće

Produžena kašnjenja u izdavanju potvrda o poreskim dugovima (do 15 dana) utiču na postojeće obrazovne ustanove i pružaoce obuke koji su i sami preduzeća kojima je redovno potrebna poreska dokumentacija
recommendations_mne: Uspostaviti hitnu privremenu proceduru registracije u roku od 72 sata kako bi se omogućilo osnivanje preduzeća dok se ne završi trajna regulatorna harmonizacija, osiguravajući da diplomirani polaznici preduzetničkih programa i korisnici Inovacionog fonda ne budu neograničeno blokirani

Uspostaviti formalni međuresorno proces procjene regulatornog uticaja koji uključuje Ministarstvo prosvjete kao obaveznog konsultanta za svako zakonodavstvo koje utiče na osnivanje preduzeća, tržišta rada ili puteve zapošljavanja

Produžiti rokove za startap grantove Inovacionog fonda i ciljeve programa Garancije za mlade za trajanje zamrzavanja registracije, kako programi zapošljavanja vezani za obrazovanje ne bi bili kažnjeni zbog neuspjeha izvan njihove kontrole

Naručiti hitnu studiju o broju nedavno diplomiranih polaznika SOO-a i studija koji od januara 2026. godine nisu bili u mogućnosti da registruju preduzeća, radi kvantifikacije specifičnog uticaja na obrazovanje
Ubrzati regulatornu harmonizaciju Zakona o privrednim društvima sa procedurama ovjere i pripreme dokumenata, uz javni vremenski okvir i mehanizam odgovornosti

Integrisati simulacijske vježbe registracije preduzeća u nastavne programe preduzetništva u SOO-u i na univerzitetima, kako bi studenti stekli praktično iskustvo sa stvarnim procedurama, što istovremeno služi i kao sistem ranog upozorenja kada procedure zakazuju

Zatražiti tehničku pomoć EU putem instrumenata IPA ili Erasmus+ za izgradnju kapaciteta radi podrške regulatornom usklađivanju, predstavljajući to kao pitanje obrazovnog cjevovoda do zapošljavanja relevantno za Poglavlje 26 (Obrazovanje i kultura) pravne tekovine EU

assessments.summary: The complete halt of business registration across Montenegro since January 2026 poses a serious threat to the tourism sector, sustainable development, and northern region economic prospects. With tourism contributing approximately 25-30% of GDP and the critical pre-season preparation window (February-April) now underway, the inability to register new tourism enterprises — hotels, restaurants, tour operators, adventure tourism outfits, agritourism ventures — directly undermines seasonal economic output. The normative gap between the new Companies Act (effective January 1, 2026) and the IRMS tax system reflects a governance failure that disproportionately harms the already economically disadvantaged northern region, where entrepreneurship support is weakest and new business formation is most needed for balanced development.
assessments.executive_summary: This is a negative development for Montenegro's tourism and sustainable development prospects. The nearly two-month halt in business registration falls during the critical pre-season preparation window, preventing new tourism enterprises from forming ahead of the May-October season and disproportionately harming the economically disadvantaged northern region. The government must immediately establish an emergency fast-track registration procedure and resolve the normative misalignment within weeks, not months.
assessments.reasoning:
The business registration halt must be evaluated against the seasonal rhythm of Montenegro's tourism economy and the structural challenges of northern region development.

TIMING AND TOURISM IMPACT: Montenegro's tourism sector is heavily seasonal, with the vast majority of activity concentrated between May and October. The period from February through April is when new seasonal businesses — beach bars, excursion companies, rental agencies, restaurants, adventure tourism operators — must register, secure permits, and prepare for opening. A registration freeze that began in January and persists through late February has already consumed nearly half of this critical preparation window. Every week of continued delay reduces the number of new tourism businesses that can operate in the 2026 season. According to the Primorski Portal and CdM reporting, not a single new company has been registered in 2026. This is not merely an administrative inconvenience; it is an economic blockade during the most time-sensitive period for Montenegro's largest economic sector.

NORTHERN REGION DEVELOPMENT: The northern municipalities — Kolasin, Zabljak, Plav, Rozaje, Bijelo Polje, Pljevlja — already suffer from significant economic disparity compared to the coast. New business formation in the north is essential for developing ecotourism, agritourism, adventure sports, and rural hospitality offerings that can reduce the north-south economic gap. The registration freeze is particularly damaging here because the north has fewer established businesses and relies more heavily on new entrepreneurial initiatives. Potential ski tourism operators, national park ecotourism ventures, and rural accommodation providers who planned to launch in 2026 are now stuck in limbo. Unlike coastal businesses that may have existing operations to fall back on, northern entrepreneurs often lack alternative income sources.

ENVIRONMENTAL AND SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT IMPLICATIONS: While the registration halt is not directly an environmental measure, it has indirect implications for sustainability policy. Legitimate eco-tourism and green businesses that would contribute to sustainable development goals cannot register, while existing operations — including some that may not meet environmental standards — continue without new competition. The halt also undermines the narrative of Montenegro as a business-friendly destination aligned with EU standards, which is relevant to attracting environmentally conscious foreign investment. Furthermore, the delay in tax verification certificates (now taking up to 15 days versus much shorter previously) creates cascading problems for existing environmental service providers and waste management companies that need timely tax clearances.

EU ACCESSION AND CHAPTER 27 CONCERNS: The European Commission's 2025 report on Montenegro already noted only limited progress on Chapter 27, with slow implementation of the Action Plan and frequent deadline extensions. The business registration freeze — caused by a failure to align new legislation (the Companies Act effective January 1, 2026) with existing regulatory frameworks — demonstrates exactly the kind of governance and implementation deficit that the EU has flagged repeatedly. The inability to coordinate normative changes across government agencies undermines Montenegro's credibility on much more complex Chapter 27 obligations, which require sustained cross-ministerial coordination on environmental impact assessment, waste management, water quality, and nature protection. If Montenegro cannot synchronize a tax system rollout with company law, the EU will have legitimate doubts about its capacity to implement the far more demanding environmental acquis.

ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS: The Tax Administration's statement that this is a normative rather than technical problem is revealing. It means the IRMS system works, but nobody ensured that the Law on Business Entities was harmonized with the regulations governing document verification and preparation before the system went live on January 12, 2026. This is a classic implementation failure — passing legislation without ensuring the regulatory ecosystem is ready. The government's interim measure of issuing paper documents is inadequate when the fundamental registration pathway is blocked.

REGIONAL COMPETITIVENESS: Montenegro competes directly with Croatia, Albania, and Greece for tourism investment. A complete business registration freeze sends a damaging signal to foreign investors considering tourism ventures in Montenegro. Croatia and Albania both offer relatively streamlined business registration processes; Montenegro's current dysfunction risks redirecting investment to competitors precisely when it needs to be attracting new tourism capacity.
key_concerns: The registration freeze coincides with the critical February-April pre-season preparation period, meaning new tourism businesses cannot form in time for the May-October season, directly reducing 2026 tourism economic output.
Northern region entrepreneurs — who are most dependent on new business formation for economic development — are disproportionately harmed because they lack established businesses to fall back on.

The normative gap between the Companies Act and IRMS regulations reveals a governance coordination failure that undermines Montenegro's credibility on far more complex EU Chapter 27 implementation requirements.

Tax verification certificates now taking up to 15 days creates cascading delays for existing businesses including environmental service providers, waste management companies, and tourism operators needing timely clearances.
Montenegro's competitiveness as a tourism investment destination is damaged relative to Croatia, Albania, and Greece, which maintain functioning business registration systems.
The interim paper-based workaround is inadequate — it does not resolve the fundamental inability to register new companies, only addresses ancillary certificate issuance.
recommendations: Establish an emergency fast-track registration procedure within 10 days, using a temporary regulatory bridge that allows registration to proceed while full normative harmonization is completed.
Set a hard deadline of March 15, 2026 for full resolution of the normative misalignment between the Companies Act and IRMS-related regulations, with named officials accountable for delivery.
Create a dedicated tourism business registration window that prioritizes seasonal tourism enterprises to ensure they can open before the May 2026 season begins.

Provide automatic deadline extensions for all tourism permits, licenses, and tax obligations that were affected by the registration freeze, so that new businesses are not penalized for government-caused delays.

Commission an independent audit of the IRMS rollout process to understand why normative harmonization was not completed before the system went live, and apply lessons learned to future regulatory transitions including Chapter 27 environmental legislation.

Establish a northern region entrepreneurship support desk that provides free legal and administrative assistance to entrepreneurs in municipalities north of Podgorica, helping them navigate the post-freeze registration process.

counter_proposal.title: Emergency Tourism-Priority Registration Bridge
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than waiting for full normative harmonization, the Ministry would implement a two-track emergency solution: an immediate temporary registration pathway using pre-IRMS procedures for all new businesses, combined with a tourism-priority fast lane that ensures seasonal enterprises can register within 48 hours. Simultaneously, a task force with a hard March 15 deadline would complete the normative alignment. The northern region would receive dedicated support through mobile registration units visiting municipal centers.
key_changes: Reactivate the pre-IRMS registration pathway as a temporary parallel system until normative gaps are closed, allowing business formation to resume immediately.
Create a tourism-sector fast lane guaranteeing 48-hour registration for businesses classified under hospitality, food service, tour operations, rental services, and adventure tourism.
Deploy mobile registration units to northern municipalities (Kolasin, Zabljak, Plav, Rozaje, Bijelo Polje) to bring the registration service directly to underserved areas.
Establish a cross-ministerial normative harmonization task force with a hard deadline of March 15 and daily progress reporting to the Prime Minister.
Waive all registration fees for businesses that were delayed by the freeze and provide a 90-day grace period for initial tax filing obligations.
expected_benefits: Immediate resumption of business formation nationwide, preventing further economic damage during the pre-tourism season window.
Protection of 2026 tourism season capacity by ensuring new seasonal businesses can register and prepare before May.
Reduced north-south economic disparity through targeted mobile registration support in underserved northern municipalities.
Stronger EU accession credibility by demonstrating rapid crisis response and cross-ministerial coordination capacity.
Preserved investor confidence in Montenegro as a tourism investment destination relative to regional competitors.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. Reactivating pre-IRMS registration procedures requires a government decree rather than parliamentary legislation, meaning it can be implemented within days. The mobile registration units can leverage existing Tax Administration staff and equipment. The main political obstacle is institutional reluctance to admit the IRMS rollout was premature, but the economic cost of continued inaction far exceeds any reputational cost of a temporary workaround. The March 15 deadline for full normative harmonization is ambitious but achievable given that the issues are normative rather than technical.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Hitni Most za Registraciju s Prioritetom za Turizam
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto čekanja na potpunu normativnu harmonizaciju, Ministarstvo bi implementiralo hitno rješenje na dvije linije: neposredni privremeni put registracije korišćenjem procedura prije IRMS-a za sva nova preduzeća, u kombinaciji s turističkim prioritetnim brzim koridorom koji osigurava da sezonska preduzeća mogu biti registrovana u roku od 48 sati. Istovremeno, radna grupa s čvrstim rokom do 15. marta dovršila bi normativno usklađivanje. Sjeverni region dobio bi posebnu podršku putem mobilnih jedinica za registraciju koje posjećuju opštinske centre.
key_changes_mne: Reaktivirati put registracije koji je prethodio IRMS-u kao privremeni paralelni sistem dok se ne otklone normativni nedostaci, čime se odmah nastavlja osnivanje preduzeća.

Kreirati brzi koridor za turistički sektor koji garantuje registraciju u roku od 48 sati za preduzeća razvrstana u oblasti ugostiteljstva, prehrambenih usluga, turoperaterskih djelatnosti, rent-a-car usluga i avanturističkog turizma.
Rasporediti mobilne jedinice za registraciju u sjeverne opštine (Kolašin, Žabljak, Plav, Rožaje, Bijelo Polje) kako bi se usluga registracije direktno pružila nedovoljno pokrivenim područjima.
Uspostaviti međuresornu radnu grupu za normativnu harmonizaciju s čvrstim rokom do 15. marta i svakodnevnim izvještavanjem o napretku Predsjedniku Vlade.
Osloboditi preduzeća koja su bila pogođena zamrzavanjem od svih naknada za registraciju i odobriti rok počeka od 90 dana za početno podnošenje poreskih prijava.
expected_benefits_mne: Trenutno nastavljanje osnivanja preduzeća na nivou cijele države, čime se sprečava dalja ekonomska šteta tokom prozora pred turističku sezonu.
Zaštita kapaciteta turističke sezone 2026. godine osiguravanjem da nova sezonska preduzeća mogu biti registrovana i pripremljena prije maja.
Smanjenje ekonomske nejednakosti između sjevera i juga putem ciljane mobilne podrške registraciji u nedovoljno pokrivenim sjevernim opštinama.
Jačanje kredibiliteta pristupanja EU demonstriranjem brzog odgovora na krizu i kapaciteta za međuresornu koordinaciju.
Očuvano povjerenje investitora u Crnu Goru kao destinaciju za turističke investicije u odnosu na regionalne konkurente.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Reaktivacija procedura registracije koje su prethodile IRMS-u zahtijeva vladinu uredbu, a ne parlamentarno zakonodavstvo, što znači da se može implementirati u roku od nekoliko dana. Mobilne jedinice za registraciju mogu koristiti postojeće osoblje i opremu Poreske uprave. Glavna politička prepreka je institucionalna nevoljkost da se prizna kako je uvođenje IRMS-a bilo preuranjeno, ali ekonomski trošak daljeg nedjelovanja daleko prevazilazi bilo kakav reputacijski trošak privremenog zaobilaznog rješenja. Rok do 15. marta za potpunu normativnu harmonizaciju je ambiciozan, ali ostvariv s obzirom na to da su problemi normativne, a ne tehničke prirode.

assessments.summary_mne: Potpuni zastoj u registraciji preduzeća širom Crne Gore od januara 2026. godine predstavlja ozbiljnu prijetnju turističkom sektoru, održivom razvoju i ekonomskim izgledima sjevernog regiona. Budući da turizam čini oko 25–30% BDP-a, a kritični prozor pripreme pred sezonu (februar–april) je već u toku, nemogućnost registracije novih turističkih preduzeća — hotela, restorana, turoperatora, avanturističkih agencija i agroturističkih preduzeća — direktno ugrožava sezonski ekonomski učinak. Normativni jaz između novog Zakona o privrednim društvima (na snazi od 1. januara 2026.) i poreskog sistema IRMS odražava propust u upravljanju koji nesrazmjerno šteti sjevernom regionu — ekonomski već zapostavljenom — gdje je podrška preduzetništvu najslabija, a osnivanje novih preduzeća najpotrebnije za uravnotežen razvoj.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ovo je negativan razvoj događaja za turizam i izglede održivog razvoja Crne Gore. Skoro dvomjesečni zastoj u registraciji preduzeća dešava se upravo u kritičnom prozoru pripreme pred sezonu, sprečavajući nova turistička preduzeća da se osnuju prije sezone maj–oktobar i nesrazmjerno štedeći ekonomski zapostavljen sjeverni region. Vlada mora hitno uspostaviti ubrzanu proceduru registracije i riješiti normativni nesklad u roku od sedmica, a ne mjeseci.
key_concerns_mne: Zamrzavanje registracije poklapa se s kritičnim periodom pripreme pred sezonu (februar–april), što znači da nova turistička preduzeća ne mogu biti osnovana na vrijeme za sezonu maj–oktobar, čime se direktno smanjuje turistički ekonomski učinak u 2026. godini.
Preduzetnici sjevernog regiona — koji su najviše zavisni od osnivanja novih preduzeća radi ekonomskog razvoja — nesrazmjerno su pogođeni jer nemaju već postojeće firme na koje bi se mogli osloniti.

Normativni jaz između Zakona o privrednim društvima i propisa o IRMS-u otkriva propust u koordinaciji upravljanja koji narušava kredibilitet Crne Gore u pogledu daleko složenijih zahtjeva implementacije u okviru Poglavlja 27 EU.

Potvrde o poreskom stanju koje sada čekaju i do 15 dana stvaraju niz kašnjenja za postojeća preduzeća, uključujući pružaoce usluga zaštite životne sredine, komunalna preduzeća i turističke operatere koji trebaju pravovremena odobrenja.
Konkurentnost Crne Gore kao destinacije za turistička ulaganja opada u poređenju s Hrvatskom, Albanijom i Grčkom, koje imaju funkcionalne sisteme registracije preduzeća.
Privremeno rješenje zasnovano na papirnoj dokumentaciji nije dostatno — ono ne rješava suštinsku nemogućnost registracije novih preduzeća, već se odnosi samo na sporedna pitanja izdavanja potvrda.
recommendations_mne: Uspostaviti hitnu ubrzanu proceduru registracije u roku od 10 dana, koristeći privremeni regulatorni most koji omogućava nastavak registracije dok se ne završi potpuno normativno usklađivanje.

Postaviti čvrst rok do 15. marta 2026. za potpuno rješavanje normativnog nesklada između Zakona o privrednim društvima i propisa vezanih za IRMS, uz imenovanje konkretnih službenika odgovornih za realizaciju.
Otvoriti poseban šalter za registraciju turističkih preduzeća koji daje prednost sezonskim turističkim subjektima kako bi mogli početi s radom prije početka sezone u maju 2026. godine.

Obezbijediti automatsko produženje rokova za sve turističke dozvole, licence i poreske obaveze na koje je uticalo zamrzavanje registracije, kako nova preduzeća ne bi bila kažnjena zbog kašnjenja koja je prouzrokovala država.

Naručiti nezavisnu reviziju procesa uvođenja IRMS-a kako bi se razumjelo zašto normativno usklađivanje nije završeno prije puštanja sistema u rad, te primijeniti naučene lekcije na buduće regulatorne tranzicije, uključujući ekološko zakonodavstvo u okviru Poglavlja 27.

Uspostaviti desk podrške preduzetništvu u sjevernom regionu koji pruža besplatnu pravnu i administrativnu pomoć preduzetnicima u opštinama sjeverno od Podgorice, pomažući im da se snađu u procesu registracije nakon zamrzavanja.

assessments.summary: The nationwide halt of business registration since January 2026, caused by normative misalignment in the new Integrated Registry and Management System (IRMS), poses indirect but significant risks to public health and social protection. While not a health policy decision per se, the freeze prevents the establishment of new private healthcare facilities, pharmacies, dental practices, and social service providers. It also threatens to push aspiring entrepreneurs into informality, undermining pension and social insurance contributions. For a country already struggling with low health workforce density and brain drain, blocking the pathway for health professionals to open private practices risks accelerating emigration. The government's failure to align regulations before launching the new system reflects poor inter-ministerial coordination that has tangible downstream effects on healthcare access and social welfare.
assessments.executive_summary: The business registration freeze is a negative development for health and social protection: it blocks new private clinics, pharmacies, and social enterprises from forming, while pushing would-be entrepreneurs toward informality where pension and social contributions go unpaid. The Ministry of Health urges immediate emergency regulatory alignment and a fast-track pathway for health sector registrations to prevent further erosion of healthcare capacity and workforce retention.
assessments.reasoning:
The business registration halt, now nearly two months long, creates several health-sector-specific concerns that merit serious attention.

First, private healthcare expansion is frozen. Montenegro relies increasingly on private healthcare providers to supplement the public system. Private clinics are concentrated in Podgorica, Tivat, Kotor, and Budva, and serve both citizens and a growing expat population. The registration freeze means no new private medical practices, dental clinics, physiotherapy centres, or pharmacies can be legally established. This is particularly concerning given that Montenegro has only 199 doctors per 100,000 people and 554 nurses per 100,000 people, both well below EU averages. Any obstacle to expanding healthcare capacity, whether public or private, is a step backward.

Second, the pharmaceutical supply chain is affected. New pharmaceutical distributors, wholesale operations, or pharmacy branches cannot be registered. Under the Law on Medicines and the regulatory requirements overseen by CInMED (the Agency for Medicines and Medical Devices), pharmacy operation requires prior business registration with the Central Registry (CRPS) before a health licence can be obtained from the Ministry of Health. The registration freeze therefore creates a bottleneck at the very first step of the licensing process.

Third, the freeze risks worsening health workforce brain drain. Montenegro already suffers from significant emigration of health professionals to EU member states. Health workers who wish to establish private practices as an alternative to emigrating are now unable to do so. Every month the freeze continues, the incentive to leave for countries where business formation is straightforward grows stronger. This is a critical concern given the regional pattern documented by the European Observatory on Health Systems: Southeast European countries are losing health workers at alarming rates.

Fourth, the social protection dimension is significant. Although Montenegro reformed its health insurance system from a contributions-based model to tax-funded universal coverage (making health insurance residence-based rather than employment-based), pension and disability insurance still depends on formal employment. The Fond PIO receives contributions of 15% from employees and 5.5% from employers. When aspiring entrepreneurs cannot register, they either remain unemployed, work informally, or leave the country. According to existing data, approximately 24.5% of economic activity in Montenegro already occurs in the grey zone, with 22.3% of workers informally employed.
