Prolonging the registration freeze will expand this informal sector, reducing pension fund revenues and leaving more people without adequate pension and disability coverage.

Fifth, social service delivery is impacted. New NGOs, social enterprises, and community-based organisations focused on disability rights, deinstitutionalisation, mental health, or eldercare cannot be formally registered. Montenegro has committed to deinstitutionalisation and community-based care models as part of its EU accession process. Blocking the creation of new service providers directly undermines these commitments.

Sixth, the 15-day delays in obtaining tax compliance certificates compound the problem. Even existing healthcare entities that need certificates for licensing renewals, public procurement participation, or RFZO contract renewals face administrative bottlenecks that can disrupt ongoing operations.

From an EU accession perspective, this regulatory failure reflects poorly on Montenegro's administrative capacity under multiple chapters: Chapter 28 (Consumer and Health Protection), Chapter 3 (Right of Establishment and Freedom to Provide Services), and Chapter 16 (Taxation). The EU expects candidate countries to maintain functioning business registration systems. A two-month nationwide freeze is a serious governance failure that EU monitoring bodies will note.

The root cause, a normative gap where new system requirements were not matched with updated regulations, points to a failure of inter-ministerial coordination. The Tax Administration, Ministry of Finance, and Ministry of Economy should have ensured full regulatory alignment before the January 2026 go-live date. The Ministry of Health was apparently not consulted about the downstream effects on health sector registrations, which is itself a governance concern.

Sources consulted: CdM reporting on the registration halt, WHO European Observatory Health Systems in Action: Montenegro (2024), UNICEF Montenegro Social Protection Situational Analysis, WHO financial protection report for Montenegro (April 2025), Investitor.me reporting on IRMS system launch, and PWC tax summaries for Montenegro.
key_concerns: New private healthcare facilities (clinics, dental practices, physiotherapy centres) cannot be legally established, limiting expansion of healthcare capacity in a country with below-EU-average doctor and nurse density
Pharmacy and pharmaceutical distributor registration is frozen, potentially constraining medicine supply chain expansion and creating gaps in pharmaceutical access particularly in underserved areas

Health professionals unable to register private practices face increased incentive to emigrate, worsening an already critical brain drain problem in a country with only 199 doctors per 100,000 population
Aspiring entrepreneurs pushed into informal economy will not contribute to the Fond PIO pension and disability insurance fund, weakening social protection sustainability

New social enterprises and NGOs focused on disability services, mental health, and deinstitutionalisation cannot be registered, undermining Montenegro's EU accession commitments on community-based care
15-day delays in tax compliance certificates may disrupt existing healthcare providers' ability to renew licences, participate in public procurement, or maintain RFZO contracts
The regulatory failure signals poor inter-ministerial coordination, with no evidence that health sector impacts were assessed before the IRMS system launch
recommendations: Establish an emergency fast-track registration pathway for health sector entities (private clinics, pharmacies, social service providers) using transitional regulatory provisions while full normative alignment is completed

Conduct an immediate inter-ministerial impact assessment involving the Ministry of Health, Ministry of Finance, and Ministry of Economy to identify all health-sector-specific regulatory gaps in the IRMS system
Issue a government decree setting a hard deadline of no later than 15 March 2026 for full normative alignment, with named officials accountable for delivery
Extend validity of all existing healthcare licences, FZOCG contracts, and health sector permits by 90 days to prevent disruptions caused by delayed tax compliance certificates
Create a dedicated help desk within the Tax Administration for health sector registrations, recognising the time-sensitive nature of healthcare service provision
Commission a rapid assessment of how many health professionals have delayed or abandoned plans to open private practices due to the freeze, to quantify brain drain risk
Include a mandatory health impact assessment in future major regulatory or IT system transitions affecting business registration

counter_proposal.title: Health Sector Emergency Registration Protocol
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Health would immediately establish a parallel registration mechanism for health sector entities, bypassing the stalled IRMS normative process through a ministerial decree that authorises provisional health practice licences based on completed documentation, with full IRMS registration to follow once the normative gaps are resolved. This would be combined with automatic 90-day extensions for all existing health sector permits and contracts, and a dedicated inter-ministerial task force with a fixed 30-day deadline to resolve all health-related regulatory misalignments.
key_changes: Issue a Ministry of Health decree enabling provisional health practice authorisations independent of full IRMS business registration
Create a joint Ministry of Health and Tax Administration working group with a 30-day mandate to resolve all health sector regulatory gaps
Automatically extend all existing health licences, pharmacy permits, and FZOCG provider contracts by 90 days
Establish a digital pre-registration portal where health sector applicants can submit all documentation now and receive priority processing once the system is operational
Require future major IT or regulatory system changes to include a mandatory health sector impact assessment with sign-off from the Ministry of Health
expected_benefits: Unblocks establishment of new private clinics, pharmacies, and dental practices within days rather than waiting months for full system resolution
Reduces emigration incentive for health professionals who wish to start private practices in Montenegro
Protects existing healthcare providers from administrative disruptions caused by delayed tax certificates
Maintains continuity of social service provision including disability and mental health community-based services
Demonstrates to EU monitoring bodies that Montenegro can manage regulatory transitions without compromising essential public services
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The Ministry of Health already has legal authority under the Law on Healthcare to issue provisional practice authorisations. The main requirement is political will for inter-ministerial coordination and a government-level decree to establish the transitional mechanism. No new legislation is needed, only a ministerial decree and a government conclusion directing the Tax Administration to cooperate. The 90-day extension of existing permits is a standard administrative tool used in previous regulatory transitions.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Protokol hitne registracije u zdravstvenom sektoru
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo zdravlja bi odmah uspostavilo paralelni mehanizam registracije za subjekte u zdravstvenom sektoru, zaobilazeći zastali normativni proces IRMS-a putem ministarskog dekreta kojim se odobravaju privremene dozvole za obavljanje zdravstvene djelatnosti na osnovu kompletne dokumentacije, uz punu IRMS registraciju koja bi uslijedila nakon što se normativni nedostaci otklone. Ovo bi bilo kombinovano sa automatskim produženjima od 90 dana za sve postojeće dozvole i ugovore u zdravstvenom sektoru, kao i s namjenskom međuresornom radnom grupom s fiksnim rokom od 30 dana za rješavanje svih regulatornih neusklađenosti vezanih za zdravstvo.
key_changes_mne: Donošenje dekreta Ministarstva zdravlja kojim se omogućavaju privremena ovlašćenja za obavljanje zdravstvene djelatnosti, nezavisno od pune poslovne registracije u IRMS-u
Osnivanje zajedničke radne grupe Ministarstva zdravlja i Poreske uprave s mandatom od 30 dana za otklanjanje svih regulatornih nedostataka u zdravstvenom sektoru
Automatsko produženje svih postojećih zdravstvenih licenci, dozvola za apoteke i ugovora pružalaca usluga s FZOCG za 90 dana

Uspostavljanje digitalnog portala za predsregistraciju, gdje podnosioci zahtjeva iz zdravstvenog sektora mogu odmah dostaviti svu dokumentaciju i dobiti prioritetnu obradu čim sistem postane operativan
Uvođenje obaveze da buduće značajne promjene IT ili regulatornih sistema uključuju obaveznu procjenu uticaja na zdravstveni sektor uz saglasnost Ministarstva zdravlja
expected_benefits_mne: Deblokira osnivanje novih privatnih klinika, apoteka i stomatoloških ordinacija u roku od nekoliko dana, umjesto čekanja mjesecima na puno rješavanje sistemskih problema
Smanjuje podsticaj za iseljavanje zdravstvenih radnika koji žele otvoriti privatnu praksu u Crnoj Gori
Štiti postojeće pružaoce zdravstvenih usluga od administrativnih poremećaja uzrokovanih kašnjenjem poreskih uvjerenja
Održava kontinuitet pružanja socijalnih usluga, uključujući usluge za osobe s invaliditetom i mentalno zdravlje u zajednici
Pred tijelima EU za monitoring demonstrira da Crna Gora može upravljati regulatornim tranzicijama bez ugrožavanja ključnih javnih usluga
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Ministarstvo zdravlja već ima zakonska ovlašćenja na osnovu Zakona o zdravstvenoj zaštiti da izdaje privremena ovlašćenja za obavljanje djelatnosti. Glavni uslov je politička volja za međuresornu koordinaciju i dekret na nivou Vlade za uspostavljanje tranzicionog mehanizma. Nije potrebno novo zakonodavstvo – dovoljan je ministarski dekret i zaključak Vlade kojim se Poreska uprava usmjerava na saradnju. Produženje postojećih dozvola za 90 dana standardni je administrativni alat koji je korišćen u prethodnim regulatornim tranzicijama.

assessments.summary_mne: Nacionalni zastoj u registraciji preduzeća od januara 2026. godine, uzrokovan normativnim neusklađenostima u novom Integrisanom registarskom i upravljačkom sistemu (IRUS), predstavlja indirektan, ali značajan rizik po javno zdravlje i socijalnu zaštitu. Iako se po sebi ne radi o odluci iz oblasti zdravstvene politike, zamrzavanje sprečava osnivanje novih privatnih zdravstvenih ustanova, apoteka, stomatoloških ordinacija i pružalaca socijalnih usluga. Prijeti i gurkanjem budućih preduzetnika u sivu ekonomiju, čime se narušavaju uplate doprinosa za penzijsko i socijalno osiguranje. Za zemlju koja se već suočava s niskom gustinom zdravstvenih radnika i odlivom mozgova, blokiranje puta zdravstvenim stručnjacima da otvore privatne ordinacije rizikuje ubrzavanje emigracije. Neuspjeh Vlade da uskladi propise prije pokretanja novog sistema odražava slabu međuresornu koordinaciju, koja ima opipljive posljedice po pristup zdravstvenim uslugama i socijalnu zaštitu.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Zamrzavanje registracije preduzeća negativan je razvoj događaja za zdravlje i socijalnu zaštitu: blokira osnivanje novih privatnih klinika, apoteka i socijalnih preduzeća, istovremeno gurajući potencijalne preduzetnike ka sivoj ekonomiji u kojoj penzijski i socijalni doprinosi ostaju neuplaćeni. Ministarstvo zdravlja poziva na hitno usklađivanje propisa u vanrednom postupku i na uvođenje ubrzanog puta registracije za subjekte u zdravstvenom sektoru, kako bi se spriječilo dalje urušavanje zdravstvenih kapaciteta i zadržavanje zdravstvenih radnika.
key_concerns_mne: Nove privatne zdravstvene ustanove (klinike, stomatološke ordinacije, centri za fizioterapiju) ne mogu se legalno osnovati, čime se ograničava proširenje zdravstvenih kapaciteta u zemlji s gustinom ljekara i medicinskih sestara ispod prosjeka EU

Registracija apoteka i farmaceutskih distributera je zamrznuta, što može ograničiti razvoj lanca snabdijevanja lijekovima i stvoriti praznine u pristupu farmaceutskim proizvodima, posebno u nedovoljno pokrivenim područjima

Zdravstveni stručnjaci koji nisu u mogućnosti da registruju privatne ordinacije suočavaju se s pojačanim podsticajima za emigraciju, čime se pogoršava već kritičan problem odliva mozgova u zemlji s tek 199 ljekara na 100.000 stanovnika
Budući preduzetnici gurnuti u sivu ekonomiju neće uplaćivati doprinose Fondu PIO za penzijsko i invalidsko osiguranje, čime se slabi održivost sistema socijalne zaštite

Nova socijalna preduzeća i NVO usmjerene na usluge za osobe s invaliditetom, mentalno zdravlje i deinstitucionalizaciju ne mogu se registrovati, čime se narušavaju crnogorske obaveze prema EU u pogledu usluga u zajednici

Kašnjenja od 15 dana u izdavanju potvrda o poreskom izmirenju mogu poremetiti mogućnost postojećih zdravstvenih ustanova da obnove licence, učestvuju u javnim nabavkama ili održavaju ugovore s RFZO-om
Regulatorna greška ukazuje na slabu međuresornu koordinaciju, bez ikakvih dokaza da su uticaji na zdravstveni sektor procijenjeni prije pokretanja IRUS sistema
recommendations_mne: Uspostaviti hitan ubrzani postupak registracije za subjekte u zdravstvenom sektoru (privatne klinike, apoteke, pružaoce socijalnih usluga) putem prelaznih regulatornih odredbi, dok se ne završi potpuno normativno usklađivanje

Sprovesti hitnu međuresornu procjenu uticaja uz učešće Ministarstva zdravlja, Ministarstva finansija i Ministarstva ekonomije, kako bi se identifikovale sve regulatorne praznine specifične za zdravstveni sektor u IRUS sistemu
Donijeti vladinu uredbu kojom se utvrđuje čvrst rok, najkasnije do 15. marta 2026. godine, za potpuno normativno usklađivanje, uz imenovanje odgovornih zvaničnika za realizaciju

Produžiti važenje svih postojećih zdravstvenih licenci, ugovora s FZOCG-om i dozvola u zdravstvenom sektoru za 90 dana, radi sprečavanja poremećaja nastalih usljed kašnjenja u izdavanju potvrda o poreskom izmirenju
Osnovati posebnu šalter-službu u okviru Poreske uprave za registracije u zdravstvenom sektoru, uzimajući u obzir vremensku osjetljivost pružanja zdravstvenih usluga

Naručiti brzu procjenu broja zdravstvenih stručnjaka koji su odložili ili odustali od planova za otvaranje privatnih ordinacija zbog zamrzavanja registracije, u cilju kvantifikacije rizika od odliva mozgova
Uvesti obaveznu procjenu uticaja na zdravlje u budućim velikim regulatornim ili IT tranzicijama koje utiču na registraciju preduzeća

assessments.summary: The nationwide halt of business registration since January 2026 represents a severe failure of fiscal governance. The root cause is a normative gap: the government launched the Integrated Revenue Management System (IRMS) on 1 January 2026 without first ensuring that the Law on Business Entities and subordinate regulations were aligned with the new tax system's requirements. This has produced a near-two-month period in which zero new companies have been registered across Montenegro, directly suppressing new tax revenue, undermining investor confidence, and contradicting Montenegro's EU accession commitments on economic governance and business environment reform.
assessments.executive_summary: The Ministry of Finance views the business registration halt as a strongly negative outcome reflecting a fundamental failure to coordinate regulatory reform with IT system deployment. The top concern is the immediate and compounding loss of tax revenue and economic dynamism during a critical period for EU accession. The key recommendation is to issue emergency regulatory amendments within days, not weeks, and establish a mandatory regulatory pre-clearance process for all future system launches.
assessments.reasoning:
The analysis rests on several interconnected fiscal and economic concerns.

DIRECT REVENUE LOSS: Montenegro registers approximately 5,000-7,000 new business entities per year based on the growth from 75,000 entities in February 2025 to 92,000 by November 2025. That implies roughly 500-700 new registrations per month. With nearly two months of complete stoppage, an estimated 1,000-1,400 potential businesses have been blocked from entering the formal economy. Each new business generates corporate tax (9-15%), VAT contributions, employment-related taxes (IOPPD), and social contributions. Even conservatively, the cumulative fiscal impact of delayed registrations over two months runs into the hundreds of thousands of euros in deferred or lost revenue, with compounding effects as these businesses would have generated activity throughout 2026.

IRMS SYSTEM CONTEXT: The Tax Administration launched the Integrated Revenue Management System (IRMS) on 1 January 2026, merging several previously separate systems into a unified platform. According to reporting by CdM, the Tax Administration itself acknowledges the registration stoppage is normative rather than technical, meaning the laws and bylaws governing document verification and registration procedures were not updated to match the new system's requirements before launch. This is a planning and coordination failure at the highest level. As of 19 February, only 10,080 items had been electronically processed, and citizens report waiting up to 15 days for tax compliance certificates that previously took much less time.

IMPACT ON FOREIGN INVESTMENT: Montenegro's FDI reached USD 532 million in 2024, but the IMF and the Montenegrin Foreign Investors Council have repeatedly flagged bureaucratic sluggishness as a barrier to investment. The MFIC White Book on Investment Climate already identifies regulatory unpredictability as a key concern. A two-month nationwide registration freeze sends a devastating signal to foreign investors at precisely the moment Montenegro needs to diversify FDI away from real estate (which rose to dominate FDI inflows while productive investment fell to just 12.8% in 2024). Prospective investors in tourism, technology, and services who cannot register entities will simply choose neighbouring jurisdictions like Serbia or Albania.

EU ACCESSION IMPLICATIONS: Montenegro aims to close EU accession negotiations by end of 2026 and achieve membership by 2028. As of January 2026, 13 of 33 chapters are provisionally closed. The European Commission's 2025 Progress Report evaluates Montenegro on economic governance, business environment, and institutional capacity. A systemic failure to register businesses due to regulatory misalignment directly undermines performance in Chapter 6 (Company Law), Chapter 16 (Taxation), and the broader economic criteria. The Commission consistently calls for predictable, stable regulatory environments. This episode provides ammunition for skeptics of Montenegro's readiness.

BUDGET IMPACT: Montenegro's annual budget is approximately 2 billion EUR. While the direct revenue loss from two months of registration stoppage is modest relative to total budget, the secondary effects are significant: delayed economic activity reduces VAT collections, income tax receipts, and social contributions throughout 2026. More critically, the reputational damage to Montenegro's business environment could suppress private sector growth and investment for quarters beyond the immediate resolution of the technical issue.

SYSTEMIC GOVERNANCE FAILURE: The Tax Administration's characterization of this as a normative rather than technical problem actually makes it worse from a fiscal governance perspective. Technical glitches in new IT systems are expected and forgivable. Failing to align the legal framework before launching a nationwide system suggests either inadequate interministerial coordination, rushed implementation timelines, or insufficient legal review. This is the kind of institutional weakness that the IMF Article IV consultation (November 2025) implicitly warns about when calling for stronger fiscal institutions.

COMPARISON WITH BEST PRACTICE: EU member states and advanced candidate countries routinely conduct regulatory impact assessments and legal compatibility reviews before launching integrated tax systems. Estonia's e-Residency and digital tax platforms, for example, underwent extensive legal harmonization before deployment. Montenegro's approach of launching first and harmonizing later is the opposite of international best practice and suggests a governance process that prioritizes visible deliverables over functional outcomes.
key_concerns: Direct loss of tax revenue from an estimated 1,000-1,400 blocked business registrations over nearly two months, affecting corporate tax, VAT, and employment-related fiscal receipts

Severe reputational damage to Montenegro's investment climate at a time when FDI is already overly concentrated in real estate (productive investment fell to 12.8% of FDI in 2024) and diversification is critical
Undermining of EU accession progress, particularly in Chapters 6 (Company Law) and 16 (Taxation), where regulatory predictability and institutional capacity are key evaluation criteria

Systemic governance failure: the Tax Administration acknowledges the issue is normative (legal misalignment) rather than technical, indicating the IRMS was launched without completing necessary legal harmonization
Cascade effects on the informal economy: businesses unable to register formally may operate informally, expanding the grey economy and further eroding the tax base
Additional reported delays of up to 15 days for tax compliance certificates create further friction for existing businesses, compounding the economic drag
recommendations: Issue emergency regulatory amendments (by Government decree or urgent parliamentary procedure) within 5 business days to resolve the normative misalignment between the Law on Business Entities and IRMS requirements
Establish a temporary parallel registration channel using pre-IRMS procedures to immediately unblock new business formation while permanent regulatory fixes are finalized
Conduct a full regulatory impact assessment of all laws and bylaws affected by IRMS to identify and resolve any remaining normative gaps before they manifest as new blockages

Create a mandatory interministerial legal compatibility review process for all future IT system launches, requiring sign-off from the Ministry of Finance, Ministry of Justice, and relevant line ministries before go-live
Publish weekly public updates on IRMS performance metrics (registrations processed, average processing times, backlog size) to restore business and investor confidence
Commission an independent audit of the IRMS procurement and implementation process to determine accountability for the failure to harmonize regulations before launch
Provide fiscal relief (fee waivers or expedited processing) for businesses whose registration was delayed by the system failure, to mitigate reputational damage and demonstrate responsiveness

counter_proposal.title: Emergency Regulatory Bridge and Dual-Track Registration Recovery
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Finance would have ensured legal harmonization was completed before the IRMS launch date, but given the current crisis, proposes a two-track approach: (1) immediate activation of a temporary registration pathway using pre-IRMS legal procedures to unblock business formation within days, and (2) a fast-track regulatory amendment process with a hard 10-day deadline to permanently resolve all normative gaps. This would be paired with a fiscal impact mitigation package including fee waivers for affected entrepreneurs and a public transparency dashboard tracking IRMS recovery metrics.
key_changes: Activate pre-IRMS registration procedures as a temporary parallel channel within 48 hours
Set a hard 10-day deadline for Government to adopt emergency regulatory amendments resolving all identified normative gaps
Establish a daily interministerial task force (Finance, Justice, Economy, Tax Administration) to identify and resolve remaining legal inconsistencies
Waive registration fees and expedite processing for all businesses delayed by the stoppage
Launch a public IRMS Recovery Dashboard publishing daily metrics on registrations processed, pending, and average wait times
Mandate a pre-launch legal compatibility certification for all future government IT system deployments
expected_benefits: Immediate unblocking of business registration would recover an estimated 500-700 registrations per month, restoring tax revenue flow
Fee waivers and expedited processing would demonstrate government accountability and partially restore investor confidence
Public transparency dashboard would provide real-time accountability and reduce information asymmetry that fuels negative media coverage
Interministerial task force would prevent further normative gaps from emerging as IRMS functionality expands
Pre-launch legal certification mandate would prevent recurrence of this type of failure in future government digitalization projects
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. Activating pre-IRMS procedures requires only an administrative decision, not legislative change. Emergency regulatory amendments can be adopted by Government decree under existing fast-track provisions. Fee waivers are within the Ministry of Finance's discretionary authority. The transparency dashboard can be built using existing IRMS data feeds. The main obstacle is political will and interministerial coordination, not technical or legal constraints.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Hitni Regulatorni Most i Oporavak Registracije na Dvije Kolosijeke
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo finansija bi bilo osiguralo završetak pravnog usklađivanja prije datuma pokretanja IRMS-a, ali s obzirom na trenutnu krizu, predlaže pristup na dvije kolosijeke: (1) trenutačna aktivacija privremenog puta registracije korišćenjem pravnih procedura iz perioda prije IRMS-a, kako bi se odblokirao osnivanje preduzeća u roku od nekoliko dana, i (2) ubrzani proces regulatornih izmjena sa čvrstim rokom od 10 dana za trajno rješavanje svih normativnih praznina. Ovo bi bilo praćeno paketom mjera za ublažavanje fiskalnog uticaja, uključujući oslobađanje od naknada za pogođene preduzetnike i javnu nadzornu tablu transparentnosti koja prati pokazatelje oporavka IRMS-a.
key_changes_mne: Aktivirati procedure registracije iz perioda prije IRMS-a kao privremeni paralelni kanal u roku od 48 sati
Utvrditi čvrst rok od 10 dana za Vladu da usvoji hitne regulatorne izmjene kojima se rješavaju sve identifikovane normativne praznine
Uspostaviti svakodnevnu međuresorsku radnu grupu (Finansije, Pravda, Ekonomija, Poreska uprava) za identifikovanje i rješavanje preostalih pravnih nekonzistentnosti
Osloboditi od naknada za registraciju i ubrzati obradu za sva preduzeća kojima je kasnjenjem nanijeta šteta zbog zastoja
Pokrenuti javnu Nadzornu tablu oporavka IRMS-a koja objavljuje dnevne podatke o obrađenim i čekajućim registracijama te prosječnim vremenima čekanja
Uvesti obaveznu provjeru pravne usklađenosti prije pokretanja za sva buduća uvođenja vladinih IT sistema
expected_benefits_mne: Trenutačno deblokiranje registracije preduzeća omogućilo bi oporavak procijenjenih 500–700 registracija mjesečno, vraćajući tok poreskih prihoda
Oslobađanje od naknada i ubrzana obrada demonstrirali bi odgovornost Vlade i djelimično povratili povjerenje investitora
Javna nadzorna tabla transparentnosti pružila bi odgovornost u realnom vremenu i smanjila informacionu asimetriju koja hrani negativno medijsko izvještavanje
Međuresorska radna grupa spriječila bi nastajanje novih normativnih praznina kako se proširuju funkcionalnosti IRMS-a
Obavezna pravna certifikacija prije pokretanja spriječila bi ponavljanje ovog tipa propusta u budućim projektima digitalizacije Vlade
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Aktiviranje procedura iz perioda prije IRMS-a zahtijeva samo administrativnu odluku, a ne zakonodavnu promjenu. Hitne regulatorne izmjene mogu se usvojiti vladinom uredbom u okviru postojećih odredbi o ubrzanom postupku. Oslobađanje od naknada u okviru je diskrecijskog ovlašćenja Ministarstva finansija. Nadzorna tabla transparentnosti može se izgraditi korišćenjem postojećih tokova podataka IRMS-a. Glavna prepreka je politička volja i međuresorska koordinacija, a ne tehnička ili pravna ograničenja.

assessments.summary_mne: Obustava registracije privrednih subjekata na nacionalnom nivou od januara 2026. godine predstavlja ozbiljan neuspjeh fiskalne uprave. Korijeni problema leže u normativnoj praznini: Vlada je pokrenula Integrisani sistem upravljanja prihodima (ISUP) 1. januara 2026. godine, bez prethodnog usklađivanja Zakona o privrednim društvima i podzakonskih akata sa zahtjevima novog poreskog sistema. Posljedica toga je period od skoro dva mjeseca u kojem nijedan novi privredni subjekt nije registrovan u Crnoj Gori, što direktno umanjuje prihode od poreza, narušava povjerenje investitora i protivriječi crnogorskim obavezama u okviru pristupanja EU u oblasti ekonomskog upravljanja i reforme poslovnog ambijenta.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ministarstvo finansija obustavu registracije privrednih subjekata ocjenjuje kao izrazito negativan ishod koji odražava fundamentalni propust u koordinaciji regulatorne reforme i uvođenja IT sistema. Najhitnija briga jeste neposredan i kumulativan gubitak poreskih prihoda i ekonomskog dinamizma u kritičnom periodu za pristupanje EU. Ključna preporuka je donošenje hitnih regulatornih izmjena u roku od dana, a ne sedmica, te uspostavljanje obaveznog postupka prethodne regulatorne provjere za sve buduće implementacije sistema.
key_concerns_mne: Direktan gubitak poreskih prihoda usljed procijenjeno 1.000 do 1.400 blokiranih registracija privrednih subjekata tokom skoro dva mjeseca, što utiče na poreze na dobit, PDV i fiskalne prihode vezane za zapošljavanje

Ozbiljno narušavanje ugleda crnogorskog investicionog ambijenta u trenutku kada su strana direktna ulaganja već prekomjerno koncentrisana u sektoru nekretnina (produktivna ulaganja pala su na 12,8% SDI u 2024. godini), a diversifikacija je od ključnog značaja

Ugrožavanje napretka u pristupanju EU, posebno u poglavljima 6 (Pravo privrednih društava) i 16 (Oporezivanje), u kojima su regulatorna predvidivost i institucionalni kapaciteti ključni kriterijumi ocjenjivanja

Sistemski propust u upravljanju: Poreska uprava potvrđuje da je problem normativne prirode (pravna neusklađenost), a ne tehničke, što ukazuje na to da je ISUP pokrenut bez završetka neophodnog pravnog usklađivanja
Kaskadni efekti na sivu ekonomiju: privredni subjekti koji nijesu u mogućnosti da se formalno registruju mogu poslovati neformalno, čime se proširuje siva ekonomija i dodatno erodira poreska osnovica
Dodatna prijavljena kašnjenja do 15 dana za potvrde o poreskoj usklađenosti stvaraju dodatne prepreke za postojeće privredne subjekte, pogoršavajući ekonomska opterećenja
recommendations_mne: Donijeti hitne regulatorne izmjene (uredbom Vlade ili hitnom parlamentarnom procedurom) u roku od 5 radnih dana radi otklanjanja normativne neusklađenosti između Zakona o privrednim društvima i zahtjeva ISUP-a

Uspostaviti privremeni paralelni kanal za registraciju korišćenjem procedura koje su prethodile ISUP-u, kako bi se odmah deblokirao postupak osnivanja novih privrednih subjekata dok se ne finalizuju trajna regulatorna rješenja

Sprovesti sveobuhvatnu procjenu regulatornog uticaja svih zakona i podzakonskih akata na koje ISUP utiče, kako bi se identifikovale i otklonile eventualne preostale normativne praznine prije nego što se manifestuju kao novi problemi

Uvesti obavezan međuministarski postupak provjere pravne usklađenosti za sve buduće implementacije IT sistema, uz zahtjev za saglasnost Ministarstva finansija, Ministarstva pravde i nadležnih resornih ministarstava prije puštanja u rad
Objavljivati sedmične javne izvještaje o pokazateljima učinka ISUP-a (broj obrađenih registracija, prosječna vremena obrade, veličina zaostatka) radi povratka povjerenja privrede i investitora
Naručiti nezavisnu reviziju postupka nabavke i implementacije ISUP-a kako bi se utvrdila odgovornost za propust u usklađivanju propisa prije pokretanja sistema

Obezbijediti fiskalnu podršku (oslobađanje od naknada ili ubrzana obrada) za privredne subjekte čija je registracija kasnila zbog kvara sistema, u cilju ublažavanja reputacijske štete i demonstriranja odgovornog odnosa

assessments.summary: The nationwide halt of business registration since January 2026 represents a severe rule-of-law failure and a direct violation of the constitutional guarantee of freedom of entrepreneurship under Article 59 of the Montenegrin Constitution. The government enacted two conflicting laws in July 2025 -- the Law on Business Companies and the Law on Registration of Business and Other Subjects -- that took effect on January 1, 2026, without ensuring their mutual consistency or the readiness of implementing regulations. The Tax Administration itself acknowledges the problem is normative rather than technical, meaning the government created a legal vacuum through legislative negligence. Nearly two months of complete paralysis in company formation inflicts concrete economic harm on citizens, undermines investor confidence, and directly contradicts Montenegro's EU accession commitments under Chapters 6 and 23.
assessments.executive_summary: This decision warrants a strongly negative verdict: the government's failure to harmonize two laws it enacted simultaneously has created an unconstitutional barrier to business formation lasting nearly two months. The top concern is the direct violation of Article 59 of the Constitution guaranteeing freedom of entrepreneurship, compounded by severe damage to legal certainty and EU accession credibility. The Ministry of Justice recommends emergency regulatory action within days, not weeks, coupled with establishment of a mandatory legal harmonization review mechanism to prevent recurrence.
assessments.reasoning:
Constitutional Compliance: Article 59 of the Montenegrin Constitution guarantees the freedom of entrepreneurship, stating it may only be restricted to protect health, environment, natural resources, cultural heritage, or security and defense. None of these exceptions apply here. The complete inability to register a business since January 1, 2026 constitutes a de facto prohibition of entrepreneurship -- not a permissible restriction, but an outright denial of a constitutional right. This is not a deliberate policy choice but a result of legislative negligence, which makes it even more troubling from a constitutional standpoint. Citizens whose fundamental rights are violated by government incompetence deserve the same protection as those whose rights are violated by government intent.

Rule of Law: The principle of legal certainty -- a cornerstone of the rule of law -- requires that laws be clear, consistent, and practically implementable. The government adopted the Law on Business Companies and the Law on Registration of Business and Other Subjects in July 2025, with both taking effect on January 1, 2026. Yet these two laws contain irreconcilable provisions: the Registration Law mandates electronic-only registration, while the Business Companies Law requires notarial documentation that cannot be processed electronically under current frameworks. As Tax Administration Director Sava Laketic publicly acknowledged, the problem is normative and rooted in legal uncertainty, meaning regulators cannot accept compliant documentation because the laws themselves are contradictory. This is a textbook failure of the legislative process. The government had approximately five months between enactment and implementation to identify and resolve these conflicts, and it failed to do so.

Human Rights Implications: Beyond Article 59 of the Constitution, the halt implicates Article 1 of Protocol 1 to the European Convention on Human Rights, which protects possessions and economic interests. The ECHR has recognized that business licenses and the ability to conduct business constitute possessions within the meaning of Protocol 1. A blanket inability to form a business entity deprives citizens of economic opportunity without legal basis. Additionally, the reported 15-day delays in obtaining tax compliance certificates affects existing businesses, compounding the harm.

EU Accession and Legal Harmonization: This failure is deeply problematic in the context of Montenegro's EU accession negotiations. While the new laws were ostensibly designed to align Montenegrin corporate law with the EU acquis, their contradictory implementation achieves the opposite. Chapter 23 (Judiciary and Fundamental Rights) requires demonstrated commitment to the rule of law, including legal certainty and effective public administration. Chapter 6 (Company Law) requires a functioning company registration system. The European Commission's 2025 progress report already noted that legislative alignment had slowed in chapters 23 and 24. A two-month registration freeze -- caused entirely by the government's own legislative carelessness -- provides concrete evidence of systemic governance failures that EU assessors will note. Montenegro's stated ambition to close all negotiation chapters by 2026 is directly undermined by this situation.

Anti-Corruption Concerns: While there is no direct evidence of corruption in this specific failure, the regulatory vacuum creates conditions that can facilitate corruption. When formal channels for business registration are blocked, pressure builds for informal solutions, workarounds, or preferential treatment. Any ad hoc mechanisms created to process registrations outside the broken system risk lacking proper oversight and transparency. The government should be mindful that regulatory failures of this nature, even when caused by incompetence rather than intent, can erode public trust and create opportunities for abuse.

Legislative Process Failures: The root cause of this crisis is a failure of legislative quality control. Two laws drafted and adopted as part of the same reform package should have been reviewed for mutual consistency before enactment. The absence of adequate regulatory impact assessments, the lack of a transition period with contingency provisions, and the failure to prepare implementing regulations before the laws took effect all point to systemic weaknesses in Montenegro's legislative process. Director Laketic's admission that they were not entirely aware of the magnitude of the impact suggests insufficient stakeholder consultation during the drafting phase.
key_concerns: Direct violation of Article 59 of the Montenegrin Constitution, which guarantees freedom of entrepreneurship -- a fundamental right that has been effectively suspended for nearly two months without any lawful justification

Complete breakdown of legal certainty caused by two contradictory laws (Law on Business Companies and Law on Registration of Business and Other Subjects) enacted simultaneously by the same government, demonstrating a systemic failure in legislative quality control

Severe damage to Montenegro's EU accession credibility, particularly under Chapter 23 (rule of law) and Chapter 6 (company law), at a critical juncture when Montenegro aims to close all negotiation chapters

Economic harm to citizens and foreign investors unable to establish businesses, with potential chilling effects on investment that will persist beyond the technical resolution of the registration halt
Risk that the regulatory vacuum incentivizes informal or corrupt pathways for business formation, undermining the institutional integrity that anti-corruption reforms are meant to build
Reported 15-day delays in issuing tax compliance certificates to existing businesses, indicating the registration freeze is symptomatic of broader dysfunction in the Tax Administration's new system
recommendations: Enact emergency regulatory amendments within 5 working days to resolve the conflict between the Law on Business Companies and the Law on Registration of Business and Other Subjects, using an expedited parliamentary procedure given the constitutional rights at stake

Establish a temporary transitional mechanism allowing both paper-based notarial and electronic registration to operate in parallel until full electronic registration infrastructure is tested and certified as legally compliant

The Ministry of Justice should conduct a formal constitutional review of the registration halt to determine whether affected citizens are entitled to compensation for economic losses caused by the government's legislative failure

Institute a mandatory Legal Harmonization Review (ex-ante regulatory impact assessment) for all legislation packages before enactment, requiring certification that new laws are mutually consistent and that implementing regulations are prepared

Commission an independent audit of the entire legislative process that led to this failure, including the adequacy of stakeholder consultations, inter-ministerial coordination, and regulatory readiness assessments

Issue a formal public communication acknowledging the constitutional dimension of the problem and providing a binding timeline for resolution, rather than vague assurances of functionality within weeks
Proactively brief EU delegations and the European Commission on remedial measures to mitigate damage to Montenegro's Chapter 23 and Chapter 6 assessments

counter_proposal.title: Emergency Legal Harmonization and Constitutional Remediation Framework
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Justice would have prevented this crisis through a mandatory pre-enactment legal consistency review and would now resolve it through a three-track approach: immediate emergency regulatory fix within days, a transitional dual-channel registration system, and a structural reform of the legislative process to prevent recurrence. Rather than waiting for parliamentary amendments over weeks, we would invoke the government's constitutional obligation to protect fundamental rights by issuing emergency bylaws and interpretive guidance that allow registration to resume immediately while permanent legislative fixes proceed in parallel.
key_changes: Issue an emergency government decree within 48 hours providing interpretive guidance that resolves the conflict between electronic registration requirements and notarial documentation, enabling the Tax Administration to accept both formats during a transitional period

Deploy a legal task force composed of Ministry of Justice, Ministry of Finance, and Tax Administration experts to draft permanent harmonizing amendments within 10 days, with fast-track parliamentary adoption

Establish a mandatory Legal Consistency Certification process requiring the Ministry of Justice to sign off on all legislative packages before they proceed to parliamentary vote, certifying that constituent laws are mutually compatible
Create a citizen redress mechanism for businesses that suffered quantifiable economic losses due to the registration halt, funded from the state budget as acknowledgment of governmental liability
Implement a mandatory 90-day pilot testing period for any new regulatory system before full nationwide rollout, including stress-testing against all related legislation
expected_benefits: Immediate resumption of business registration, ending the constitutional violation within days rather than weeks
Restoration of legal certainty and investor confidence through clear transitional rules
Structural prevention of future legislative conflicts through mandatory consistency certification
Accountability and citizen trust through a formal redress mechanism
Stronger EU accession positioning by demonstrating capacity for rapid self-correction and institutional learning
Reduced risk of corruption by closing the regulatory vacuum that incentivizes informal workarounds
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The emergency decree and interpretive guidance are within the government's existing executive authority and can be issued within 48 hours. The transitional dual-channel registration requires only administrative instruction to the Tax Administration. The permanent legislative fix requires parliamentary action but can be expedited given cross-party interest in resolving an issue that harms all constituencies. The Legal Consistency Certification process is a procedural reform that requires no new legislation, only a government decision on internal procedures. The citizen redress mechanism is the most politically challenging element but is legally straightforward under existing state liability frameworks.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Okvir za hitnu pravnu harmonizaciju i ustavnu sanaciju
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo pravde bi bilo spriječilo ovu krizu kroz obaveznu provjeru pravne usklađenosti prije donošenja propisa, a sada bi je riješilo putem trostrukog pristupa: hitnim regulatornim rješenjem u roku od nekoliko dana, prelaznim sistemom registracije sa dva kanala i strukturnom reformom zakonodavnog procesa radi sprečavanja ponavljanja. Umjesto čekanja na parlamentarne izmjene koje bi trajale nedeljama, pozvali bismo se na ustavnu obavezu Vlade da zaštiti osnovna prava, donošenjem hitnih podzakonskih akata i tumačnih smjernica koje bi omogućile neposredno nastavljanje registracije, dok paralelno teku trajne zakonodavne izmjene.
key_changes_mne: Donošenje hitne vladine uredbe u roku od 48 sati kojom se daju tumačne smjernice za rješavanje sukoba između zahtjeva za elektronsku registraciju i notarske dokumentacije, čime bi se Poreskoj upravi omogućilo prihvatanje oba formata tokom prelaznog perioda

Uspostavljanje pravne radne grupe sastavljene od stručnjaka Ministarstva pravde, Ministarstva finansija i Poreske uprave radi izrade trajnih usklađujućih izmjena u roku od 10 dana, uz ubrzano parlamentarno usvajanje

Uvođenje obaveznog postupka pravne certifikacije usklađenosti kojim se od Ministarstva pravde zahtijeva da odobri sve zakonodavne pakete prije upućivanja na parlamentarno glasanje, potvrđujući međusobnu kompatibilnost pojedinih zakona

Uspostavljanje mehanizma pravne zaštite za privredne subjekte koji su pretrpjeli mjerljive ekonomske gubitke usljed zastoja u registraciji, finansiranog iz državnog budžeta kao priznanje vladine odgovornosti
Uvođenje obaveznog probnog perioda od 90 dana za svaki novi regulatorni sistem prije punog uvođenja na nacionalnom nivou, uključujući testiranje opterećenja u odnosu na svu relevantnu legislativu
expected_benefits_mne: Neposredno nastavljanje registracije privrednih subjekata, čime se ustavna povreda okončava u roku od dana, a ne nedjelja
Uspostavljanje pravne sigurnosti i investitorskog povjerenja kroz jasna prelazna pravila
Strukturno sprečavanje budućih zakonodavnih sukoba kroz obaveznu certifikaciju usklađenosti
Odgovornost i povjerenje građana kroz formalni mehanizam pravne zaštite
Jačanje pozicije u procesu pristupanja EU demonstriranjem sposobnosti brzog samokorigovanja i institucionalnog učenja
Smanjenje rizika od korupcije zatvaranjem regulatornog vakuuma koji podstiče neformalna zaobilaženja propisa
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Hitna uredba i tumačne smjernice nalaze se u okviru postojećih izvršnih ovlašćenja Vlade i mogu biti donijete u roku od 48 sati. Prelazna registracija sa dva kanala zahtijeva samo administrativno uputstvo Poreskoj upravi. Trajne zakonodavne izmjene zahtijevaju parlamentarnu akciju, ali se mogu ubrzati s obzirom na međustranački interes za rješavanje pitanja koje šteti svim izbornim jedinicama. Postupak pravne certifikacije usklađenosti predstavlja proceduralnu reformu koja ne zahtijeva novo zakonodavstvo, već samo odluku Vlade o internim procedurama. Mehanizam pravne zaštite za građane je politički najzahtjevniji element, ali je pravno jednostavan u okviru postojećih propisa o odgovornosti države.

assessments.summary_mne: Nacionalni zastoj u registraciji preduzeća koji traje od januara 2026. godine predstavlja ozbiljan propust vladavine prava i direktno kršenje ustavne garancije slobode preduzetništva iz člana 59 Ustava Crne Gore. Vlada je u julu 2025. godine donijela dva međusobno suprotstavljena zakona -- Zakon o privrednim društvima i Zakon o registraciji privrednih i drugih subjekata -- koji su stupili na snagu 1. januara 2026. godine, bez obezbjeđivanja njihove međusobne usklađenosti ili spremnosti provedbenih propisa. Sama Poreska uprava priznaje da je problem normativne, a ne tehničke prirode, što znači da je vlada zakonodavnim nemarom stvorila pravni vakuum. Gotovo dva mjeseca potpune paralize u osnivanju privrednih društava nanosi konkretnu ekonomsku štetu građanima, potkopava povjerenje investitora i direktno je u suprotnosti sa obavezama Crne Gore u okviru procesa pristupanja EU prema Poglavljima 6 i 23.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ova odluka zaslužuje izrazito negativnu ocjenu: propust Vlade da uskladi dva zakona koja je istovremeno donijela stvorio je neustavnu prepreku za osnivanje preduzeća koja traje gotovo dva mjeseca. Ključni razlog za zabrinutost jeste direktno kršenje člana 59 Ustava kojim se garantuje sloboda preduzetništva, čemu se pridodaje ozbiljna šteta po pravnu sigurnost i kredibilitet u procesu pristupanja EU. Ministarstvo pravde preporučuje hitno regulatorno djelovanje u roku od dana, a ne sedmica, uz uspostavljanje obaveznog mehanizma za provjeru pravne usklađenosti kako bi se spriječilo ponavljanje ovakvih situacija.
key_concerns_mne: Direktno kršenje člana 59 Ustava Crne Gore, kojim se garantuje sloboda preduzetništva -- temeljno pravo koje je de facto suspendovano gotovo dva mjeseca bez ikakvog zakonskog opravdanja

Potpuni slom pravne sigurnosti uzrokovan dvama međusobno suprotnim zakonima (Zakon o privrednim društvima i Zakon o registraciji privrednih i drugih subjekata) koje je ista vlada donijela istovremeno, što ukazuje na sistemski propust u kontroli kvaliteta zakonodavstva

Ozbiljna šteta po kredibilitet Crne Gore u procesu pristupanja EU, posebno u okviru Poglavlja 23 (vladavina prava) i Poglavlja 6 (pravo privrednih društava), u ključnom trenutku kada Crna Gora nastoji da zatvori sva pregovaračka poglavlja

Ekonomska šteta koja se nanosi građanima i stranim investitorima koji ne mogu da osnuju preduzeće, uz potencijalne efekte odvraćanja od ulaganja koji će se osjećati i nakon tehničkog rješavanja zastoja u registraciji
Rizik da regulatorni vakuum potiče neformalne ili koruptivne puteve za osnivanje preduzeća, čime se potkopava institucionalni integritet koji antikorupcijske reforme treba da izgrade

Prijavljeni zastoji od 15 dana u izdavanju potvrda o poreskom izmirenju postojećim preduzećima, što ukazuje na to da je zamrzavanje registracije simptom širih disfunkcija u novom sistemu Poreske uprave
recommendations_mne: Donijeti hitne regulatorne izmjene u roku od 5 radnih dana radi rješavanja sukoba između Zakona o privrednim društvima i Zakona o registraciji privrednih i drugih subjekata, primjenom ubrzane parlamentarne procedure s obzirom na ustavna prava koja su ugrožena

Uspostaviti privremeni prelazni mehanizam koji omogućava paralelno funkcionisanje papirne notarske i elektronske registracije sve dok infrastruktura za elektronsku registraciju ne bude testirana i certificirana kao pravno usklađena

Ministarstvo pravde treba da sprovede formalnu ustavnopravnu analizu zastoja u registraciji kako bi se utvrdilo da li pogođeni građani imaju pravo na naknadu ekonomske štete nastale zakonodavnim propustom Vlade

Uvesti obaveznu Provjeru pravne usklađenosti (ex-ante procjenu regulatornog uticaja) za sve zakonodavne pakete prije njihovog usvajanja, uz obaveznu potvrdu da su novi zakoni međusobno usklađeni i da su provedbeni propisi pripremljeni

Naručiti nezavisnu reviziju cjelokupnog zakonodavnog procesa koji je doveo do ovog propusta, uključujući adekvatnost konsultacija s dionicima, međuresornu koordinaciju i procjene regulatorne spremnosti
Izdati formalnu javnu saopštenje kojim se priznaje ustavna dimenzija problema i utvrđuje obavezujući rokovi za rješavanje, umjesto nejasnih uvjeravanja o funkcionalnosti sistema u narednim sedmicama
Proaktivno informisati delegacije EU i Evropsku komisiju o poduzetim korektivnim mjerama kako bi se ublažila šteta po ocjene Crne Gore u okviru Poglavlja 23 i Poglavlja 6

assessments.summary: The complete halt of business registration across Montenegro since January 1, 2026 represents a severe failure of regulatory governance with devastating consequences for economic development, employment, and investor confidence. The root cause -- normative misalignment between the Law on Business Companies and the Law on Registration of Business Entities, both adopted in July 2025 and made effective January 1, 2026 -- was entirely foreseeable and preventable. Nearly two months with zero new company formations in a country where 99.3% of enterprises are small businesses, and where annual business creation drives employment growth, constitutes an economic emergency that undermines Montenegro's EU accession credibility and FDI attractiveness.
assessments.executive_summary: This decision outcome is strongly negative: a complete nationwide halt of business registration for nearly two months is an unprecedented regulatory failure that directly suppresses job creation, deters foreign investment, and pushes entrepreneurial activity into the informal economy. The top concern is that this was entirely preventable -- the legislative misalignment between two laws adopted simultaneously was foreseeable, yet no transitional provisions or regulatory impact assessment caught the conflict before the January 1 effective date. The key recommendation is immediate adoption of emergency transitional regulations allowing parallel paper-and-electronic registration while permanent legislative fixes proceed through parliament.
assessments.reasoning:
The analysis rests on several critical dimensions that fall squarely within the Ministry of Economy and Labour's mandate.

ECONOMIC IMPACT AND BUSINESS FORMATION: Montenegro had approximately 58,998 active enterprises in 2024, with 9.1% year-on-year growth, meaning roughly 5,000-5,500 new businesses were formed that year. With 99.33% of enterprises being small businesses, the SME sector is the backbone of the Montenegrin economy. A complete freeze on new company registration since January 1, 2026 -- now approaching nearly two months -- means potentially 800-900 businesses that would have been created in this period simply do not exist. Each of these represents lost employment, lost tax revenue, and lost economic activity. For a small economy of approximately 6 billion EUR GDP, this is not marginal -- it is structurally damaging.

REGULATORY FAILURE ANALYSIS: The core problem is a normative conflict between the Law on Business Companies (Zakon o privrednim drustvima) and the Law on Registration of Business and Other Subjects (Zakon o registraciji privrednih i drugih subjekata), both adopted by Parliament in July 2025 and made effective January 1, 2026. The new registration law mandates strict electronic processes through the IRMS (Integrated Revenue Management System), but notaries cannot authenticate documents in formats required by the Tax Administration without violating the electronic registration mandate. This creates a legal catch-22 acknowledged by Tax Administration Director Sava Laketic: accepting notarized papers breaches legal requirements, but rejecting them halts all registration. The fact that two laws adopted in the same parliamentary session by the same government are incompatible reflects a fundamental failure of regulatory quality control, inter-ministerial coordination, and regulatory impact assessment (RIA) processes that Montenegro is required to implement under EU accession Chapter 3 (Right of Establishment and Freedom to Provide Services).

FOREIGN DIRECT INVESTMENT CONSEQUENCES: Montenegro attracted USD 532 million in net FDI in 2024, with FDI accounting for 6.8% of GDP. However, 60% of FDI is concentrated in real estate, and the country has been actively trying to diversify toward productive sectors. The Montenegrin Foreign Investors Council (MFIC) Ease of Doing Business Index already stands at only 6.5 out of 10, with bureaucratic obstacles cited as a major concern. A situation where it is literally impossible to register a new company for two months sends a catastrophic signal to potential investors. The 2025 U.S. Investment Climate Statement already noted "sluggish bureaucracy" as an obstacle; this episode confirms the worst fears of international investors. Companies planning to establish operations in Montenegro -- whether in IT, renewable energy, or manufacturing -- cannot proceed, and some will redirect to Serbia, North Macedonia, or Albania where registration remains functional.

LABOR MARKET EFFECTS: With unemployment at approximately 11% and an informal economy estimated at 30% of GDP, Montenegro cannot afford any disruption to formal business creation. New company formation is the primary channel for formal job creation, particularly for youth (youth unemployment remains significantly higher than the overall rate). The registration freeze has three labor market consequences: (1) would-be entrepreneurs who cannot register will either delay hiring or operate informally, expanding the shadow economy; (2) workers who would have been formally employed at new enterprises remain unemployed or informally employed without labor protections; (3) the emigration of working-age population -- already a critical demographic challenge -- is reinforced when talented individuals see that even starting a business is impossible due to government incompetence.

ADDITIONAL COLLATERAL DAMAGE: Beyond registration, citizens and businesses report that tax clearance certificates (potvrde o izmirenim poreskim obavezama) now take up to 15 days to obtain, compared to much shorter timeframes previously. These certificates are required for public procurement participation, bank loans, and various administrative procedures. This secondary effect further constrains existing businesses and creates a broader economic chilling effect.

EU ACCESSION IMPLICATIONS: Montenegro's EU accession process requires alignment with the acquis across multiple chapters. Chapter 3 (Right of Establishment) requires that business registration be efficient and non-discriminatory. Chapter 6 (Company Law) requires functional company registration systems. Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment) requires that labor market institutions function properly. A two-month nationwide registration freeze is the kind of systemic failure that EU progress reports highlight as evidence of insufficient administrative capacity. It also contradicts Montenegro's commitments under the Western Balkans Common Regional Market initiative, which aims to facilitate cross-border business establishment.

PREVENTABILITY: Tax Administration Director Laketic himself acknowledged the Tax Administration "underestimated the operational impact" and was not "fully aware how significantly this would affect business processes." This admission confirms that no proper regulatory impact assessment was conducted before the laws took effect. A professional workshop on the application of the new laws was held on January 29, 2026 -- nearly a month after they became effective -- which is far too late. The regulatory impact assessment should have identified the notary-electronic registration incompatibility months before the effective date.
key_concerns: Zero new businesses registered nationwide for nearly two months, potentially blocking 800-900 company formations and associated job creation

The regulatory conflict between the Law on Business Companies and the Law on Registration was entirely foreseeable and reflects a systemic failure of inter-ministerial coordination and regulatory impact assessment
Foreign investors receive a devastating signal about Montenegro's business environment reliability, risking FDI diversion to competing Western Balkan economies
Would-be entrepreneurs are pushed toward informal economy operations, worsening the already estimated 30% shadow economy share of GDP
Tax clearance certificates now take up to 15 days, creating cascading delays for existing businesses in public procurement and banking
The freeze disproportionately impacts northern and rural regions where new SME formation is critical for reducing regional economic disparities
No emergency transitional measures were adopted to allow continued registration while legislative fixes proceed, suggesting inadequate crisis management
EU accession credibility is damaged, particularly regarding Chapter 3 (Right of Establishment) and administrative capacity benchmarks
recommendations: Immediately adopt emergency government decree or ministerial instruction establishing transitional registration procedures that allow parallel paper and electronic submissions until permanent legislative harmonization is completed

Fast-track parliamentary amendments to resolve the normative conflict between the Law on Business Companies and the Law on Registration of Business Entities, with a target adoption deadline of no more than two weeks
Establish a retroactive simplified registration window for all businesses that attempted to register during the freeze period, with fee waivers and expedited processing

Conduct a formal post-incident review of why the regulatory impact assessment process failed to identify the incompatibility between the two laws before their effective date, and publish findings publicly
Mandate that the Tax Administration restore tax clearance certificate issuance to a maximum of 3 business days, with interim self-declaration mechanisms where certificates are delayed

Create a dedicated task force including the Ministry of Finance, Ministry of Economy, Tax Administration, and Notary Chamber to stress-test all pending regulatory changes against existing legislation before future effective dates
Establish a public dashboard tracking daily business registration volumes to provide transparency and early warning for any future systemic disruptions
Formally notify the EU Delegation and relevant Chapter negotiation teams about remedial actions taken, to mitigate damage to the accession progress narrative

counter_proposal.title: Emergency Dual-Track Registration and Regulatory Harmonization Protocol
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Economy and Labour would have ensured continuity of business registration through three immediate actions: first, issuing an emergency ministerial instruction within 48 hours of identifying the problem (which should have been January 2 at the latest) establishing temporary paper-based registration procedures alongside the electronic IRMS system; second, convening the Notary Chamber and Tax Administration for an emergency session to agree on interim document formats acceptable to both legal frameworks; third, establishing a dedicated registration helpline and temporary physical registration points in each municipality. Simultaneously, we would fast-track a single omnibus legislative amendment covering both the Law on Business Companies and the Law on Registration to permanently resolve the conflict, rather than waiting for the standard parliamentary timeline.
key_changes: Issue emergency ministerial instruction within 48 hours allowing temporary paper-based registration in parallel with electronic IRMS, using a legal basis in the Law on State Administration
Convene emergency joint session of Tax Administration, Notary Chamber, and Ministry of Justice to agree on interim acceptable document formats
Establish temporary physical registration desks in all 25 municipalities, staffed by Tax Administration personnel with authority to process applications
Submit omnibus legislative amendment to Parliament under urgent procedure covering both conflicting laws simultaneously
Grant automatic 60-day extensions on all administrative deadlines for businesses affected by the registration freeze
Create retroactive fee waiver and expedited processing for all applications submitted during the freeze period
expected_benefits: Business registration restored within days rather than weeks or months, preventing further economic damage
Estimated 800-900 blocked business formations proceed, generating immediate employment and tax revenue
Foreign investor confidence partially restored through demonstrated crisis management capacity
Informal economy expansion prevented by maintaining accessible formal registration channels
EU accession narrative protected by showing rapid institutional response to regulatory failures
Northern and rural municipalities maintain access to business formation, supporting regional development goals
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. Emergency ministerial instructions are a standard administrative tool under the Law on State Administration and can be issued without parliamentary approval. The Notary Chamber and Tax Administration have an existing institutional relationship. Physical registration points existed before IRMS and could be reactivated quickly. The omnibus legislative amendment under urgent parliamentary procedure typically requires 7-14 days. The main political obstacle is institutional pride and reluctance to acknowledge the severity of the failure, but the economic costs of inaction far exceed the political costs of emergency action.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Protokol o hitnoj dvojnoj registraciji i harmonizaciji propisa
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo ekonomije i rada bilo bi osiguralo kontinuitet registracije privrednih subjekata putem tri hitne mjere: prvo, izdavanjem hitnog ministarskog uputstva u roku od 48 sati od utvrđivanja problema (što je trebalo biti najkasnije 2. januara), kojim bi se uspostavile privremene procedure registracije na papiru uz elektronski IRMS sistem; drugo, sazivanjem hitne sjednice Notarske komore i Poreske uprave radi dogovora o privremenim formatima dokumenata prihvatljivim u okviru oba pravna sistema; treće, uspostavljanjem posebne linije za pomoć pri registraciji i privremenih fizičkih registracionih punktova u svakoj opštini. Istovremeno, ubrzano bismo sproveli jedinstvenu sveobuhvatnu zakonodavnu izmjenu koja bi obuhvatila i Zakon o privrednim društvima i Zakon o registraciji, s ciljem trajnog rješavanja sukoba propisa, umjesto čekanja na standardnu parlamentarnu proceduru.
key_changes_mne: Izdavanje hitnog ministarskog uputstva u roku od 48 sati kojim se dopušta privremena registracija na papiru paralelno s elektronskim IRMS-om, uz primjenu pravnog osnova iz Zakona o državnoj upravi
Sazivanje hitne zajedničke sjednice Poreske uprave, Notarske komore i Ministarstva pravde radi dogovora o privremeno prihvatljivim formatima dokumenata
Uspostavljanje privremenih fizičkih registracionih šaltera u svih 25 opština, uz osoblje Poreske uprave ovlašćeno za obradu zahtjeva
Podnošenje sveobuhvatnih zakonodavnih izmjena Skupštini po hitnom postupku, uz istovremeno obuhvatanje oba zakona u sukobu
Odobravanje automatskog produženja od 60 dana za sve administrativne rokove privrednih subjekata pogođenih zamrzavanjem registracije
Uvođenje retroaktivnog oslobađanja od taksi i ubrzane obrade za sve zahtjeve podnijete tokom perioda zamrzavanja
expected_benefits_mne: Registracija privrednih subjekata obnovljena u roku od nekoliko dana, a ne sedmica ili mjeseci, čime se sprečava dalja ekonomska šteta
Procijenjenih 800–900 blokiranih osnivanja privrednih subjekata biva realizovano, čime se ostvaruje neposredno zapošljavanje i prihodi od poreza
Djelimično obnovljeno povjerenje stranih investitora kroz demonstriranu sposobnost upravljanja kriznim situacijama
Sprečavanje širenja sive ekonomije kroz održavanje pristupačnih formalnih kanala registracije
Zaštita narativa o pristupanju EU kroz prikaz brzog institucionalnog odgovora na propuste u regulativi
Sjeverne i ruralne opštine zadržavaju pristup osnivanju privrednih subjekata, čime se podržavaju ciljevi regionalnog razvoja
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Hitna ministarska uputstva standardni su administrativni instrument predviđen Zakonom o državnoj upravi i mogu se izdati bez odobrenja Skupštine. Notarska komora i Poreska uprava imaju uspostavljen institucionalni odnos. Fizički registracioni punktovi postojali su prije uvođenja IRMS-a i mogli bi se brzo reaktivirati. Sveobuhvatne zakonodavne izmjene po hitnom parlamentarnom postupku obično zahtijevaju 7 do 14 dana. Glavna politička prepreka jeste institucionalni ponos i nevoljkost da se prizna ozbiljnost propusta, ali ekonomski troškovi nečinjenja daleko premašuju političke troškove hitnog djelovanja.

assessments.summary_mne: Potpuni zastoj registracije preduzeća širom Crne Gore od 1. januara 2026. godine predstavlja ozbiljan neuspjeh regulatornog upravljanja s razornim posljedicama po ekonomski razvoj, zapošljavanje i povjerenje investitora. Glavni uzrok — normativna neusklađenost između Zakona o privrednim društvima i Zakona o registraciji privrednih subjekata, oba usvojena u julu 2025. i stupila na snagu 1. januara 2026. — bio je u potpunosti predvidiv i mogao se spriječiti. Skoro dva mjeseca bez ijednog osnivanja novog preduzeća u zemlji gdje 99,3% privrednih subjekata čine mala preduzeća, i gdje godišnje osnivanje firmi pokreće rast zaposlenosti, predstavlja ekonomsku vanrednu situaciju koja narušava kredibilitet Crne Gore u procesu pristupanja EU i njenu atraktivnost za strana direktna ulaganja.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ishod ove odluke je izrazito negativan: potpuni zastoj registracije preduzeća širom države u trajanju od skoro dva mjeseca predstavlja neviđen regulatorni neuspjeh koji direktno guši otvaranje radnih mjesta, odbija strane investitore i tjera preduzetničku aktivnost u sivu ekonomiju. Ključna zabrinutost leži u tome što je sve ovo bilo u potpunosti moguće spriječiti — zakonodavna neusklađenost između dva istovremeno usvojena zakona bila je predvidiva, a ipak nijedna prelazna odredba niti procjena uticaja propisa nije uočila taj sukob prije stupanja na snagu 1. januara. Ključna preporuka jeste hitno usvajanje vanrednih prelaznih propisa koji će omogućiti paralelnu papirnu i elektronsku registraciju dok trajni zakonodavni ispravci prolaze parlamentarnu proceduru.
key_concerns_mne: Nula novih preduzeća registrovanih širom države skoro dva mjeseca, što potencijalno blokira 800–900 osnivanja kompanija i s tim povezano otvaranje radnih mjesta
Regulatorni sukob između Zakona o privrednim društvima i Zakona o registraciji bio je u potpunosti predvidiv i odražava sistemski neuspjeh međuresorske koordinacije i procjene uticaja propisa
Strani investitori dobijaju poražavajući signal o pouzdanosti poslovnog ambijenta Crne Gore, uz rizik preusmjeravanja stranih direktnih ulaganja prema konkurentskim ekonomijama Zapadnog Balkana
Potencijalni preduzetnici bivaju gurnuti prema poslovanju u sivoj ekonomiji, čime se pogoršava već procijenjeno učešće sive ekonomije od 30% u BDP-u
Uvjerenja o poreskom stanju sada se čekaju i do 15 dana, što izaziva kaskadna kašnjenja za postojeća preduzeća u javnim nabavkama i bankarstvu
Zamrzavanje nesrazmjerno pogađa sjeverne i ruralne krajeve, gdje je osnivanje novih malih i srednjih preduzeća ključno za smanjenje regionalnih ekonomskih nejednakosti
Nisu usvojene nikakve vanredne prelazne mjere koje bi omogućile nastavak registracije dok traje zakonodavno usklađivanje, što ukazuje na neadekvatno upravljanje krizom
Kredibilitet u procesu pristupanja EU je narušen, posebno u pogledu Poglavlja 3 (Pravo poslovnog nastana) i mjerila administrativnih kapaciteta
recommendations_mne: Hitno usvojiti uredbu Vlade ili uputstvo ministarstva kojim se uspostavljaju prelazne procedure registracije, a koje omogućavaju paralelno podnošenje papirne i elektronske dokumentacije dok se ne završi trajno zakonodavno usklađivanje

Ubrzano provesti parlamentarne izmjene radi rješavanja normativnog sukoba između Zakona o privrednim društvima i Zakona o registraciji privrednih subjekata, s ciljanim rokom usvajanja od najviše dvije sedmice
Uspostaviti retroaktivni pojednostavljeni registracioni prozor za sva preduzeća koja su pokušala da se registruju tokom perioda zamrzavanja, uz oslobađanje od taksi i ubrzanu obradu zahtjeva

Sprovesti formalnu analizu incidenta radi utvrđivanja zašto proces procjene uticaja propisa nije prepoznao nekompatibilnost između dva zakona prije datuma njihovog stupanja na snagu, te javno objaviti nalaze
Naložiti Poreskoj upravi da vrati rok izdavanja uvjerenja o poreskom stanju na najviše 3 radna dana, uz uvođenje privremenih mehanizama samodeklarisanja u slučajevima kašnjenja

Formirati posebnu radnu grupu u kojoj bi učestvovali Ministarstvo finansija, Ministarstvo ekonomije, Poreska uprava i Notarska komora, kako bi se svi predstojeći regulatorni propisi provjerili u odnosu na postojeće zakonodavstvo prije budućih datuma stupanja na snagu
Uspostaviti javnu kontrolnu tablu koja svakodnevno prati obim registracije preduzeća, radi obezbjeđivanja transparentnosti i ranog upozorenja na eventualne buduće sistemske poremećaje
Zvanično obavijestiti Delegaciju EU i nadležne timove za pregovaračka poglavlja o preduzetim sanacionim mjerama, kako bi se ublažila šteta po narativ o napretku u procesu pristupanja

assessments.summary: The complete halt of business registration across Montenegro since January 2026 represents a serious failure of legislative coordination that directly undermines multiple EU accession commitments. The root cause -- normative misalignment between the new Law on Business Companies (effective 1 January 2026) and existing regulations governing pre-registration verification procedures -- reveals a systemic failure in the legislative drafting and harmonization process. This is especially damaging given that Montenegro provisionally closed Chapters 3, 6, and 20 in recent years on the basis of demonstrated competence in company law, establishment rights, and enterprise policy. A nearly two-month nationwide shutdown of new company formation directly contradicts the EU Reform Agenda's objective of increasing the number of enterprises and formalizing the economy, and puts at risk up to EUR 383.5 million in Growth Plan funding tied to business environment reforms.
assessments.executive_summary: This decision warrants a strongly negative verdict. A nearly two-month nationwide freeze on business registration caused by legislative misalignment between the new Company Law and existing regulations represents a fundamental failure of regulatory coordination that jeopardizes provisionally closed Chapters 3, 6, and 20, and directly contradicts the EU Reform Agenda commitments tied to EUR 383.5 million in Growth Plan funding. The government must immediately adopt emergency regulatory harmonization measures and establish a mandatory pre-implementation legislative compatibility review mechanism to prevent such failures in the future.
assessments.reasoning:
REGULATORY FAILURE AND LEGISLATIVE COORDINATION

The situation is unambiguous: Montenegro adopted a new Law on Business Companies in August 2025, with full application from 1 January 2026, alongside the deployment of the new IRMS (Integrated Revenue Management System) by the Tax Administration. However, as confirmed by the Tax Administration itself, the regulations governing pre-registration document verification and authentication procedures were not updated to align with the new company law framework. This normative gap has made it impossible to register any new business entity since the start of 2026. As of the reporting date (20 February 2026), this represents approximately 50 days of complete registration paralysis nationwide.

The Tax Administration's own statement -- that the issue is "normative rather than technical" -- is both an admission and an indictment. It means this was an entirely preventable failure. The government had over four months (August to December 2025) between the law's adoption and its application date to identify and resolve regulatory inconsistencies. The failure to do so points to inadequate inter-institutional coordination, specifically between the Ministry of Finance, the Tax Administration, the CRPS (Central Registry of Business Entities), and the legislative drafting bodies.

IMPACT ON PROVISIONALLY CLOSED CHAPTERS

This situation is particularly alarming because it directly implicates three provisionally closed negotiation chapters:

Ch. 3 (Right of Establishment and Freedom to Provide Services) -- provisionally closed -- guarantees the freedom to establish a business. A blanket nationwide inability to register new companies is a de facto suspension of this fundamental EU single market freedom. While provisional closure does not mean the acquis obligations disappear, the European Commission monitors implementation continuously. A systemic failure of this nature could prompt the Commission to flag backsliding in its next progress report, potentially leading to calls for reopening the chapter.

Ch. 6 (Company Law) -- provisionally closed -- requires that company law frameworks function effectively and align with EU standards. The new Law on Business Companies was supposed to strengthen Montenegro's alignment with the EU acquis on company law. Instead, its implementation has created a legal vacuum. The irony is acute: a law designed to modernize and EU-align company governance has rendered company formation impossible.

Ch. 20 (Enterprise and Industrial Policy) -- provisionally closed in December 2024 -- specifically requires a functioning business environment that supports enterprise creation and growth. A complete halt in new business registration is the antithesis of what this chapter demands.

IMPACT ON OPEN CHAPTERS

Ch. 16 (Taxation) -- open -- The IRMS system is central to Montenegro's tax administration modernization. While the Tax Administration insists the system works, the fact that entrepreneurs report 15-day delays for tax compliance certificates (versus much shorter timeframes previously) contradicts claims of smooth functioning. These delays compound the registration halt, as tax compliance certificates are prerequisite documents for many business activities. This undermines credibility on Chapter 16 benchmarks related to efficient tax administration.

Ch. 23 (Judiciary and Fundamental Rights) -- open, key benchmark chapter -- Legal certainty is a fundamental principle under this chapter. The Tax Administration itself acknowledges "a certain level of legal uncertainty in specific procedures." When the state cannot guarantee that laws function coherently upon implementation, it raises fundamental rule-of-law concerns about the quality of the legislative process, impact assessment procedures, and inter-ministerial coordination.

Ch. 8 (Competition Policy) -- open -- A registration halt creates an artificial barrier to market entry. For nearly two months, no new competitor has been able to enter any market in Montenegro. This is a de facto restriction on competition that benefits incumbent businesses at the expense of aspiring entrepreneurs.

EU REFORM AGENDA AND GROWTH PLAN FUNDING

Montenegro's Reform Agenda 2024-2027 explicitly identifies improving the business environment and increasing the number of enterprises as priority objectives. The EU Growth Plan allocates EUR 383.5 million to Montenegro (EUR 110 million in grants, EUR 273.5 million in concessional loans), conditional on meeting reform benchmarks.
