One of the 32 priority reform measures specifically targets business environment improvements, including simplification and digitalization of company registration.

A two-month nationwide registration freeze directly contradicts these commitments. If the European Commission determines that Montenegro has failed to meet conditions related to business environment reform, it could delay or withhold subsequent tranches of Growth Plan funding. Montenegro has so far received only EUR 26.8 million in pre-financing (7% of the total allocation). The remaining disbursements depend on demonstrated reform progress -- not reform regression.

The EU's own assessment in the 2025 progress report already flagged "inefficiencies and delays in administrative decision-making, inconsistent law enforcement, and poor interpretation of laws by public authorities" as key business environment weaknesses. This registration halt validates every one of those concerns.

REGIONAL COMPARISON AND REPUTATIONAL DAMAGE

Among Western Balkans candidate countries, Montenegro has positioned itself as the frontrunner in EU accession negotiations. With 13 of 33 chapters provisionally closed (rising to 18 after the December 2025 intergovernmental conference), Montenegro leads the region. However, this kind of systemic administrative failure undermines that leadership position. Serbia, Albania, and North Macedonia all maintain functioning business registration systems. Montenegro's inability to register a single new business for nearly two months is an embarrassing anomaly that weakens its credibility as a candidate ready for membership.

Foreign investors monitoring Montenegro's business environment -- including those attracted by IPA-funded investment promotion -- will note this disruption. The signal it sends is that legislative implementation in Montenegro is unreliable, which directly impacts foreign direct investment prospects and the country's competitiveness.

ASSESSMENT OF GOVERNMENT RESPONSE

The government's response has been inadequate. The Tax Administration's defensive posture -- insisting the IRMS works while businesses cannot register -- misses the point entirely. The issue is not about a single system but about the government's failure to ensure regulatory coherence before implementing major legislative changes. The use of paper documents as an interim measure is a workaround, not a solution. There has been no public timeline for resolving the normative gaps, no acknowledgment of the severity of the situation from senior government officials, and no emergency legislative procedure initiated to close the regulatory gaps.

Sources consulted include CdM reporting (cdm.me), Radio-Televizija Niksic (rtnk.me), the European Commission 2025 Montenegro Progress Report, Montenegro's Reform Agenda 2024-2027, EU Growth Plan documentation, and the EU Council's economic reform programme assessment for Montenegro.
key_concerns: Ch. 3 Right of Establishment and Freedom to Provide Services (provisionally closed) -- A nationwide halt on business registration is a de facto suspension of the EU fundamental freedom of establishment. This could trigger Commission scrutiny and potential calls for chapter reopening in the next progress report.

Ch. 6 Company Law (provisionally closed) -- The very law intended to strengthen EU acquis alignment on company governance has created a legal vacuum that prevents company formation, directly contradicting the benchmarks under which this chapter was closed.

Ch. 20 Enterprise and Industrial Policy (provisionally closed December 2024) -- A complete freeze on new enterprise creation is diametrically opposed to the enterprise promotion and business environment requirements of this recently closed chapter.

Ch. 16 Taxation (open) -- Reports of 15-day delays for tax compliance certificates and the normative failures surrounding IRMS implementation undermine Montenegro's credibility on tax administration modernization benchmarks.

Ch. 23 Judiciary and Fundamental Rights (open, key benchmark) -- The acknowledged 'legal uncertainty in specific procedures' reveals deficiencies in legislative quality, impact assessment, and rule-of-law fundamentals that are central to this gating chapter.
Ch. 8 Competition Policy (open) -- Two months of zero new market entrants constitutes a de facto barrier to competition that advantages incumbent businesses.

EU Growth Plan funding at risk -- The EUR 383.5 million allocation is conditional on reform progress, with business environment improvement as an explicit priority. A registration freeze could trigger adverse assessments affecting future disbursements.

Reform Agenda credibility gap -- The Reform Agenda 2024-2027 specifically targets increasing the number of enterprises and formalizing the economy. A registration halt produces the exact opposite outcome.

Reputational damage to Montenegro's frontrunner accession status -- As the leading Western Balkans candidate, such a basic administrative failure undermines Montenegro's credibility as membership-ready.

Legislative coordination failure -- The four-month gap between law adoption (August 2025) and application (January 2026) was sufficient to identify and resolve normative inconsistencies, indicating systemic weakness in inter-institutional coordination.
recommendations: Immediately initiate an emergency legislative procedure to adopt regulatory amendments harmonizing the Law on Business Companies with all subordinate regulations governing pre-registration verification and document preparation, with a maximum 15-day deadline for adoption.

Establish a temporary fast-track registration mechanism through an emergency government decree that allows business registration to proceed under transitional rules until full normative harmonization is completed.

Mandate a mandatory Regulatory Impact Assessment (RIA) and inter-institutional compatibility review for all major legislation at least 90 days before application date, with sign-off required from all implementing agencies (Tax Administration, CRPS, relevant ministries).

Commission an independent audit of the IRMS implementation, including the 15-day delays for tax compliance certificates, and publish the results transparently to address stakeholder concerns and EU Commission scrutiny under Ch. 16.

Proactively brief the European Commission Delegation in Podgorica and the DG NEAR desk officer on the situation, the root causes, and the remediation timeline, to mitigate reputational damage before the next progress report assessment period.

Request technical assistance under IPA III for strengthening legislative drafting capacity and inter-institutional coordination mechanisms, specifically targeting the quality assurance of regulatory frameworks before implementation.

Establish a dedicated inter-ministerial working group (Ministry of Finance, Ministry of Economic Development, Tax Administration, CRPS, Secretariat for Legislation) to conduct a comprehensive review of all regulations affected by the new Company Law and IRMS, with weekly progress reporting to the government.
Develop and publish a public remediation timeline with clear milestones, demonstrating accountability and enabling affected entrepreneurs to plan accordingly.

Ensure that all businesses that were unable to register during the freeze period are offered expedited processing and fee waivers once registration resumes, as a concrete measure to mitigate economic harm.

Include this incident as a case study in Montenegro's Reform Agenda progress reporting, with lessons learned and institutional safeguards adopted, to demonstrate capacity for self-correction to EU partners.

counter_proposal.title: Emergency Regulatory Harmonization and Prevention Framework
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of EU Integration would pursue a three-track approach: (1) immediate emergency resolution through a government decree enabling transitional registration rules within 7 days, (2) comprehensive normative harmonization within 30 days through an inter-ministerial task force with EU technical assistance, and (3) permanent institutional reform establishing a mandatory pre-implementation regulatory compatibility review mechanism to prevent recurrence. This approach prioritizes restoring business registration immediately while addressing the systemic coordination failure that caused the crisis.
key_changes: Adopt an emergency government decree within 7 days establishing transitional registration rules that allow business formation to resume immediately, using the previous regulatory framework for pre-registration procedures as a temporary bridge.

Create an inter-ministerial Regulatory Harmonization Task Force with a 30-day mandate to identify and resolve all normative inconsistencies between the new Company Law, IRMS procedures, and subordinate regulations, with EU technical assistance from IPA III.

Introduce a mandatory Legislative Implementation Readiness Assessment (LIRA) protocol requiring all implementing agencies to certify regulatory compatibility at least 60 days before any major law takes effect, with the Secretariat for Legislation serving as the final sign-off authority.

Establish a permanent Regulatory Coordination Unit within the General Secretariat of the Government, specifically tasked with preventing normative gaps during legislative transitions, staffed with EU-trained legal harmonization experts.
Develop a digital regulatory tracking system that maps dependencies between primary legislation and all subordinate acts, automatically flagging potential conflicts when new laws are adopted.
expected_benefits: Immediate restoration of business registration within 7-10 days, preventing further economic damage.
Comprehensive resolution of all normative gaps within 30 days, ensuring full legal certainty.
Permanent prevention mechanism (LIRA protocol) that eliminates the risk of future regulatory implementation failures.
Strengthened credibility with the European Commission by demonstrating rapid self-correction capacity and systemic institutional improvement.
Protection of EUR 383.5 million in Growth Plan funding by demonstrating continued reform commitment.
Enhanced legislative quality aligned with EU better regulation principles, supporting progress on Chapters 23 and 16.
Improved investor confidence through transparent remediation and institutional safeguards.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. Emergency government decrees are an established mechanism in Montenegrin law and can be adopted within days. Inter-ministerial task forces have been successfully used for EU accession-related harmonization before. IPA III technical assistance for legislative capacity building is already programmed and can be redirected. The LIRA protocol requires only a government decision, not a new law. The main risk is political will -- the government must acknowledge the severity of the failure rather than deflecting responsibility to the Tax Administration alone.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Okvir za hitnu regulatornu harmonizaciju i prevenciju
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo evropskih integracija primijeniće pristup zasnovan na tri kolosijeka: (1) hitno vanredno rješenje putem vladine uredbe koja omogućava prelazna pravila registracije u roku od 7 dana, (2) sveobuhvatna normativna harmonizacija u roku od 30 dana kroz međuresornu radnu grupu uz tehničku pomoć EU, i (3) trajna institucionalna reforma kojom se uspostavlja obavezni mehanizam provjere regulatorne usklađenosti prije implementacije, kako bi se spriječilo ponavljanje sličnih situacija. Ovaj pristup daje prioritet hitnom uspostavljanju poslovne registracije, uz istovremeno otklanjanje sistemskog koordinacionog propusta koji je uzrokovao krizu.
key_changes_mne: Donošenje hitne vladine uredbe u roku od 7 dana kojom se uspostavljaju prelazna pravila registracije koja odmah omogućavaju nastavak osnivanja privrednih subjekata, koristeći prethodni regulatorni okvir za postupke pred-registracije kao privremeni most.

Osnivanje međuresorne Radne grupe za regulatornu harmonizaciju s mandatom od 30 dana radi identifikovanja i otklanjanja svih normativnih neusklađenosti između novog Zakona o privrednim društvima, procedura IRMS-a i podzakonskih akata, uz tehničku pomoć EU u okviru IPA III.

Uvođenje obaveznog protokola Procjene spremnosti za implementaciju zakonodavstva (LIRA) kojim se od svih organa nadležnih za sprovođenje zahtijeva da potvrde regulatornu usklađenost najmanje 60 dana prije stupanja na snagu svakog važnijeg zakona, pri čemu Sekretarijat za zakonodavstvo ima ulogu konačnog organa odobrenja.

Uspostavljanje stalnog Odjeljenja za regulatornu koordinaciju u okviru Generalnog sekretarijata Vlade, s posebnim zadatkom sprječavanja normativnih praznina tokom zakonodavnih prelaznih perioda, koje će biti kadrovsko popunjeno stručnjacima za pravnu harmonizaciju obučenim u EU.
Razvoj digitalnog sistema praćenja regulative koji mapira zavisnosti između primarne legislative i svih podzakonskih akata, uz automatsko označavanje potencijalnih sukoba pri donošenju novih zakona.
expected_benefits_mne: Hitno uspostavljanje poslovne registracije u roku od 7–10 dana, čime se sprječava dalja ekonomska šteta.
Sveobuhvatno otklanjanje svih normativnih praznina u roku od 30 dana, čime se osigurava potpuna pravna sigurnost.
Trajni preventivni mehanizam (LIRA protokol) koji eliminiše rizik od budućih propusta u implementaciji regulative.
Ojačana kredibilnost pred Evropskom komisijom kroz demonstraciju kapaciteta brzog samokorigovanja i sistemskog institucionalnog unapređenja.
Zaštita sredstava Plana rasta u iznosu od 383,5 miliona EUR kroz demonstraciju kontinuiranog reformskog opredjeljenja.
Unapređen kvalitet zakonodavstva usklađen s principima bolje regulative EU, čime se podržava napredak u Poglavljima 23 i 16.
Poboljšano povjerenje investitora kroz transparentno saniranje situacije i institucionalne mehanizme zaštite.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Hitne vladine uredbe su uspostavljeni mehanizam u crnogorskom pravu i mogu biti donijete u roku od nekoliko dana. Međuresorne radne grupe su ranije uspješno korišćene za harmonizaciju u kontekstu pristupanja EU. Tehnička pomoć IPA III za jačanje kapaciteta u oblasti zakonodavstva već je programirana i može biti preusmjerena. LIRA protokol zahtijeva samo vladinu odluku, a ne novi zakon. Glavni rizik je politička volja — Vlada mora da prizna ozbiljnost propusta, umjesto da odgovornost prebacuje isključivo na Poresku upravu.

assessments.summary_mne: Potpuni prestanak registracije privrednih subjekata u Crnoj Gori od januara 2026. godine predstavlja ozbiljan neuspjeh zakonodavne koordinacije koji direktno podriva višestruke obaveze preuzete u procesu pristupanja EU. Temeljni uzrok — normativna neusklađenost između novog Zakona o privrednim društvima (na snazi od 1. januara 2026.) i postojećih propisa koji uređuju postupke prethodne provjere pred registraciju — ukazuje na sistemski nedostatak u procesu izrade i harmonizacije zakonodavstva. Ovo je posebno štetno imajući u vidu da je Crna Gora privremeno zatvorila poglavlja 3, 6. i 20. u posljednjih nekoliko godina, na osnovu dokazane kompetentnosti u oblasti prava privrednih društava, prava osnivanja i politike preduzetništva. Skoro dvomjesečni nacionalni zastoj u osnivanju novih privrednih subjekata direktno je suprotan cilju Reformske agende EU koji se odnosi na povećanje broja preduzeća i formalizaciju ekonomije, te dovodi u pitanje do 383,5 miliona EUR sredstava iz Plana rasta uslovljenih reformama poslovnog okruženja.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ova odluka zaslužuje izrazito negativnu ocjenu. Skoro dvomjesečno nacionalno zamrzavanje registracije privrednih subjekata uzrokovano normativnom neusklađenošću između novog Zakona o privrednim društvima i postojećih propisa predstavlja fundamentalni propust regulatorne koordinacije koji ugrožava privremeno zatvorena poglavlja 3, 6. i 20, te je u direktnoj suprotnosti s obavezama iz Reformske agende EU vezanim za 383,5 miliona EUR sredstava Plana rasta. Vlada mora hitno usvojiti mjere za usklađivanje propisa u vidu hitnih regulatornih intervencija i uspostaviti obavezan mehanizam provjere zakonodavne kompatibilnosti prije implementacije, kako bi se u budućnosti spriječili ovakvi propusti.
key_concerns_mne: Pogl. 3 Pravo osnivanja i sloboda pružanja usluga (privremeno zatvoreno) — Nacionalni zastoj u registraciji privrednih subjekata predstavlja faktičku suspenziju osnovne slobode EU — slobode osnivanja. Ovo bi moglo pokrenuti nadzor Komisije i potencijalne pozive za otvaranje poglavlja u sljedećem izvještaju o napretku.

Pogl. 6 Pravo privrednih društava (privremeno zatvoreno) — Upravo zakon koji je trebalo da ojača usklađenost s pravnom tekovinom EU u oblasti upravljanja privrednim društvima stvorio je pravni vakuum koji onemogućava osnivanje preduzeća, što je u direktnoj suprotnosti s mjerilima na osnovu kojih je ovo poglavlje zatvoreno.

Pogl. 20 Politika preduzetništva i industrijska politika (privremeno zatvoreno decembra 2024.) — Potpuno zamrzavanje osnivanja novih privrednih subjekata dijametralno je suprotno zahtjevima u pogledu promovisanja preduzetništva i poslovnog okruženja ovog nedavno zatvorenog poglavlja.

Pogl. 16 Oporezivanje (otvoreno) — Izvještaji o kašnjenjima od 15 dana za izdavanje potvrda o poreskoj usklađenosti i normativni propusti u implementaciji IRMS-a narušavaju kredibilitet Crne Gore u pogledu mjerila modernizacije poreske uprave.

Pogl. 23 Pravosuđe i temeljna prava (otvoreno, ključno mjerilo) — Prepoznata 'pravna nesigurnost u određenim postupcima' ukazuje na nedostatke u kvalitetu zakonodavstva, procjeni uticaja i temeljima vladavine prava, koji su od centralnog značaja za ovo ključno poglavlje.
Pogl. 8 Politika konkurencije (otvoreno) — Dva mjeseca bez novih učesnika na tržištu predstavlja faktičku prepreku konkurenciji koja pogoduje postojećim privrednim subjektima.

Sredstva EU Plana rasta pod rizikom — Alokacija od 383,5 miliona EUR uslovljena je napretkom reformi, pri čemu je unapređenje poslovnog okruženja eksplicitni prioritet. Zamrzavanje registracije moglo bi pokrenuti negativne ocjene koje će uticati na buduće isplate.
Deficit vjerodostojnosti Reformske agende — Reformska agenda 2024–2027. konkretno cilja na povećanje broja preduzeća i formalizaciju ekonomije. Zastoj u registraciji proizvodi tačno suprotan ishod.

Reputacijska šteta za status Crne Gore kao predvodnika pristupnog procesa — Kao vodeći kandidat Zapadnog Balkana, ovakav elementarni administrativni propust narušava kredibilitet Crne Gore kao države spremne za članstvo.

Neuspjeh zakonodavne koordinacije — Četvoromjesečni razmak između usvajanja zakona (avgust 2025.) i početka primjene (januar 2026.) bio je dovoljan za prepoznavanje i otklanjanje normativnih neusklađenosti, što ukazuje na sistemsku slabost u međuinstitucionalnoj koordinaciji.
recommendations_mne: Odmah pokrenuti hitni zakonodavni postupak radi usvajanja regulatornih izmjena koje usklađuju Zakon o privrednim društvima sa svim podzakonskim aktima koji uređuju postupke prethodne provjere i pripreme dokumentacije pred registraciju, uz maksimalni rok za usvajanje od 15 dana.

Uspostaviti privremeni ubrzani mehanizam registracije putem hitne vladine uredbe koji će omogućiti registraciju privrednih subjekata prema prelaznim pravilima, sve do okončanja potpune normativne harmonizacije.

Uvesti obavezu sprovođenja Procjene regulatornog uticaja (RIA) i međuinstitucionalne provjere kompatibilnosti za sva značajna zakonska akta najmanje 90 dana prije datuma primjene, uz obaveznu saglasnost svih nadležnih organa (Poreska uprava, CRPS, relevantna ministarstva).

Naručiti nezavisnu reviziju implementacije IRMS-a, uključujući kašnjenja od 15 dana za izdavanje potvrda o poreskoj usklađenosti, i transparentno objaviti rezultate kako bi se odgovorilo na zabrinutosti zainteresovanih strana i nadzor Evropske komisije u okviru Pogl. 16.

Proaktivno obavijestiti Delegaciju Evropske komisije u Podgorici i nadležnog službenika DG NEAR o situaciji, uzrocima i vremenskom okviru sanacije, kako bi se umanjila reputacijska šteta prije narednog perioda ocjenjivanja izvještaja o napretku.

Zatražiti tehničku pomoć u okviru IPA III za jačanje kapaciteta izrade zakonodavstva i mehanizama međuinstitucionalne koordinacije, s posebnim fokusom na osiguranje kvaliteta regulatornih okvira prije implementacije.

Uspostaviti posebnu međuresornu radnu grupu (Ministarstvo finansija, Ministarstvo ekonomskog razvoja, Poreska uprava, CRPS, Sekretarijat za zakonodavstvo) radi sveobuhvatnog pregleda svih propisa na koje utiču novi Zakon o privrednim društvima i IRMS, uz sedmično izvještavanje Vlade o napretku.
Izraditi i javno objaviti vremenski plan sanacije s jasnim prekretnicama, demonstrirajući odgovornost i omogućavajući pogođenim preduzetnicima da planiraju svoje aktivnosti.

Osigurati da svim privrednim subjektima koji tokom perioda zamrzavanja nijesu mogli obaviti registraciju bude ponuđena ubrzana obrada i oslobađanje od taksi kada registracija bude ponovo uspostavljena, kao konkretna mjera ublažavanja ekonomske štete.

Uvrstiti ovaj slučaj kao studiju slučaja u izvještavanju o napretku Crne Gore u okviru Reformske agende, s izvučenim poukama i usvojenim institucionalnim zaštitnim mjerama, kako bi se EU partnerima demonstrirala sposobnost samokorekcije.

debate.decision_id: Business Registration Halted Across Montenegro Due to Regulatory Gaps in New Tax System
debate.consensus_summary: All nine ministries consulted -- Finance, Justice, Economy and Labour, EU Integration, Interior, Education, Health, Tourism, and Environment -- converge on an unambiguous conclusion: the nationwide halt of business registration since January 2026 constitutes a severe governance failure that was entirely foreseeable and preventable. Every ministry agrees that the root cause is a normative misalignment between the Law on Business Companies and the Law on Registration of Business Entities, both adopted in July 2025 and made effective on 1 January 2026, without ensuring mutual consistency or the readiness of implementing regulations. There is universal agreement that: (1) an emergency interim registration mechanism must be established within days, not weeks; (2) the failure of inter-ministerial coordination during the legislative process was systemic and unacceptable; (3) the freeze directly damages Montenegro's EU accession credibility across multiple negotiation chapters; (4) the informal economy will expand as aspiring entrepreneurs are pushed into unregistered operations; (5) a formal post-incident audit must be commissioned to establish accountability; and (6) affected businesses deserve expedited processing, fee waivers, and deadline extensions once registration resumes. The unanimity of negative assessments across ministries with vastly different mandates -- from environmental protection to public health to national security -- underscores the cross-cutting severity of this administrative paralysis.
disagreements: Priority sectors for emergency fast-track registration: Each ministry advocates for its own sector to receive preferential treatment. The Ministry of Health wants private clinics and pharmacies prioritized; Tourism demands seasonal tourism enterprises go first given the May-October season; Environment insists on waste management and renewable energy companies; Economy and Labour wants a blanket approach covering all SMEs equally. These competing claims reveal the impossibility of a sector-by-sector workaround -- the only viable solution is restoring universal registration capacity.

Timeline for resolution: The Ministry of Finance and Ministry of Justice demand emergency regulatory amendments within 5 working days. The Ministry of Tourism sets a hard deadline of March 15. The Ministry of Environment similarly targets March 15. The Ministry of EU Integration proposes a maximum 15-day deadline. The Ministry of Education does not set a specific date but demands action within 72 hours for a temporary procedure. These varying timelines reflect different assessments of political feasibility versus urgency.

Constitutional and legal liability dimension: The Ministry of Justice alone raises the question of whether the registration halt constitutes a violation of Article 59 of the Montenegrin Constitution (freedom of entrepreneurship) and whether affected citizens may be entitled to compensation for economic losses. Other ministries frame the issue as an administrative failure without engaging the constitutional rights dimension, creating a significant disagreement about the legal gravity of the situation.

Scope of the underlying problem: The Ministry of Interior and Ministry of EU Integration treat the registration freeze as symptomatic of deeper institutional dysfunction requiring fundamental reform of legislative quality control processes. The Ministry of Finance and Ministry of Economy focus more narrowly on the specific normative gap and its immediate fiscal and economic consequences. This divergence matters for whether the parliamentary response should be a targeted legislative fix or a broader governance reform initiative.

Role of the IRMS system itself: While the Tax Administration officially maintains the problem is purely normative and the IRMS system functions correctly, the Ministry of Interior and multiple media reports (citing the Notary Chamber, the Union of Employers, and legal practitioners) indicate that technical failures in the IRMS platform compound the regulatory gaps. The distinction between normative and technical failure remains contested and affects where accountability is assigned.
debate.debate_transcript:
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATE TRANSCRIPT -- Skupstina Crne Gore
Subject: Business Registration Halt Across Montenegro Due to Regulatory Gaps in New Tax System
Date of Debate: 22 February 2026

---

OPENING STATEMENT BY THE SPEAKER

The Speaker opens the session by noting that all nine consulted ministries have returned negative or strongly negative assessments of the situation, with scores ranging from 2 to 4 out of 10. The Speaker observes that such unanimity across the entire government apparatus is exceptional and warrants serious parliamentary attention. The Speaker invites the ruling coalition to address the chamber first.

---

RULING COALITION POSITION (Europe Now! / URA)

The Europe Now!-led ruling coalition acknowledges the severity of the registration freeze but frames it as an unavoidable transitional cost of a necessary modernisation effort. The coalition argues that the new Law on Business Companies and the IRMS system represent landmark reforms aligned with EU Directive 2017/1132 and Directive 2019/1151 on digital company formation. The coalition points to the fact that the parliamentary majority has already submitted amendments to the Law on Business Entities that will enable fully electronic company formation without requiring the physical presence of founders, and notes that the compliance deadline has been extended to 15 June 2026.

The coalition contends that the previous regulatory framework was outdated and that the transition to a modern, digital system will ultimately benefit the business environment. It acknowledges the normative gap but characterises it as a coordination failure at the administrative level -- specifically between the Tax Administration and the Central Registry of Business Entities (CRPS) -- rather than a failure of the legislative vision itself.

The coalition cites Tax Administration statistics showing 101,733 tax submissions processed and 10,080 requests handled as of 19 February, with 600 to 800 cases processed daily, arguing that the broader IRMS platform is functioning and that the registration-specific blockage is being addressed. The coalition commits to resolving the normative misalignment through expedited parliamentary procedure and establishing transitional registration mechanisms.

However, the coalition faces a difficult position: its own Ministry of Finance scored the situation at 2 out of 10, and the Ministry of EU Integration -- the portfolio most central to the coalition's political identity -- also gave a score of 2 out of 10, warning that provisionally closed EU negotiation chapters could face reopening scrutiny.

---

DPS-LED OPPOSITION RESPONSE

The DPS-led opposition, joined by SDP, delivers a forceful critique. The opposition argues that the registration freeze exposes a fundamental pattern of the current government: ambitious announcements followed by chaotic implementation. The opposition notes that the Law on Business Companies and the Law on Registration of Business Entities were both adopted in July 2025, giving the government a full four months -- from August to December -- to identify and resolve normative inconsistencies before the 1 January 2026 effective date. The opposition characterises the failure to do so as either incompetence or negligence.

The opposition draws attention to the Ministry of Justice assessment, which identifies a potential violation of Article 59 of the Constitution guaranteeing freedom of entrepreneurship. The opposition argues that a fundamental constitutional right has been de facto suspended for nearly two months without any legal basis, and demands that the Constitutional Court be asked to review the situation. The opposition further argues that affected citizens and businesses should be entitled to compensation for economic losses caused by the government's legislative negligence.

The opposition highlights the Ministry of Interior's warning that the freeze is expanding the informal economy, already estimated at 30 to 40 percent of GDP, and creating blind spots for anti-money laundering monitoring. The opposition calls this a security crisis, not merely an administrative inconvenience.

The DPS bloc specifically targets the government's EU accession narrative. Citing the Ministry of EU Integration assessment, the opposition notes that Chapters 3, 6, and 20 -- all provisionally closed on the basis of demonstrated competence in company law and business environment -- are now at risk of scrutiny in the next European Commission progress report. The opposition argues that the government's claim to be the most pro-European administration in Montenegro's history is contradicted by an administrative failure that no serious EU candidate country would tolerate.

The opposition demands: (1) an emergency parliamentary session within 48 hours to adopt corrective legislation; (2) the immediate establishment of parallel paper-based registration alongside the electronic system; (3) a formal independent audit of the IRMS procurement and implementation process; and (4) political accountability, including the possibility of a no-confidence motion against the Minister of Finance if the situation is not resolved within 10 days.

---

DEMOCRATIC FRONT / DNP BLOC POSITION

The DF/DNP nationalist bloc frames the crisis through the lens of sovereignty and foreign dependency. This bloc argues that the IRMS system was developed as part of a World Bank-funded Revenue Administration Reform Project, and that Montenegro's dependence on externally designed IT systems for core state functions demonstrates a dangerous surrender of institutional sovereignty. The bloc questions why a foreign-designed system was allowed to replace functioning domestic procedures without adequate testing and legal preparation.

The DF/DNP bloc seizes on the Ministry of Interior's assessment to argue that the registration freeze creates security vulnerabilities that foreign intelligence services and organised crime networks can exploit. The bloc contends that the freeze is especially damaging for Serbian-community businesses in northern Montenegro, where new enterprise formation is critical for economic survival and where the state already provides inadequate support.

The bloc calls for: (1) immediate restoration of paper-based registration at municipal level; (2) a parliamentary investigation into the World Bank's role in the IRMS project and whether the system was adequately adapted to Montenegrin legal requirements; and (3) a moratorium on further digitalisation of state services until existing systems are proven functional.

While the DF/DNP bloc shares the opposition's criticism of the government, it diverges on the EU dimension. Where DPS frames the damage in terms of EU accession progress, the DF/DNP bloc argues that the crisis proves Montenegro should not be rushing to adopt EU-imposed regulatory frameworks that its institutions cannot implement. The bloc suggests that the pace of EU harmonisation is itself a root cause of the problem.

---

MINORITY PARTIES POSITION (BS, FORCA, Albanian Alternative)

The minority party representatives focus on the disproportionate impact of the registration freeze on ethnic minority communities in the north and south of Montenegro. These parties note that minority entrepreneurs -- Bosniaks, Albanians, and others in economically disadvantaged municipalities -- are precisely the populations most dependent on new business formation for economic inclusion. The freeze, these parties argue, deepens existing regional inequalities and undermines years of international donor investment in minority economic empowerment programmes.

The minority bloc highlights the Ministry of Education's concern about brain drain: young graduates from minority communities already face higher barriers to entrepreneurship, and the registration freeze provides yet another reason to emigrate. These parties call for: (1) a dedicated support desk for minority entrepreneurs once registration resumes; (2) targeted fee waivers for businesses in municipalities with minority populations above 20 percent; and (3) inclusion of the Ministry for Human and Minority Rights in the inter-ministerial working group tasked with resolving the crisis.

---

CROSS-CUTTING DEBATE ON KEY MINISTRY FINDINGS

The chamber engages in substantive debate on several ministry findings that cut across party lines:

On the fiscal impact, the Ministry of Finance estimates 1,000 to 1,400 blocked business registrations over the freeze period, representing direct losses in corporate tax, VAT, and employment-related fiscal receipts. Even the ruling coalition acknowledges this is significant, though it disputes the upper-bound estimates.

On the tourism season, the Ministry of Tourism's warning about the February-to-April pre-season preparation window generates broad agreement. All blocs recognise that if new tourism businesses -- hotels, restaurants, tour operators, adventure tourism outfits -- cannot register before May, the 2026 season will be materially affected. Tourism contributes approximately 25 to 30 percent of GDP, making this a point where economic self-interest overrides partisan positioning.

On the health sector impact, the Ministry of Health's concern about frozen registration of private clinics and pharmacies resonates across party lines. With Montenegro having only 199 doctors per 100,000 population -- well below the EU average -- the inability to expand private healthcare capacity is recognised as genuinely dangerous.

On EU accession, the Ministry of EU Integration's warning about EUR 383.5 million in Growth Plan funding being potentially at risk commands attention from all pro-EU blocs (Europe Now!, URA, DPS, SDP, and minority parties). Only the DF/DNP bloc dismisses this concern, arguing that conditionality-based EU funding is itself a form of dependency that Montenegro should reduce.

---

DEBATE ON ACCOUNTABILITY

A sharp exchange occurs over accountability. The DPS-led opposition demands to know who specifically authorised the 1 January go-live date for the new laws without confirming regulatory readiness. The opposition notes that the Tax Administration itself acknowledged it was apparently unaware of the full scope of the registration crisis for weeks, raising questions about internal reporting and oversight.

The ruling coalition responds that the legislation was adopted with broad parliamentary support -- including DPS votes on certain provisions -- and that the implementation responsibility sits with the Tax Administration as an executive agency. The coalition argues that the opposition is engaging in political opportunism rather than constructive problem-solving.

The DF/DNP bloc interjects that both the ruling coalition and the DPS-led opposition share responsibility: the laws were drafted and debated during a period when both major blocs were focused on political manoeuvring rather than legislative quality. The bloc argues that the entire political class has failed Montenegro's entrepreneurs.

---

ASSESSMENT OF THE NOTARY CHAMBER'S POSITION

Multiple blocs reference the Notary Chamber's refusal to perform electronic certifications until procedural ambiguities are resolved. The DPS-led opposition argues this demonstrates that the problem extends beyond a simple normative gap -- professional bodies responsible for implementing the law have concluded it is unworkable in its current form. Attorney Andrea Kazic's public statement that the economy is practically blocked and the IRMS website is inaccessible, slowed down, or works only an hour or two receives attention as evidence contradicting the Tax Administration's claim that the system is functional.

The ruling coalition responds that the Notary Chamber's concerns are being addressed through the submitted legislative amendments and that resistance from notaries partly reflects institutional conservatism rather than genuine legal obstacles.

---

SUMMARY OF PARLIAMENTARY POSITIONS

The ruling coalition (Europe Now!/URA) accepts the need for urgent corrective action but defends the underlying reform vision and commits to expedited legislative amendments. Position: the crisis is real but temporary, and the modernisation trajectory is correct.

The DPS-led opposition (DPS/SDP) demands emergency legislative action within 48 hours, parallel paper and electronic registration, an independent audit, and political accountability. Position: the crisis reveals systemic governance failure by the current administration.

The DF/DNP bloc demands restoration of paper-based procedures, a parliamentary investigation into the World Bank-funded IRMS project, and a moratorium on further digitalisation. Position: the crisis reveals the danger of externally imposed reform agendas.

Minority parties (BS/FORCA/Albanian Alternative) demand targeted support for minority entrepreneurs, regional equity measures, and inclusion in the inter-ministerial resolution process. Position: the crisis deepens existing inequalities and demands an inclusive response.

---

PROCEDURAL OUTCOME

The chamber appears likely to reach broad agreement on the need for emergency legislative amendments, with disagreement centring on the scope of the response (narrow technical fix versus broad governance reform), the accountability mechanism (internal review versus independent audit versus no-confidence proceedings), and the framing of the crisis (temporary implementation hiccup versus systemic institutional failure versus sovereignty concern).

The Speaker notes that the Union of Employers has formally requested that the compliance deadline be extended from 31 March to 30 September 2026, and that the Tax Administration has already postponed implementation deadlines three times. The Speaker observes that repeated deadline extensions without resolving the underlying normative conflict risk normalising a state of permanent regulatory uncertainty.

All party blocs, despite their divergent framings, converge on one operational demand: business registration must resume immediately through whatever interim mechanism is available, and a hard deadline must be set for permanent resolution. The debate underscores that when a state cannot perform the basic function of registering a business, all other policy objectives -- EU accession, environmental protection, healthcare expansion, tourism development, educational outcomes, and national security -- are simultaneously undermined.
debate.consensus_summary_mne: Svih devet konsultovanih ministarstava — Finansija, Pravde, Ekonomije i rada, Evropskih integracija, Unutrašnjih poslova, Prosvjete, Zdravlja, Turizma i Životne sredine — dolazi do nedvosmislenog zaključka: obustava registracije preduzeća na nacionalnom nivou od januara 2026. godine predstavlja ozbiljan propust u upravljanju koji je bio u potpunosti predvidiv i koji se mogao spriječiti. Sva ministarstva saglasna su da je korijeni uzrok normativna neusklađenost između Zakona o privrednim društvima i Zakona o registraciji privrednih subjekata, oba usvojena u julu 2025. godine i stupila na snagu 1. januara 2026, bez osiguranja međusobne usklađenosti ili spremnosti provedbenih propisa. Postoji opšta saglasnost da: (1) hitni privremeni mehanizam registracije mora biti uspostavljen u roku od dana, a ne nedjelja; (2) propust međuresorske koordinacije tokom zakonodavnog procesa bio je sistemski i neprihvatljiv; (3) zamrzavanje direktno narušava kredibilitet Crne Gore u procesu pristupanja EU kroz više pregovaračkih poglavlja; (4) neformalna ekonomija će se širiti kako se potencijalni preduzetnici guraju u neregistrovane djelatnosti; (5) mora biti naručena formalna revizija nakon incidenta radi utvrđivanja odgovornosti; i (6) pogođena preduzeća zaslužuju ubrzanu obradu, oslobađanje od taksi i produženje rokova kada registracija bude nastavljena. Jednoglasnost negativnih ocjena ministarstava s izuzetno različitim mandatima — od zaštite životne sredine do javnog zdravlja i nacionalne bezbjednosti — naglašava sveobuhvatnu ozbiljnost ove administrativne paralize.
disagreements_mne: Prioritetni sektori za hitnu ubrzanu registraciju: Svako ministarstvo zagovara prednostni tretman za sopstveni sektor. Ministarstvo zdravlja želi da se prioritet da privatnim klinikama i apotekama; Turizam zahtijeva da sezonska turistička preduzeća idu prva, s obzirom na sezonu od maja do oktobra; Životna sredina insistira na preduzećima za upravljanje otpadom i obnovljivim izvorima energije; Ekonomija i rad želi sveobuhvatan pristup koji ravnopravno pokriva sva MSP. Ovi suprotstavljeni zahtjevi otkrivaju neizvodivost rješenja po sektorima — jedino održivo rješenje jeste obnova univerzalnog kapaciteta registracije.

Vremenski okvir za rješavanje: Ministarstvo finansija i Ministarstvo pravde zahtijevaju hitne izmjene propisa u roku od 5 radnih dana. Ministarstvo turizma postavlja čvrst rok od 15. marta. Ministarstvo životne sredine slično cilja na 15. mart. Ministarstvo evropskih integracija predlaže maksimalni rok od 15 dana. Ministarstvo prosvjete ne određuje konkretan datum, ali zahtijeva djelovanje u roku od 72 sata za uspostavljanje privremene procedure. Ovi različiti vremenski okviri odražavaju različite procjene političke izvodivosti u odnosu na hitnost.

Ustavna i pravna dimenzija odgovornosti: Jedino Ministarstvo pravde postavlja pitanje da li obustava registracije predstavlja kršenje člana 59 Ustava Crne Gore (sloboda preduzetništva) i da li pogođeni građani mogu imati pravo na naknadu štete zbog ekonomskih gubitaka. Ostala ministarstva tretiraju problem kao administrativni propust, ne ulazeći u dimenziju ustavnih prava, što stvara značajno neslaganje o pravnoj težini situacije.

Obim temeljnog problema: Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova i Ministarstvo evropskih integracija tretiraju zamrzavanje registracije kao simptom dublje institucionalne disfunkcije koja zahtijeva temeljnu reformu procesa kontrole kvaliteta zakonodavstva. Ministarstvo finansija i Ministarstvo ekonomije fokusiraju se uže na konkretan normativni jaz i njegove neposredne fiskalne i ekonomske posljedice. Ovo razilaženje je važno jer određuje da li parlamentarni odgovor treba da bude ciljana zakonodavna intervencija ili šira inicijativa za reformu upravljanja.

Uloga samog sistema IRMS-a: Dok Poreska uprava zvanično tvrdi da je problem isključivo normativne prirode i da sistem IRMS funkcioniše ispravno, Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova i brojni medijski izvještaji (pozivajući se na Notarsku komoru, Uniju poslodavaca i pravne praktičare) ukazuju na to da tehničke greške na platformi IRMS produbljuju normativne praznine. Razlika između normativnog i tehničkog propusta ostaje sporna i utiče na to gdje se pripisuje odgovornost.
debate.debate_transcript_mne:
TRANSKRIPT PARLAMENTARNE RASPRAVE — Skupština Crne Gore
Predmet: Obustava registracije preduzeća u Crnoj Gori zbog regulatornih praznina u novom poreskom sistemu
Datum rasprave: 22. februar 2026.

---

UVODNA IZJAVA PREDSJEDAVAJUĆEG

Predsjedavajući otvara sjednicu konstatujući da su svih devet konsultovanih ministarstava dostavila negativne ili izrazito negativne ocjene situacije, s ocjenama u rasponu od 2 do 4 od 10. Predsjedavajući primjećuje da je takva jednoglasnost čitavog vladinog aparata izuzetna i zaslužuje ozbiljnu parlamentarnu pažnju. Predsjedavajući poziva vladajuću koaliciju da se prva obrati skupštinskoj sali.

---

STAV VLADAJUĆE KOALICIJE (Evropa sad! / URA)

Vladajuća koalicija predvođena pokretom Evropa sad! priznaje ozbiljnost zamrzavanja registracije, ali ga okviruje kao neizbježan tranzicioni trošak neophodnog procesa modernizacije. Koalicija tvrdi da novi Zakon o privrednim društvima i sistem IRMS predstavljaju ključne reforme usklađene s Direktivom EU 2017/1132 i Direktivom 2019/1151 o digitalnom osnivanju privrednih društava. Koalicija ističe da je parlamentarna većina već podnijela izmjene Zakona o privrednim subjektima koje će omogućiti potpuno elektronsko osnivanje privrednih društava bez obaveze fizičkog prisustva osnivača, te napominje da je rok za usklađivanje produžen do 15. juna 2026.

Koalicija tvrdi da je prethodni regulatorni okvir bio zastareo i da će prelazak na moderan digitalni sistem na kraju ići u korist poslovnog ambijenta. Ona priznaje normativni jaz, ali ga karakteriše kao koordinacijski propust na administrativnom nivou — konkretno između Poreske uprave i Centralnog registra privrednih subjekata (CRPS) — a ne kao propust zakonodavne vizije same po sebi.

Koalicija citira statistike Poreske uprave koje pokazuju 101.733 obrađene poreske prijave i 10.080 riješenih zahtjeva zaključno s 19. februarom, s dnevnom obradom od 600 do 800 predmeta, tvrdeći da šira platforma IRMS funkcioniše i da se blokada specifična za registraciju rješava. Koalicija se obavezuje da će normativnu neusklađenost riješiti ubrzanom parlamentarnom procedurom i uspostaviti prelazne mehanizme registracije.

Međutim, koalicija se nalazi u teškoj poziciji: njeno sopstveno Ministarstvo finansija situaciju je ocijenilo s 2 od 10, a Ministarstvo evropskih integracija — portfolio najcentralniji za politički identitet koalicije — takođe je dalo ocjenu 2 od 10, upozoravajući da privremeno zatvorena pregovaračka poglavlja EU mogu biti podvrgnuta ponovnom preispitivanju.

---

ODGOVOR OPOZICIJE PREDVOĐENE DPS-om

Opozicija predvođena DPS-om, kojoj se pridružio SDP, iznosi snažnu kritiku. Opozicija tvrdi da zamrzavanje registracije razotkriva temeljni obrazac trenutne vlade: ambiciozne najave praćene haotičnom provedbom. Opozicija napominje da su i Zakon o privrednim društvima i Zakon o registraciji privrednih subjekata usvojeni u julu 2025. godine, dajući vladi punih četiri mjeseca — od avgusta do decembra — da identifikuje i otkloni normativne neusklađenosti prije datuma stupanja na snagu 1. januara 2026. Opozicija ovaj propust karakteriše kao nekompetentnost ili nemar.

Opozicija skreće pažnju na ocjenu Ministarstva pravde, koja identifikuje potencijalnu povredu člana 59 Ustava kojim se garantuje sloboda preduzetništva. Opozicija tvrdi da je osnovno ustavno pravo de facto suspendovano gotovo dva mjeseca bez ikakvog pravnog osnova, te zahtijeva da se Ustavni sud pozove da preispita situaciju. Opozicija dalje tvrdi da pogođeni građani i preduzeća treba da budu entitled na naknadu ekonomskih gubitaka nastalih usljed zakonodavnog nemara vlade.

Opozicija ističe upozorenje Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova da zamrzavanje širi neformalna ekonomiju, čiji se udio već procjenjuje na 30 do 40 posto BDP-a, i stvara slijepe tačke za praćenje pranja novca. Opozicija ovo naziva sigurnosnom krizom, a ne pukom administrativnom neugodnošću.

Blok DPS-a posebno cilja na vladinu narativu o pristupanju EU. Pozivajući se na ocjenu Ministarstva evropskih integracija, opozicija napominje da su Poglavlja 3, 6 i 20 — sva privremeno zatvorena na osnovu demonstrirane kompetentnosti u oblasti kompanijskog prava i poslovnog ambijenta — sada izložena riziku preispitivanja u narednom izvještaju Evropske komisije o napretku. Opozicija tvrdi da se vladin zahtjev da bude najproevropskija administracija u istoriji Crne Gore demantuje administrativnim propustom koji nijedna ozbiljna zemlja kandidat za EU ne bi tolerisala.

Opozicija zahtijeva: (1) hitnu parlamentarnu sjednicu u roku od 48 sati radi usvajanja korektivnog zakonodavstva; (2) trenutno uspostavljanje paralelne registracije na papiru pored elektronskog sistema; (3) formalnu nezavisnu reviziju procesa nabavke i implementacije sistema IRMS; i (4) političku odgovornost, uključujući mogućnost izglasavanja nepovjerenja ministru finansija ukoliko situacija ne bude riješena u roku od 10 dana.

---

STAV BLOKA DEMOKRATSKI FRONT / DNP

Nacionalistički blok DF/DNP krizu sagledava kroz prizmu suvereniteta i zavisnosti od stranih aktera. Ovaj blok tvrdi da je sistem IRMS razvijen u okviru projekta reforme poreske administracije finansiranog od strane Svjetske banke, te da zavisnost Crne Gore od inostrano dizajniranih IT sistema za temeljne državne funkcije pokazuje opasno odricanje od institucionalnog suvereniteta. Blok propituje zašto je inostrano dizajniran sistem smio zamijeniti funkcionalne domaće procedure bez adekvatnog testiranja i pravne pripreme.

Blok DF/DNP-a koristi ocjenu Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova kako bi argumentovao da zamrzavanje registracije stvara sigurnosne ranjivosti koje strani obavještajni servisi i organizovane kriminalne mreže mogu iskoristiti. Blok tvrdi da je zamrzavanje posebno štetno za srpska preduzeća u sjevernoj Crnoj Gori, gdje je osnivanje novih preduzeća ključno za ekonomski opstanak i gdje država već pruža nedovoljnu podršku.

Blok zahtijeva: (1) trenutno uspostavljanje papirne registracije na opštinskom nivou; (2) parlamentarnu istragu o ulozi Svjetske banke u projektu IRMS i o tome da li je sistem adekvatno prilagođen crnogorskim pravnim zahtjevima; i (3) moratorijum na dalju digitalizaciju državnih usluga dok se ne dokaže funkcionalnost postojećih sistema.

Iako blok DF/DNP-a dijeli kritiku opozicije prema vladi, razilazi se s njom po pitanju EU dimenzije. Dok DPS krizu okviruje u smislu štete po napredak pristupanja EU, blok DF/DNP-a tvrdi da kriza dokazuje da Crna Gora ne treba da žuri s usvajanjem regulatornih okvira koje nameće EU, a koje njene institucije ne mogu provesti. Blok sugeriše da je tempo harmonizacije s EU sam po sebi korijeni uzrok problema.

---

STAV MANJINSKIH STRANAKA (BS, FORCA, Albanska alternativa)

Predstavnici manjinskih stranaka fokusiraju se na nesrazmjeran uticaj zamrzavanja registracije na etničke manjinske zajednice na sjeveru i jugu Crne Gore. Ove stranke napominju da su manjinski preduzetnici — Bošnjaci, Albanci i drugi u ekonomski zapostavljenim opštinama — upravo populacije najzavisnije od osnivanja novih preduzeća za ekonomsku inkluziju. Zamrzavanje, tvrde ove stranke, produbljuje postojeće regionalne nejednakosti i potkopava godine ulaganja međunarodnih donatora u programe ekonomskog osnaživanja manjina.

Manjinski blok ističe zabrinutost Ministarstva prosvjete zbog odliva mozgova: mladi diplomirani iz manjinskih zajednica već se suočavaju s većim preprekama za preduzetništvo, a zamrzavanje registracije daje još jedan razlog za emigraciju. Ove stranke zahtijevaju: (1) posvećeni info-pult za manjinske preduzetnike kada registracija bude nastavljena; (2) ciljana oslobađanja od taksi za preduzeća u opštinama s udjelom manjinskog stanovništva iznad 20 posto; i (3) uključivanje Ministarstva za ljudska i manjinska prava u međuresorsku radnu grupu zaduženu za rješavanje krize.

---

MEĐUSEKTORSKA RASPRAVA O KLJUČNIM NALAZIMA MINISTARSTAVA

Skupština vodi suštinsku raspravu o nekoliko nalaza ministarstava koji se tiču svih poslaničkih klubova:

O fiskalnom uticaju, Ministarstvo finansija procjenjuje 1.000 do 1.400 blokiranih registracija preduzeća tokom perioda zamrzavanja, što predstavlja direktne gubitke u prihodima od poreza na dobit, PDV-a i fiskalnih prihoda vezanih za zapošljavanje. Čak i vladajuća koalicija priznaje da je ovo značajno, iako osporava procjene gornje granice.

O turističkoj sezoni, upozorenje Ministarstva turizma o prozoru pripreme predsezone od februara do aprila nailazi na široku saglasnost. Svi blokovi prepoznaju da ako nova turistička preduzeća — hoteli, restorani, turistički operateri, avanturističke agencije — ne mogu biti registrovana prije maja, sezona 2026. će biti materijalno pogođena. Turizam doprinosi oko 25 do 30 posto BDP-a, čineći ovo tačkom gdje ekonomski interes nadilazi stranačko pozicioniranje.

O uticaju na zdravstveni sektor, zabrinutost Ministarstva zdravlja zbog zamrznute registracije privatnih klinika i apoteka rezonira s poslanicima svih stranaka. S obzirom na to da Crna Gora ima samo 199 ljekara na 100.000 stanovnika — znatno ispod prosjeka EU — nemogućnost proširenja privatnih zdravstvenih kapaciteta prepoznaje se kao istinski opasna.

O pristupanju EU, upozorenje Ministarstva evropskih integracija o potencijalnom riziku od gubitka 383,5 miliona eura iz Plana rasta privlači pažnju svih proevropskih blokova (Evropa sad!, URA, DPS, SDP i manjinske stranke). Jedino blok DF/DNP-a odbacuje ovu brigu, tvrdeći da je EU finansiranje zasnovano na uslovljenosti samo po sebi oblik zavisnosti koji Crna Gora treba da smanjuje.

---

RASPRAVA O ODGOVORNOSTI

Dolazi do oštre razmjene u pogledu odgovornosti. Opozicija predvođena DPS-om zahtijeva da se zna ko je konkretno odobrio datum pokretanja novih zakona od 1. januara bez potvrde regulatorne spremnosti. Opozicija napominje da je sama Poreska uprava priznala da navodno nije bila svjesna punog obima registracijske krize sedmicama, pokrecući pitanja o internom izvještavanju i nadzoru.

Vladajuća koalicija odgovara da je zakonodavstvo usvojeno uz široku parlamentarnu podršku — uključujući glasove DPS-a za određene odredbe — i da odgovornost za implementaciju leži na Poreskoj upravi kao izvršnoj agenciji. Koalicija tvrdi da se opozicija bavi političkim oportunizmom, a ne konstruktivnim rješavanjem problema.

Blok DF/DNP-a umeće se tvrdnjom da i vladajuća koalicija i opozicija predvođena DPS-om dijele odgovornost: zakoni su bili nacrtani i raspravljani u periodu kada su se oba velika bloka fokusirala na politička manevriranja, a ne na zakonodavni kvalitet. Blok tvrdi da je čitava politička klasa zakazala pred crnogorskim preduzetnicima.

---

OCJENA STAVA NOTARSKE KOMORE

Višestruki blokovi pozivaju se na odbijanje Notarske komore da vrši elektronske ovjere dok se ne razriješe proceduralne nejasnoće. Opozicija predvođena DPS-om tvrdi da ovo demonstrira da problem prevazilazi jednostavnu normativnu prazninu — profesionalna tijela odgovorna za primjenu zakona zaključila su da je u sadašnjem obliku neprimjenljiv. Javnoj izjavi advokata Andree Kazić da je ekonomija praktično blokirana i da je web-stranica IRMS-a nedostupna, usporena ili radi samo sat-dva posvećuje se pažnja kao dokazu koji je u suprotnosti s tvrdnjom Poreske uprave da sistem funkcioniše.

Vladajuća koalicija odgovara da se zabrinutosti Notarske komore rješavaju kroz podnijete zakonodavne izmjene i da otpor notara dijelom odražava institucionalnu konzervativnost, a ne stvarne pravne prepreke.

---

REZIME PARLAMENTARNIH STAVOVA

Vladajuća koalicija (Evropa sad!/URA) prihvata potrebu za hitnim korektivnim mjerama, ali brani temeljnu reformsku viziju i obavezuje se na ubrzane zakonodavne izmjene. Stav: kriza je realna, ali privremena, a trajektorija modernizacije je ispravna.

Opozicija predvođena DPS-om (DPS/SDP) zahtijeva hitne zakonodavne mjere u roku od 48 sati, paralelnu papirnu i elektronsku registraciju, nezavisnu reviziju i političku odgovornost. Stav: kriza otkriva sistemski propust u upravljanju od strane sadašnje administracije.

Blok DF/DNP-a zahtijeva uspostavljanje papirnih procedura, parlamentarnu istragu o projektu IRMS finansiranom od Svjetske banke i moratorijum na dalju digitalizaciju. Stav: kriza otkriva opasnost od eksternih reformskih agenada koje su nametnute izvana.

Manjinske stranke (BS/FORCA/Albanska alternativa) zahtijevaju ciljanu podršku za manjinske preduzetnike, mjere regionalne ravnopravnosti i uključenost u međuresorski proces rješavanja. Stav: kriza produbljuje postojeće nejednakosti i zahtijeva inkluzivan odgovor.

---

PROCEDURALNI ISHOD

Izgleda da je skupština na putu da postigne široku saglasnost o potrebi za hitnim zakonodavnim izmjenama, s razilaženjima koja se tiču obima odgovora (uski tehnički popravak nasuprot široj reformi upravljanja), mehanizma odgovornosti (interna revizija nasuprot nezavisnoj reviziji nasuprot postupku izglasavanja nepovjerenja) i okviriranja krize (privremeni implementacioni zastoj nasuprot sistemskom institucionalnom propustu nasuprot brizi o suverenitetu).

Predsjedavajući napominje da je Unija poslodavaca zvanično zatražila produženje roka usklađivanja sa 31. marta na 30. septembra 2026. godine, i da je Poreska uprava već tri puta odložila rokove implementacije. Predsjedavajući primjećuje da ponavljana produženja rokova bez rješavanja temeljnog normativnog sukoba rizikuju normalizaciju stanja trajne regulatorne neizvjesnosti.

Svi poslanički blokovi, uprkos različitim pristupima, slažu se oko jednog operativnog zahtjeva: registracija preduzeća mora odmah biti nastavljena kroz bilo koji dostupni privremeni mehanizam, i mora biti određen čvrst rok za trajno rješenje. Rasprava naglašava da kada država ne može da obavlja osnovnu funkciju registracije preduzeća, svi ostali ciljevi politike — pristupanje EU, zaštita životne sredine, proširenje zdravstvene zaštite, razvoj turizma, obrazovni ishodi i nacionalna bezbjednost — bivaju istovremeno potkopani.

blind_spots: Chapter 6 (Company Law) was provisionally closed on December 16, 2025 -- just 16 days before this crisis began on January 1, 2026. Not a single ministry flagged this extraordinary irony: Montenegro convinced the EU it had a functioning company law framework and then immediately demonstrated the opposite. This could trigger the European Commission to reconsider or flag backsliding on a freshly closed chapter.

No ministry raised the question of legal liability and compensation for affected entrepreneurs. Citizens whose constitutional right to entrepreneurship (Article 59) has been suspended for nearly two months may have grounds for damages claims against the state. The Ombudsman's role and potential constitutional court referrals were not discussed.

The IRMS project was funded by the World Bank through a EUR 14 million Revenue Administration Reform Project (plus EUR 4.8 million additional financing). No ministry asked whether the World Bank has been notified of the implementation failure, whether project conditionality was met, or whether this represents a misuse of international development funds. The system reportedly cost EUR 8.5 million with a consortium led by wedoIT -- accountability for the vendor's delivery is absent from the analysis.

Political accountability is entirely absent. No ministry named which minister or ministers bear responsibility for the coordination failure. The Minister of Finance, the Minister of Economy, and the Secretary-General of the Government all had roles in ensuring legislative coherence. The assessments critique 'the system' without identifying who specifically failed.

No ministry examined whether the Notary Chamber of Montenegro was consulted during the legislative drafting process. Since the core conflict involves notarial authentication requirements clashing with electronic-only registration mandates, the Notary Chamber's input (or exclusion from consultations) is directly relevant to understanding how this failure occurred.

The impact on the digital nomad and remote worker community was not assessed. Montenegro has actively marketed itself as a digital nomad destination and introduced temporary residence permits for remote workers. Foreign entrepreneurs who relocated to Montenegro expecting to register businesses are now stranded in legal limbo, which will generate negative international press.

No ministry questioned whether similar legislative coordination failures may exist in other laws adopted in the same July 2025 parliamentary session. If two major laws from the same session are mutually incompatible, a systematic review of all legislation from that period may be warranted.

The absence of a publicly announced timeline or emergency legislative procedure was insufficiently emphasized. Nearly two months into a nationwide crisis, the government has not initiated an expedited legislative amendment process or published a resolution roadmap. The Tax Administration's workshop on January 29 -- held a month after the laws took effect -- suggests a reactive rather than proactive crisis posture.

None of the ministries assessed the impact on public procurement. Entities that cannot register cannot bid on government contracts, reducing competition in public tenders and potentially inflating costs to the state budget during Q1 2026.
critic_report.overall_analysis:
This is not a policy decision gone wrong -- it is a governance catastrophe that reveals systemic institutional dysfunction at the heart of Montenegro's public administration. For nearly two months, not a single new business has been registered anywhere in Montenegro, a country of 620,000 people where small enterprises comprise 99.3% of the business fabric. The cause is not a cyberattack, not a natural disaster, but the government's own failure to ensure that two laws it drafted, adopted in the same parliamentary session (July 2025), and implemented on the same date (January 1, 2026), were actually compatible with each other.

INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION: My research confirms the core facts reported by CdM and Radio-Televizija Niksic. The IRMS portal has experienced severe availability issues since launch, with the Central Registry component for company formation entirely non-functional. Tax Administration Director Sava Laketic has publicly acknowledged the normative nature of the problem. Legal experts from Adzic and Knezevic law firm have characterized the situation as an "institutional and technical collapse," noting the absence of transition provisions that international administrative reform standards require (specifically, parallel operation of old and new systems during implementation).

THE CHAPTER 6 IRONY: The most damning fact that no ministry identified is the timing. Chapter 6 (Company Law) was provisionally closed at the 24th Accession Conference on December 16, 2025. Exactly 16 days later, on January 1, 2026, the very company law framework that secured that closure rendered business registration impossible nationwide. This is not merely embarrassing -- it is potentially consequential. The European Commission monitors provisionally closed chapters for backsliding, and this episode provides concrete evidence that Montenegro's company law implementation is dysfunctional. The EU may well flag this in its 2026 progress report, and it could complicate the narrative that Montenegro is on track to close all remaining chapters.

EU PROGRESS REPORT FORESHADOWING: The European Commission's 2025 progress report on Montenegro already identified "lack of ministerial coordination and overall steering" as a governance weakness, despite the government operating in a "stable environment." This registration crisis is a textbook manifestation of exactly that weakness. The Commission also noted that "administrative capacities remain weak" and that the regulatory framework "still needs to be finalised." The government was warned. It did not listen.

FINANCIAL DIMENSIONS: The IRMS system cost approximately EUR 8.5 million, implemented by a consortium led by wedoIT, with World Bank financing through the Revenue Administration Reform Project (EUR 14 million plus EUR 4.8 million additional financing). This is substantial public money -- both Montenegrin and international -- invested in a system that was launched without ensuring the legal framework could support it. No one has publicly addressed whether this constitutes a failure of project implementation oversight or whether the World Bank's conditions for disbursement were properly met.

ECONOMIC DAMAGE: Montenegro registers roughly 500-700 new businesses per month based on available growth data (58,998 active enterprises in 2024 with 9.1% year-on-year growth). A two-month freeze therefore blocks an estimated 1,000-1,400 potential new businesses. Each represents lost employment, lost tax revenue, and lost economic dynamism. This comes at a particularly bad time: FDI composition has shifted dramatically toward real estate (52% of EUR 890 million total FDI in 2024), with productive investment falling to just 12.8%. Montenegro desperately needs new domestic enterprise formation to compensate for the decline in productive foreign investment.

INFORMAL ECONOMY EXPANSION: The U.S. State Department's 2025 Investment Climate Statement estimates Montenegro's informal economy at approximately 30% of GDP. A registration freeze does not stop economic activity -- it pushes it underground. Aspiring entrepreneurs who cannot register will operate informally, avoiding taxes, social contributions, and labor protections. This directly undermines the EU Growth Plan's conditions, which specifically target formalization of the economy as a reform priority. Montenegro's EUR 383.5 million Growth Plan allocation (EUR 110 million in grants, EUR 273.5 million in concessional loans) explicitly conditions disbursements on business environment improvements. A registration freeze produces the exact opposite outcome.

ASSESSMENT QUALITY: The seven ministry assessments are notably thorough and substantive -- far above typical bureaucratic rubber-stamping. Each brings genuine sector-specific analysis with concrete data points and logical reasoning. The Ministry of Justice's constitutional analysis is particularly strong, correctly identifying the Article 59 violation and ECHR Protocol 1 implications. The Ministry of Finance provides solid fiscal impact estimates. The Ministry of EU Integration delivers the most comprehensive acquis analysis. However, there are shared weaknesses: all seven assessments cite similar background data (the 15-day certificate delays, the normative-vs-technical distinction, general informal economy figures) without adding independent verification. More critically, none identifies specific political accountability, none raises the Chapter 6 timing irony, none discusses compensation rights for affected citizens, and none examines the World Bank project oversight dimension. The assessments diagnose the disease well but avoid naming the doctors who misdiagnosed the patient.

CRISIS RESPONSE FAILURE: Perhaps most concerning is what has not happened. Nearly two months into a complete nationwide registration freeze, the government has not: (1) initiated an emergency legislative amendment procedure, (2) published a resolution timeline, (3) appointed a crisis coordinator, (4) activated transitional provisions allowing the old system to operate in parallel, or (5) issued a senior-level public acknowledgment of the severity of the situation. The Tax Administration held a "professional workshop" on January 29 -- a month after the crisis began -- which is staggeringly inadequate. The interim measure of issuing paper documents addresses only ancillary certificate needs, not the fundamental registration blockage. This passivity in the face of a clear crisis suggests either a government that does not grasp the severity of the situation, or one that is paralyzed by the inter-ministerial coordination failures that created it.

BOTTOM LINE: This crisis converts an abstract governance weakness -- poor inter-ministerial coordination -- into concrete, measurable harm to citizens and the economy. It exposes the gap between Montenegro's legislative ambitions (EU-aligned company law) and its implementation capacity. It threatens the credibility of recently closed accession chapters and risks Growth Plan disbursements. And it has unfolded with remarkable slowness, both in its creation (laws adopted in July 2025 with a January 2026 deadline left unreconciled for five months) and in its resolution (still unresolved after nearly two months). For a country that aspires to EU membership by 2028, this is not merely an embarrassment -- it is evidence of a systemic institutional capacity deficit that will recur unless the root causes of inter-ministerial coordination failure are addressed.
critic_report.headline: Montenegro Passed EU-Aligned Company Law Then Made It Impossible to Register a Company
eu_chapter_relevance: Ch.6 Company Law -- Provisionally closed on December 16, 2025, just 16 days before the registration crisis began. The very law that secured closure has rendered company formation impossible, creating a textbook case of backsliding on a freshly closed chapter that the Commission will scrutinize.

Ch.23 Judiciary and Fundamental Rights -- The registration freeze constitutes a de facto suspension of the constitutional right to entrepreneurship (Article 59), violating legal certainty principles. The EU 2025 report already flagged Montenegro's lack of ministerial coordination; this crisis validates that concern at the most fundamental rule-of-law level.

Ch.3 Right of Establishment and Freedom to Provide Services -- Provisionally closed in December 2025. A blanket inability to register businesses is a direct suspension of the EU freedom of establishment. Both domestic and foreign entrepreneurs are equally affected, contradicting the acquis guarantees underlying this chapter's closure.

Ch.16 Taxation -- The IRMS system that was supposed to modernize tax administration has instead created 15-day delays for compliance certificates and blocked the registration pathway entirely. This undermines benchmarks on efficient tax administration and digital governance.

Ch.8 Competition Policy -- Zero new market entrants for nearly two months creates a de facto barrier to competition, insulating incumbent businesses from new competitors. This artificial restraint on market entry contradicts EU competition principles.

Ch.20 Enterprise and Industrial Policy -- Provisionally closed in December 2024. A nationwide freeze on new enterprise creation is diametrically opposed to the enterprise promotion requirements that justified this chapter's closure. Combined with FDI shifting to 52% real estate, the absence of new productive enterprises compounds structural economic weaknesses.

Ch.19 Social Policy and Employment -- The freeze pushes aspiring entrepreneurs into informal employment without social protections, pension contributions, or labor rights. With informal economy already at approximately 30% of GDP, this crisis actively expands the gap between formal and informal labor markets.

Ch.33 Financial and Budgetary Provisions -- Lost tax revenue from an estimated 1,000-1,400 blocked business registrations, combined with cascading effects on VAT, corporate tax, and social contributions, has direct budgetary implications that affect Montenegro's fiscal capacity assessments.
critic_report.headline_mne: Crna Gora usvojila zakon o privrednim društvima usklađen sa EU, a potom onemogućila registraciju preduzeća
critic_report.overall_analysis_mne:
Ovo nije politička odluka koja je loše ispala — to je katastrofa upravljanja koja otkriva sistemsku institucionalnu disfunkciju u srži crnogorske javne uprave. Gotovo dva mjeseca nijedan novi privredni subjekt nije registrovan nigdje u Crnoj Gori, zemlji od 620.000 stanovnika u kojoj mala preduzeća čine 99,3% privrednog tkiva. Uzrok nije sajber napad, nije prirodna katastrofa, već propust same vlade da obezbijedi da dva zakona koja je izradila, usvojila na istom zasjedanju parlamenta (jul 2025) i primijenila na isti datum (1. januar 2026), budu međusobno usklađena.

NEZAVISNA PROVJERA: Moje istraživanje potvrđuje ključne činjenice koje su iznijeli CdM i Radio-televizija Nikšić. Portal IRMS trpi ozbiljne probleme s dostupnošću od samog lansiranja, a komponenta Centralnog registra za osnivanje privrednih društava potpuno je nefunkcionalna. Direktor Poreske uprave Sava Laketić javno je priznao normativnu prirodu problema. Pravni stručnjaci iz advokatske kancelarije Adžić i Knežević okarakterisali su situaciju kao "institucijalni i tehnički kolaps", ukazujući na odsustvo prelaznih odredbi koje zahtijevaju međunarodni standardi administrativnih reformi (konkretno, paralelno funkcionisanje starih i novih sistema tokom implementacije).

IRONIJA POGLAVLJA 6: Najdamnativnija činjenica koju nijedno ministarstvo nije identifikovalo jeste tajming. Poglavlje 6 (Pravo privrednih društava) privremeno je zatvoreno na 24. Konferenciji o pristupanju, 16. decembra 2025. Tačno 16 dana kasnije, 1. januara 2026, upravo taj zakonodavni okvir o privrednim društvima koji je osigurao to zatvaranje učinio je registraciju preduzeća nemogućom širom države. Ovo nije samo sramotno — to je potencijalno posljedično. Evropska komisija prati privremeno zatvorena poglavlja zbog mogućeg nazadovanja, a ovaj slučaj pruža konkretan dokaz da je implementacija crnogorskog prava privrednih društava disfunkcionalna. EU to može dobro naglasiti u svom izvještaju o napretku za 2026. godinu, a to bi moglo zakomplikovati narativ da je Crna Gora na putu da zatvori sva preostala poglavlja.

NAJAVA U IZVJEŠTAJU O NAPRETKU EU: Izvještaj Evropske komisije o napretku Crne Gore za 2025. godinu već je identifikovao "nedostatak međuresorske koordinacije i opšteg usmjeravanja" kao slabost u upravljanju, uprkos tome što vlada funkcioniše u "stabilnom okruženju". Ova kriza registracije školski je primjer upravo te slabosti. Komisija je takođe napomenula da "administrativni kapaciteti ostaju slabi" i da regulatorni okvir "još uvijek treba biti finalizovan". Vlada je bila upozorena. Nije poslušala.

FINANSIJSKE DIMENZIJE: Sistem IRMS koštao je oko 8,5 miliona EUR, implementovao ga je konzorcijum predvođen kompanijom wedoIT, uz finansiranje Svjetske banke kroz Projekat reforme poreske administracije (14 miliona EUR plus 4,8 miliona EUR dodatnog finansiranja). Radi se o značajnim javnim sredstvima — crnogorskim i međunarodnim — uloženim u sistem koji je lansiran bez obezbjeđivanja da ga pravni okvir može podržati. Niko javno nije odgovorio na pitanje da li to predstavlja propust u nadzoru implementacije projekta ili da li su uslovi Svjetske banke za isplatu sredstava bili ispravno ispunjeni.

EKONOMSKA ŠTETA: Crna Gora registruje otprilike 500–700 novih privrednih subjekata mjesečno, na osnovu dostupnih podataka o rastu (58.998 aktivnih preduzeća u 2024. godini uz godišnji rast od 9,1%). Dvomjesečno zamrzavanje stoga blokira procijenjenih 1.000–1.400 potencijalnih novih preduzeća. Svako od njih predstavlja izgubljenu zaposlenost, izgubljene poreske prihode i izgubljenu ekonomsku dinamiku. To dolazi u posebno lošem trenutku: struktura SDI drastično se promijenila u korist nekretnina (52% od ukupnih SDI od 890 miliona EUR u 2024. godini), dok je produktivno ulaganje palo na svega 12,8%. Crna Gora očajnički treba nova domaća osnivanja preduzeća kako bi nadoknadila pad produktivnih stranih ulaganja.

ŠIRENJE SIVE EKONOMIJE: Izvještaj Stejt departmenta SAD o investicionoj klimi za 2025. godinu procjenjuje crnogorsku sivu ekonomiju na oko 30% BDP-a. Zamrzavanje registracije ne zaustavlja ekonomsku aktivnost — gura je u podzemlje. Budući preduzetnici koji ne mogu registrovati preduzeće poslovaće neformalno, izbjegavajući poreze, doprinose za socijalno osiguranje i zaštitu rada. To direktno podriva uslove Plana rasta EU, koji konkretno ciljaju formalizaciju ekonomije kao prioritet reformi. Dodjela sredstava Crnoj Gori u okviru Plana rasta od 383,5 miliona EUR (110 miliona u grantovima, 273,5 miliona u povlašćenim zajmovima) eksplicitno uslovljava isplate poboljšanjima poslovnog okruženja. Zamrzavanje registracije proizvodi tačno suprotan ishod.

KVALITET PROCJENA: Sedam ministarskih procjena izuzetno je temeljno i sadržajno — daleko iznad tipičnog birokratskog formalizma. Svaka donosi autentičnu sektorski specifičnu analizu s konkretnim podacima i logičkim zaključivanjem. Ustavna analiza Ministarstva pravde posebno je snažna — ispravno identifikuje kršenje člana 59 i implikacije Protokola 1 EKLJP. Ministarstvo finansija pruža solidne procjene fiskalnog uticaja. Ministarstvo evropskih integracija daje najsveobuhvatniju analizu acquis-a. Međutim, postoje zajedničke slabosti: svih sedam procjena navodi slične pozadinske podatke (kašnjenja u izdavanju uvjerenja od 15 dana, razliku između normativnog i tehničkog problema, opšte podatke o sivoj ekonomiji) bez dodavanja nezavisne provjere. Što je kritičnije, nijedna ne identifikuje konkretnu političku odgovornost, nijedna ne pominje ironiju tajminga Poglavlja 6, nijedna ne raspravlja o pravu oštećenih građana na naknadu štete, a nijedna ne ispituje dimenziju nadzora projekta Svjetske banke. Procjene dobro dijagnostikuju bolest, ali izbjegavaju da imenuju ljekare koji su pogrešno dijagnostikovali pacijenta.

PROPAST ODGOVORA NA KRIZU: Možda je najzabrinjavajuće ono što se nije dogodilo. Gotovo dva mjeseca od potpunog zamrzavanja registracije na nacionalnom nivou, vlada nije: (1) pokrenula postupak hitnih zakonodavnih izmjena, (2) objavila vremenski okvir za rješavanje problema, (3) imenovala koordinatora za vanredne situacije, (4) aktivirala prelazne odredbe koje bi omogućile paralelno funkcionisanje starog sistema, niti (5) izdala javno saopštenje višeg rukovodstva o ozbiljnosti situacije. Poreska uprava održala je "stručnu radionicu" 29. januara — mjesec dana nakon početka krize — što je zapanjujuće nedovoljno. Privremena mjera izdavanja papirnih dokumenata rješava samo sporedne potrebe za uvjerenjima, a ne temeljnu blokadu registracije. Ova pasivnost pred jasnom krizom sugeriše ili vladu koja ne shvata ozbiljnost situacije, ili onu koja je paralisana međuresorskim koordinacionim propustima koji su je stvorili.

ZAKLJUČAK: Ova kriza pretvara apstraktnu slabost upravljanja — slabu međuresorsku koordinaciju — u konkretnu, mjerljivu štetu po građane i ekonomiju. Otkriva jaz između zakonodavnih ambicija Crne Gore (zakon o privrednim društvima usklađen sa EU) i njenih implementacionih kapaciteta. Ugrožava kredibilitet nedavno zatvorenih poglavlja u pristupnom procesu i rizikuje isplate u okviru Plana rasta. I odvijala se zapanjujuće sporim tempom — i u svom nastanku (zakoni usvojeni u julu 2025. s rokom za januar 2026. ostavljeni neusaglašenima pet mjeseci) i u svom rješavanju (još uvijek neriješeno nakon gotovo dva mjeseca). Za zemlju koja teži članstvu u EU do 2028. godine, ovo nije samo sramota — to je dokaz sistemskog institucionalnog kapacitetnog deficita koji će se ponavljati ako se ne adresiraju korijeni koordinacionog propusta između ministarstava.
blind_spots_mne: Poglavlje 6 (Pravo privrednih društava) privremeno je zatvoreno 16. decembra 2025. — svega 16 dana prije nego što je ova kriza počela 1. januara 2026.
